topics.title: Study and Calibration of LVDT Transducer for Displacement Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Calibration of LVDT Transducer for Displacement Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a passive inductive transducer that converts **linear displacement** into a corresponding, proportional **electrical voltage signal** using the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction** between a single primary winding and two identical secondary windings, coupled by a movable soft-iron armature (core).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme instrumentation curriculum, the LVDT is studied as a **first-order displacement transducer** that operates on **AC excitation** (typically a **5 V RMS, 50 Hz to 5 kHz** carrier signal). The output is a **differential AC voltage** whose magnitude and phase indicate the direction and magnitude of core displacement, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The LVDT is treated as a *calibration experiment* — meaning students are expected to (a) plot the **Displacement vs. Output Voltage** characteristic, (b) determine the **Sensitivity (mV/mm)**, (c) identify the **Linear Operating Range**, and (d) compute the **Percentage Non-Linearity (%NL)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **see-saw (teeter-totter)**. When two children of equal weight sit on either end, the see-saw is **balanced (null position)** — the net movement is zero. If one child moves closer to the center, the see-saw tilts — and the **angle of tilt** is a direct measure of how far that child moved.

The LVDT works in exactly the same way:

- The **primary winding** = the pivot (excited with a fixed AC signal).
- The **two secondary windings** = the two ends of the see-saw.
- The **soft-iron core** = the child that moves.
- The **differential output voltage** = the angle of tilt.

When the core is **perfectly centered**, both secondaries induce **equal and opposite** EMFs → **net output = 0 V**. When the core shifts, one secondary gets more flux, the other gets less → a **non-zero differential voltage** appears, whose **amplitude** tells us *how far* the core moved, and whose **phase** tells us *which direction*.

> **Key physical constants / standards used in LVDT calibration:**
> - **Excitation frequency:** **1 kHz to 5 kHz** (most common: **2.5 kHz**)
> - **Excitation voltage:** **1 V to 24 V RMS** (typical: **5 V RMS**)
> - **Sensitivity range:** **0.1 mV/V/mm to 10 mV/V/mm** (depends on design)
> - **Standard displacement range:** **±0.1 mm to ±250 mm**

---

## 1.3 Classification of the LVDT

| Class | Description | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **By Output** | AC LVDT (raw differential output) vs. DC LVDT (built-in oscillator + demodulator) | Both are seen in labs |
| **By Range** | Miniature (±1 mm), Standard (±25 mm), Long-stroke (±250 mm) | Standard range is lab-typical |
| **By Construction** | Semi-closed, Closed (push-rod) type | Push-rod type is most common in student setups |

> [!TIP]
> In a typical **KTU instrumentation lab**, the LVDT is mounted on a **calibration jig** with a **micrometer head** (resolution **0.01 mm**) used to apply precise mechanical input, and a **digital voltmeter (DVM)** to read the output.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of LVDT — Step-by-Step

1. **Former (Bobbin):** A hollow cylindrical non-magnetic tube (often **stainless steel** or **fibre-glass**) that holds the windings. It provides mechanical rigidity and a low-friction path for the core.
2. **Primary Winding (P):** Uniformly wound along the central portion of the former. Connected to an **AC excitation source** $V_{ex}$.
3. **Two Secondary Windings (S₁ and S₂):** Identical in terms of turns, wire gauge, and winding pattern. They are wound on either side of the primary and connected in **series opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract).
4. **Movable Core (Armature):** A cylindrical slug of high-permeability **nickel-iron alloy (e.g., Mu-metal, 78% Ni, 22% Fe)** or **soft iron**. It slides freely inside the former.
5. **Shielding:** An outer magnetic shield (often **mu-metal can**) is provided to protect against external electromagnetic interference (EMI).
6. **End Plates & Connectors:** Mechanical mounting and electrical lead termination.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle — The "Why" and "How"

### **Why** does the LVDT need AC excitation?
Because the output relies on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, which requires a **time-varying flux** ($e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$). A DC input would induce a voltage only at the instant of switching, not continuously.

### **How** does displacement produce an output?

Let $e_{s1}$ and $e_{s2}$ be the EMFs induced in the two secondaries.

- **Null Position (Core centered):** The flux linking S₁ and S₂ is **symmetrical** → $e_{s1} = e_{s2}$. Since they are in series opposition, the net output $e_o = 0$.

- **Core displaced towards S₁ (say, +x):** Flux linkage with S₁ **increases** → $e_{s1}$ rises. Flux linkage with S₂ **decreases** → $e_{s2}$ falls. Net $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} > 0$ (and in phase with primary).

- **Core displaced towards S₂ (say, −x):** By symmetry, $e_{s2} > e_{s1}$ → net $e_o < 0$ (and **180° out of phase** with primary). This **phase reversal** is how the LVDT indicates direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The LVDT output is **ratiometric** — if the excitation voltage doubles, the output doubles. Hence, sensitivity is always expressed as **mV per V of excitation per mm of displacement** (units: **mV/V/mm**) for an excitation-independent specification.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Differential output voltage | $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$ | Net output across the series-opposing secondaries | **V (RMS)** |
| 2 | Output at null | $e_o \big\vert_{x=0} = 0$ | Residual voltage due to winding asymmetry | **V** |
| 3 | Sensitivity (raw) | $S = \dfrac{\Delta e_o}{\Delta x}$ | Slope of linear portion of calibration curve | **mV/mm** |
| 4 | Ratiometric sensitivity | $S_r = \dfrac{S}{V_{ex}}$ | Excitation-normalized sensitivity | **mV/V/mm** |
| 5 | Percentage Non-Linearity | $\%NL = \dfrac{\vert e_{actual} - e_{ideal} \vert_{max}}{e_{FS}} \times 100$ | Worst-case deviation from best-fit line | **%** |
| 6 | Full-Scale Output | $e_{FS}$ | Output at maximum rated displacement | **V** |
| 7 | Phase indicator | $\phi$ | $0°$ = one direction; $180°$ = opposite direction | **degrees** |
| 8 | Resolution | Theoretical: infinite (no friction); Practical: limited by **DVM** | Smallest detectable displacement | **µm** |

> **KTU Board-Exam Tip:** Memorize **Formulae 1, 3, and 5** — these three are the **most-frequently-asked** in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Hydraulic actuator position feedback in landing gear and flight-control surfaces |
| **Civil Engineering** | Crack-width monitoring, structural deflection of bridges, load-testing of columns |
| **Manufacturing** | Tool-setting on CNC machines, in-process gauging, press-stroke monitoring |
| **Power Plants** | Valve stem position, turbine blade tip clearance |
| **Robotics** | Closed-loop linear servo control of pick-and-place arms |
| **Automotive** | Suspension travel sensors, throttle position sensing |

> **Why LVDT is preferred over a potentiometer:** **Infinite resolution, no contact wear, frictionless motion, long life (>10⁸ cycles), and high linearity (±0.1% to ±0.5% of FS).**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage Equation of an LVDT

We model the LVDT as two coupled transformers, each consisting of the primary and one secondary. Let:

- $N_p$ = number of turns in the primary winding
- $N_{s1}, N_{s2}$ = number of turns in secondary windings 1 and 2 (with $N_{s1} = N_{s2} = N_s$ for a balanced LVDT)
- $V_{ex}$ = primary excitation voltage (RMS)
- $M_1, M_2$ = mutual inductances between primary and secondary 1, and primary and secondary 2, respectively
- $f$ = excitation frequency (Hz)
- $x$ = core displacement from null (positive towards S₁, negative towards S₂)

### **Step 1: Express the mutual inductances as functions of core position**

For a **uniformly wound, linear LVDT**, the mutual inductances vary **linearly** with displacement **only over the central linear range**. Let $k$ be a constant that depends on the core permeability, number of turns, and the geometry. Then:

$$M_1(x) = M_0 + k \cdot x$$

$$M_2(x) = M_0 - k \cdot x$$

**Logic:** When the core moves **towards S₁**, the magnetic coupling between P and S₁ **increases** linearly (so $M_1$ grows), while the coupling between P and S₂ **decreases** (so $M_2$ shrinks). The constant $M_0$ is the **mutual inductance at null** (when $x = 0$).

### **Step 2: Induced EMF in each secondary**

By the **transformer EMF equation**, the RMS voltage induced in a secondary winding due to mutual coupling is:

$$e_{s} = 2 \pi f \cdot M \cdot I_{p}$$

where $I_p$ is the primary RMS current. Alternatively, using the primary voltage and turns ratio:

$$e_{s1}(x) = \frac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_{ex} \cdot \frac{M_1(x)}{M_{max}}$$

For a clean derivation, we use the **coupled-inductor model** directly. The induced EMF in secondary $i$ is:

$$e_{si} = j \omega M_i I_p$$

where $j\omega = j \cdot 2\pi f$. Therefore:

$$e_{s1}(x) = j \omega (M_0 + kx) I_p$$

$$e_{s2}(x) = j \omega (M_0 - kx) I_p$$

### **Step 3: Compute the differential output**

The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, so the net output voltage is the **subtraction**:

$$e_o(x) = e_{s1}(x) - e_{s2}(x)$$

Substituting:

$$e_o(x) = j \omega (M_0 + kx) I_p - j \omega (M_0 - kx) I_p$$

### **Step 4: Simplify the expression**

The $M_0$ terms cancel exactly:

$$e_o(x) = j \omega \, kx \, I_p - j \omega (-kx) \, I_p$$

$$e_o(x) = 2 \, j \omega \, k \, x \, I_p$$

### **Step 5: Final compact form**

$$\boxed{\,e_o(x) = 2 \, j \omega k \, I_p \cdot x\,}$$

**Logic of the cancellation:** The derivation elegantly shows that the **DC offset (drift) introduced by the fixed primary current is completely rejected** — only the displacement-dependent part survives. This is why the LVDT is intrinsically a **zero-drift differential transducer**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** In the absence of $j \omega$, students often miss the **complex (phasor) nature** of the output. Examiners frequently award a **bonus mark** for explicitly mentioning that the **output phase** depends on the **sign of x** — i.e., $e_o$ reverses phase by **180°** when the core crosses the null position.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Percentage Non-Linearity (%NL)

During calibration, the student applies $n$ known displacements $x_i$ and records outputs $e_i$.

### **Step 1: Best-fit straight line**

Use the **least-squares method** to find the best-fit line through the data:

$$e_{fit}(x) = m \cdot x + c$$

where the slope $m$ and intercept $c$ are computed from:

$$m = \frac{n \sum x_i e_i - \sum x_i \sum e_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2}$$

$$c = \frac{\sum e_i - m \sum x_i}{n}$$

### **Step 2: Compute residuals**

For each $i$:

$$\Delta e_i = e_i - e_{fit}(x_i)$$

### **Step 3: Find the maximum absolute deviation**

$$\vert \Delta e \vert_{max} = \max_{i} \vert \Delta e_i \vert$$

### **Step 4: Compute percentage non-linearity**

$$\boxed{\,\%NL = \frac{\vert \Delta e \vert_{max}}{e_{FS}} \times 100\,}$$

where $e_{FS}$ is the full-scale output at the **maximum rated displacement**.

**Engineering interpretation:** A **%NL of 0.5%** means the LVDT deviates from a perfectly linear response by at most **0.5% of full-scale** over its operating range. A **high-quality LVDT** has **%NL ≤ 0.1%**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of LVDT Operating Zones

| Zone | Core Position | Mutual Inductance | Output | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Null Zone** | Centered ($x = 0$) | $M_1 = M_2 = M_0$ | $e_o = 0$ | Ideal zero point |
| **Positive Half** | Towards S₁ ($+x$) | $M_1 > M_2$ | $e_o > 0$, in phase | Linear region |
| **Negative Half** | Towards S₂ ($-x$) | $M_1 < M_2$ | $e_o > 0$, 180° out of phase | Linear region |
| **Over-range** | Beyond winding ends | $M_1$ or $M_2$ saturates | $e_o$ flattens | **Non-linear — must be avoided** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LVDT Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXC["AC EXCITATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        A1[Oscillator\n1 kHz to 5 kHz]
        A2[Amplifier\n1 V to 24 V RMS]
    end

    subgraph LVDT["LVDT TRANSDUCER CORE"]
        P[Primary Winding P]
        S1[Secondary Winding S1]
        S2[Secondary Winding S2]
        CORE[Movable Soft-Iron Core\nDisplacement Input x]
    end

    subgraph DEMOD["SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
        D1[Differential Amplifier]
        D2[Phase-Sensitive Demodulator]
        D3[Low-Pass Filter]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT STAGE"]
        O1[DC Voltmeter / DVM]
        O2[Display or PLC Input]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> P
    CORE -. Couples flux .-> S1
    CORE -. Couples flux .-> S2
    S1 --> D1
    S2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2

    classDef excStyle fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#3366CC,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lvdtStyle fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF8800,stroke-width:2px
    classDef demodStyle fill:#E8FFE8,stroke:#229922,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outStyle fill:#FFE8F0,stroke:#CC3366,stroke-width:2px

    class A1,A2 excStyle
    class P,S1,S2,CORE lvdtStyle
    class D1,D2,D3 demodStyle
    class O1,O2 outStyle
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **blue subsystem (left)** generates the AC excitation.
- The **orange subsystem (center)** is the LVDT itself — the dashed lines show the **magnetic flux coupling** through the movable core.
- The **green subsystem** is the **signal conditioning chain** that converts the raw differential AC into a usable DC voltage.
- The **pink subsystem (right)** is the readout.

---

## 4.2 Internal Construction of the LVDT (Cross-Sectional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CORE_ASSEMBLY["LVDT INTERNAL CONSTRUCTION"]
        SHIELD["Outer Mu-Metal Shield\n(EMI Protection)"]
        FORMER["Hollow Cylindrical Former\nNon-Magnetic Material"]
        P_W["PRIMARY WINDING P\n(Excitation Coil)"]
        S1_W["SECONDARY S1\n(Upper Pickup Coil)"]
        S2_W["SECONDARY S2\n(Lower Pickup Coil)"]
        ARMATURE["MOVABLE CORE\nNickel-Iron Alloy Armature\nDisplacement = x"]
    end

    SHIELD --- FORMER
    FORMER --- S1_W
    S1_W --- P_W
    P_W --- S2_W
    S2_W --- ARMATURE

    classDef wndStyle fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#FF6600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef coreStyle fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:3px
    classDef shldStyle fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    class P_W,S1_W,S2_W wndStyle
    class ARMATURE coreStyle
    class SHIELD,FORMER shldStyle
```

---

## 4.3 Calibration Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start Calibration]) --> SET[Set Excitation\nVex and frequency f]
    SET --> ZERO[Adjust micrometer\nto obtain null position]
    ZERO --> READ0[Record eo = 0 at x = 0]
    READ0 --> INC[Increment x by 0.5 mm\nor 1 mm step]
    INC --> READ[Record eo for each x]
    READ --> DIR{Displacement\nin both\ndirections?}
    DIR -->|Yes| INC
    DIR -->|No| PLOT[Plot eo vs x\nCalibration Curve]
    PLOT --> FIT[Fit Best Straight Line\nLeast-Squares Method]
    FIT --> CALC[Compute Sensitivity S\nand %NL]
    CALC --> REPORT([Generate Calibration Report])

    classDef procStyle fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ioStyle fill:#FFE8E8,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    class SET,ZERO,INC,PLOT,FIT,CALC procStyle
    class START,REPORT,READ0,READ,DIR ioStyle
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — 3-Mark Questions**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define LVDT. List any two advantages of LVDT over a potentiometric displacement sensor.**

**Model Answer:**

> **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a passive electromechanical transducer used to convert **linear displacement** into a proportional **electrical (AC) voltage signal**. It consists of a single primary winding and two secondary windings wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable soft-iron core. The output is the **differential voltage** between the two secondaries, which varies with the position of the core.

**Advantages over a potentiometer:**
1. **Infinite resolution** — the LVDT has no contact or friction between the core and the windings, so it can detect displacements as small as a few micrometers, limited only by the readout instrument.
2. **Frictionless operation and long life** — no mechanical wear, so it can operate for over **10⁸ cycles** without degradation, unlike a resistive potentiometer whose wiper wears out.
3. *(Bonus)* **High linearity** (±0.1% to ±0.5% of full scale) and **bidirectional measurement** with phase-based direction indication.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why is AC excitation required for an LVDT? What is the role of the soft-iron core?**

**Model Answer:**

**AC excitation is required** because the LVDT operates on the principle of **electromagnetic induction** (Faraday's Law), which states that an EMF is induced in a coil only when there is a **time-varying magnetic flux** linking it. A DC excitation would induce voltage only at the instant of switch-on or switch-off, not continuously. Therefore, a **sinusoidal AC carrier** (typically **1 kHz to 5 kHz, 5 V RMS**) is applied to the primary.

**Role of the soft-iron core:** The core is made of a **high-permeability, low-retentivity** nickel-iron alloy (e.g., **mu-metal**) to:
1. **Concentrate and channel** the magnetic flux from the primary to the two secondaries.
2. **Vary the mutual inductance** between the primary and each secondary as a function of its position.
3. Provide **low hysteresis loss** so the output responds quickly and linearly to mechanical displacement.

---

## **Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle** of an LVDT used for displacement measurement. Show that the output voltage is **directly proportional to core displacement** over the linear range. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During the calibration of an LVDT, the following readings were taken (excitation = 5 V RMS, 2.5 kHz):

| Displacement x (mm) | −5 | −3 | −1 | 0 | +1 | +3 | +5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output e₀ (mV) | −285 | −172 | −58 | 0 | +57 | +171 | +282 |

Plot the calibration curve and determine the **(i)** Sensitivity in mV/mm, **(ii)** Percentage non-linearity at ±5 mm, and **(iii)** the ratiometric sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to A(a):**

Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes for the construction diagram.

**Working Principle:**

The LVDT has a **primary winding P** energized by an AC source $V_{ex}$. Two identical **secondary windings S₁ and S₂** are connected in **series opposition** on either side of the primary. A movable **soft-iron core** is placed inside the hollow former.

When AC is applied, an alternating flux is produced in the primary, which links both secondaries through the core. The induced EMFs are:

$$e_{s1} = k_1 \cdot x_1 \quad \text{and} \quad e_{s2} = k_2 \cdot x_2$$

where $x_1, x_2$ represent the effective flux linkage to each secondary.

**Case 1 — Null position ($x = 0$):** The core is symmetric → equal flux links S₁ and S₂ → $e_{s1} = e_{s2}$ → **net output $e_o = 0$**.

**Case 2 — Core displaced by $+x$ (towards S₁):** Flux to S₁ increases, flux to S₂ decreases → $e_{s1} > e_{s2}$ → $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} > 0$, in phase with primary.

**Case 3 — Core displaced by $-x$ (towards S₂):** By symmetry, $e_{s2} > e_{s1}$ → $e_o < 0$ in magnitude but **180° out of phase** with primary, indicating opposite direction.

**Proportionality proof (sketch):**

Starting from the **derived expression in Section 3.1**:

$$e_o(x) = 2 \, j \omega k \, I_p \cdot x$$

For a **fixed excitation** (constant $V_{ex}$, constant $I_p$, constant $\omega$, constant $k$), $e_o$ is a **linear function of $x$**:

$$e_o = K \cdot x \quad \text{where} \quad K = 2 \omega k I_p = \text{constant sensitivity}$$

This confirms the **linear relationship** in the central operating range. ∎

---

#### **Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1 — Plot the calibration curve**

```
   eo (mV)
   +300 |                          *
   +250 |
   +200 |
   +150 |                  *
   +100 |
   +50  |          *
      0 |---------*---------*-----------> x (mm)
   -50  |          *
   -100 |
   -150 |                  *
   -200 |
   -250 |
   -300 |                          *
        -5   -3   -1   0  +1  +3  +5
```

The curve is a **straight line through the origin** with two symmetric branches (positive and negative x).

**Step 2 — Compute Sensitivity**

Using the **end points** (over the full range of 10 mm):

$$S = \frac{\Delta e_o}{\Delta x} = \frac{+282 - (-285)}{+5 - (-5)} = \frac{567}{10} = 56.7 \; \text{mV/mm}$$

*(Verify with midpoints: $\frac{171 - (-172)}{3 - (-3)} = \frac{343}{6} \approx 57.2$ mV/mm — consistent.)*

**Average sensitivity: $S \approx 56.9 \; \text{mV/mm}$** ✓

**Step 3 — Ratiometric Sensitivity**

$$S_r = \frac{S}{V_{ex}} = \frac{56.9 \; \text{mV/mm}}{5 \; \text{V}} = 11.38 \; \text{mV/V/mm}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Non-Linearity at ±5 mm**

The **ideal output at ±5 mm** (from the best-fit line) would be:

$$e_{ideal}(\pm 5) = 56.9 \times (\pm 5) = \pm 284.5 \; \text{mV}$$

**Actual outputs:** $+282$ mV and $-285$ mV.

**Deviations:**

$$\Delta e_{+5} = \vert 282 - 284.5 \vert = 2.5 \; \text{mV}$$

$$\Delta e_{-5} = \vert -285 - (-284.5) \vert = 0.5 \; \text{mV}$$

**Maximum deviation:** $\vert \Delta e \vert_{max} = 2.5 \; \text{mV}$

**Full-scale output** (at $x = +5$ mm):

$$e_{FS} = +282 \; \text{mV}$$

$$\%NL = \frac{2.5}{282} \times 100 = 0.887 \; \%$$

**Final Answers:**
- Sensitivity $S = 56.9 \; \text{mV/mm}$
- Ratiometric sensitivity $S_r = 11.38 \; \text{mV/V/mm}$
- Percentage non-linearity $= 0.887 \; \%$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a **phasor diagram** the operation of an LVDT in the (i) **null position**, (ii) **core displaced towards S₁**, and (iii) **core displaced towards S₂**. State clearly how the **direction of displacement** is identified from the output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LVDT has the following specifications: **Range = ±10 mm, Excitation = 5 V at 2 kHz, Sensitivity = 50 mV/V/mm**. If a **differential amplifier of gain 100** is used at the output, calculate the **(i)** output voltage at full-scale displacement, **(ii)** output voltage per mm after amplification, and **(iii)** the smallest displacement that can be resolved by a **digital voltmeter of 0.1 mV resolution** (after amplification). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B(a):**

**Phasor Analysis of LVDT Operation**

The primary voltage $V_p$ produces a primary flux $\phi_p$, which links the two secondaries.

**(i) Null Position** — Core is exactly centered.

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $E_{s1}$ magnitude | $E$ |
| $E_{s2}$ magnitude | $E$ |
| $E_{s1}$ direction | Reference (0°) |
| $E_{s2}$ direction | 180° (opposition) |
| **Net output $E_o$** | **$E - E = 0$** |

Phasor: $\vec{E}_{s1}$ and $\vec{E}_{s2}$ are **equal and opposite** → they cancel exactly.

**(ii) Core displaced towards S₁ (positive x)** — Flux to S₁ increases.

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $E_{s1}$ magnitude | $E + \Delta E$ |
| $E_{s2}$ magnitude | $E - \Delta E$ |
| $E_{s2}$ still 180° opposed to $E_{s1}$ | |
| **Net output $E_o$** | $(E + \Delta E) - (E - \Delta E) = 2\Delta E$ **in phase with $E_{s1}$ (and with $V_p$)** |

**(iii) Core displaced towards S₂ (negative x)** — Flux to S₂ increases.

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $E_{s1}$ magnitude | $E - \Delta E$ |
| $E_{s2}$ magnitude | $E + \Delta E$ |
| **Net output $E_o$** | $(E - \Delta E) - (E + \Delta E) = -2\Delta E$ |

The negative sign means $\vec{E}_o$ is **180° out of phase** with the primary voltage.

**Direction identification:** A **phase-sensitive demodulator** compares the phase of $E_o$ with that of $V_p$. If they are in phase → core is on the **S₁ side (positive x)**. If they are **180° out of phase** → core is on the **S₂ side (negative x)**. Thus, the **phase of the output encodes direction**, while the **magnitude encodes distance**. ∎

---

#### **Solution to B(b):**

**Given:**
- Range: $\pm 10$ mm
- $V_{ex} = 5$ V
- Sensitivity: $S_r = 50 \; \text{mV/V/mm}$
- Differential amplifier gain: $A = 100$
- DVM resolution: $0.1$ mV

**Step 1 — Raw LVDT sensitivity (in mV/mm)**

$$S = S_r \times V_{ex} = 50 \; \text{mV/V/mm} \times 5 \; \text{V} = 250 \; \text{mV/mm}$$

**Step 2 — (i) Output at full-scale displacement ($x = \pm 10$ mm)**

Raw LVDT output:

$$e_o(\pm 10) = S \times (\pm 10) = 250 \times (\pm 10) = \pm 2500 \; \text{mV} = \pm 2.5 \; \text{V}$$

After amplification by gain $A = 100$:

$$V_{out,FS} = e_o \times A = \pm 2.5 \; \text{V} \times 100 = \pm 250 \; \text{V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This large output voltage exceeds typical op-amp supply rails (±15 V).** In practice, the gain is reduced to avoid saturation — but the question asks for the *theoretical* amplified output. KTU board answers should clearly state the **practical limitation**.

**Step 3 — (ii) Output voltage per mm after amplification**

$$V_{out} \text{ per mm} = S \times A = 250 \; \text{mV/mm} \times 100 = 25{,}000 \; \text{mV/mm} = 25 \; \text{V/mm}$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Smallest resolvable displacement**

The DVM reads in **0.1 mV increments** (after amplification). The smallest displacement that produces a 0.1 mV change at the amplified output is:

$$x_{min} = \frac{V_{DVM,res}}{V_{out}/\text{mm}} = \frac{0.1 \; \text{mV}}{25{,}000 \; \text{mV/mm}}$$

$$x_{min} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{mm} = 4 \; \text{nm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Amplified full-scale output: $\pm 250 \; \text{V}$ *(theoretical)*
- (ii) Amplified output per mm: $25 \; \text{V/mm}$
- (iii) Smallest resolvable displacement: **$4 \; \text{nm}$**

---

## **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on LVDT questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units of ratiometric sensitivity** — Writing $S = 50$ mV/mm instead of $50$ mV/V/mm. The **/V** is **mandatory** because the same LVDT driven by 10 V will produce twice the output.
> 2. **Ignoring the phase information** — Students compute only the magnitude and lose 2 marks for not mentioning that the **180° phase shift** indicates the direction of displacement.
> 3. **Confusing AC and DC LVDT** — A DC LVDT has a built-in **oscillator and demodulator**; the output is a **DC voltage proportional to displacement**. The question stem must be read carefully to identify which type.
> 4. **Sign errors in the best-fit line** — The intercept $c$ should be **≈ 0** for a well-calibrated LVDT. A non-zero $c$ indicates **winding asymmetry or residual voltage ($E_{residual}$)**.
> 5. **In the %NL calculation** — Use **$e_{FS}$** (full-scale output) in the denominator, **not the maximum deviation**. This is the most common mistake.
> 6. **Drawing the phasor wrong** — Always show $E_{s1}$ and $E_{s2}$ in **opposition (180° apart)**, and clearly mark the **resultant** that gives the differential output.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **LVDT** = Linear Variable Differential Transformer = passive, AC-excited, **mutual-induction** displacement sensor.
- **Three windings**: one **primary** (center) + two **secondaries** (ends, connected in **series opposition**).
- **One moving part**: **soft-iron (mu-metal) core** slides inside the hollow former.
- **Excitation**: typically **1 kHz to 5 kHz, 5 V RMS** sinusoidal.
- **Null position** (core centered) → **output = 0 V** (theoretically).
- **Magnitude of $e_o$** $\propto$ **displacement** $|x|$.
- **Phase of $e_o$** (relative to $V_p$) $\to$ encodes **direction** (0° or 180°).
- **Sensitivity** $S = \Delta e_o / \Delta x$ in **mV/mm**.
- **Ratiometric sensitivity** $S_r = S / V_{ex}$ in **mV/V/mm** (the standard, excitation-independent specification).
- **%NL** $= \dfrac{\vert \Delta e \vert_{max}}{e_{FS}} \times 100$; a good LVDT has **%NL ≤ 0.5%**.
- **Resolution** is **theoretically infinite** (frictionless); practically limited by the readout DVM.
- **Real-world applications**: aerospace actuators, civil structural monitoring, CNC tool-setting, valve position, robotics, automotive suspension.
- **Advantages over potentiometer**: no contact wear, infinite resolution, long life, frictionless, bidirectional with phase detection.
- **Disadvantages**: requires AC excitation and signal conditioning, more expensive than simple resistive sensors.
- **Key derivation to remember**: $e_o(x) = 2 j\omega k I_p \cdot x$ — the **DC offset cancels**, leaving only the displacement-proportional term.
- **Calibration procedure**: (1) Set excitation, (2) Adjust to null, (3) Increment micrometer, (4) Record $e_o$ for each $x$, (5) Plot curve, (6) Find best-fit line, (7) Compute $S$, $S_r$, and %NL.
- **DC LVDT** = AC LVDT + built-in **oscillator + phase-sensitive demodulator + filter** → gives a **0–10 V DC output** proportional to displacement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Pressure Gauge Using Dead Weight Pressure Gauge Tester
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Pressure Gauge Using Dead Weight Pressure Gauge Tester

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration** is the process of comparing the output of an instrument (here, a pressure gauge) against a reference standard of known accuracy, and documenting the deviation (error) between the indicated value and the true value across the operating range.

A **Dead Weight Pressure Gauge Tester (DWPT)**, also called a **Dead Weight Tester (DWT)** or **Piston Gauge**, is a **primary pressure standard** used in metrology laboratories to generate precisely known hydrostatic pressures by stacking calibrated masses on a precision-machined piston that floats inside a closely fitted cylinder.

The fundamental governing equation is:

$$P = \frac{W}{A_{eff}} = \frac{m \cdot g}{A_{eff}}$$

where $W$ is the total downward force (weight), $m$ is the total mass (piston + weights), $g$ is the local acceleration due to gravity, and $A_{eff}$ is the effective cross-sectional area of the piston.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Piston & Stack of Coins" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a plastic syringe held vertically with its nozzle sealed, and a perfectly smooth metal rod (piston) placed inside the barrel. The metal rod slides with negligible friction. If you gently place coins on top of the rod, each coin adds a small, exact, predictable force. The pressure that builds up inside the fluid below the rod is simply the total coin-weight divided by the rod's cross-section. The Dead Weight Tester is exactly this — but engineered to laboratory precision with a 0.0001 mm tolerance cylinder, optically flat surfaces, and traceable calibration masses.**

The DWT is therefore called a **"primary standard"** because the pressure it produces is derived **directly from SI base units** (mass in kg, length in m, time in s) — not from another pressure instrument. The accuracy of a well-maintained DWT can be as high as **0.01 % of reading** or **0.005 % of full scale**, depending on class.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants Used in Calibration

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Standard gravity | $g_n$ | **9.80665 m/s²** (CIPM, 1901) |
| Typical Indian KERALA gravity | $g_{loc}$ | **≈ 9.78 m/s²** (varies ±0.02) |
| Atmospheric pressure (Kerala avg.) | $P_{atm}$ | **≈ 1.01325 bar** |
| 1 bar | — | **= 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa** |
| 1 kgf/cm² | — | **= 98066.5 Pa ≈ 0.9807 bar** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Examiners explicitly test whether students used the *local* value of $g$ rather than the textbook-standard 9.81. In Kerala, $g \approx 9.78$ m/s². This is a **favourite 2-mark trap** in the viva.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Hydraulic Force Balance

The DWT works on a **hydraulic force-balance principle**, where the gravitational force exerted by a stack of calibrated masses is balanced against the hydraulic pressure acting on a precision piston.

### 2.1.1 Why the Piston "Floats"

The piston-cylinder assembly is manufactured to a clearance of typically **1 to 5 micrometres**. The hydraulic fluid (usually a low-viscosity mineral oil, silicone oil, or a water-glycol mixture) leaks past the piston in a controlled, laminar flow regime. This thin oil film:

1. **Lubricates** the piston, eliminating solid friction.
2. **Centres** the piston automatically (hydrodynamic alignment).
3. Allows the piston to **rotate slowly** (driven by a rotating weight table or hand-spin), which averages out any lateral error.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why rotation?** A piston that does not rotate settles to the lowest friction spot and gets stuck. Slow rotation (1–30 RPM) keeps the piston in a constant state of "dynamic float" — the gold standard for low uncertainty.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. The tester is filled with hydraulic fluid, and all air is bled out.
2. The **piston** (with its integral weight table) is placed on the fluid column.
3. A **single calibrated weight** is loaded. The piston descends slightly, building pressure, and then stabilises when the generated pressure exactly supports the weight.
4. The gauge under test, connected to the same fluid line, now reads the pressure.
5. **More weights are added in steps**, producing a staircase of known pressures.
6. The DWT reading and the gauge reading are tabulated for every step. The deviation is the **calibration error** of the gauge.

## 2.2 The Governing Equations — A Closer Look

The pressure generated at the base of the piston is **not** simply $P = W/A$. A real DWT must account for:

### 2.2.1 Effective Area (Pressure Distortion Correction)

When pressure acts inside the cylinder, the cylinder expands slightly (its inside diameter grows) and the piston compresses slightly. The **effective area** is:

$$A_{eff} = A_0 \, (1 + \lambda \cdot P)$$

where $A_0$ is the area at zero pressure, $P$ is the generated pressure, and $\lambda$ is the **pressure distortion coefficient** (typically $\approx 1 \times 10^{-5}$ /bar). For a Class-A DWT working below 70 % of full scale, this correction is **< 0.01 %** and is often neglected at undergraduate level.

### 2.2.2 Surface Tension / Buoyancy Correction

The fluid column exerts a small **upward buoyancy** on the piston. The magnitude is the weight of the displaced fluid:

$$F_{buoy} = \rho_{fluid} \cdot A_{eff} \cdot h \cdot g$$

For oil ($\rho \approx 870$ kg/m³) and a column of 0.1 m, this contributes only ~0.001 % and is **negligible in KTU lab work**.

### 2.2.3 Master Equation Used in the KTU Lab

$$\boxed{P_{generated} \;=\; \frac{(m_{piston} + m_{weights}) \cdot g_{local}}{A_{eff}}}$$

If the gauge reads a value $P_{gauge}$ at the same instant:

$$\boxed{\text{Calibration Error, } e \;=\; P_{gauge} - P_{generated}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Percentage Error, } \%e \;=\; \frac{P_{gauge} - P_{generated}}{P_{generated}} \times 100 \;\%}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:------|:--------|
| 1 | Generated pressure | $P = (m \cdot g) \,/\, A_{eff}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Master equation |
| 2 | Mass term | $m = m_{piston} + \sum m_{weights}$ | kg | Always include piston mass |
| 3 | Effective area | $A_{eff} = (\pi/4) \cdot D_{eff}^{2}$ | m² | Use calibrated value from NPL/NABL certificate |
| 4 | Piston diameter | $D_{eff}$ | m | Given in tester certificate (e.g., 3.354 × 10⁻³ m²) |
| 5 | Local gravity (Kerala) | $g = 9.78$ m/s² | m/s² | Use this, not 9.81 |
| 6 | Standard gravity | $g_n = 9.80665$ m/s² | m/s² | Only if instrument is referenced to it |
| 7 | Pressure in bar | $P_{bar} = P_{Pa} \,/\, 10^{5}$ | bar | 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa |
| 8 | Pressure in kgf/cm² | $P_{kgf/cm^2} = P_{Pa} \,/\, 98066.5$ | kgf/cm² | 1 kgf/cm² = 98066.5 Pa |
| 9 | Calibration error (absolute) | $e = P_{gauge} - P_{true}$ | Pa or bar | Signed value |
| 10 | Calibration error (% FS) | $\%FS = (e \,/\, \text{Full Scale}) \times 100$ | % | Most common industrial metric |
| 11 | Repeatability | $R = P_{max} - P_{min}$ at same load | Pa | Three ascending + three descending readings |
| 12 | Hysteresis | $H = P_{asc} - P_{des}$ at same load | Pa | Indicates friction/damping |
| 13 | Linearity (max dev.) | $L = \vert P_{ind} - P_{ideal\,line}\vert_{max}$ | Pa | Best-fit straight line method |
| 14 | Combined uncertainty | $u_c = \sqrt{u_{cal}^{2} + u_{drift}^{2} + u_{res}^{2} + u_{g}^{2}}$ | Pa | k=2 for 95 % confidence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common student trap:** When the dead weight tester piston has an area quoted in **cm²** (e.g., 0.5 cm²) and weights in **kgf**, the formula becomes $P = W/A$ directly in **kgf/cm²**, bypassing the need to invoke $g$. But if weights are in **kg** and area in **m²**, you MUST include $g$ in m/s² and the answer will be in **Pa**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Calibration laboratories (NABL-accredited):** Reference standard for all pressure gauges, transmitters, and transducers.
- **Oil & gas industry:** Calibration of well-head pressure sensors and pipeline pressure transmitters.
- **Aerospace:** Validation of altimeter and air-data computer pressure sensors.
- **Process plants:** Periodic in-situ calibration of Bourdon tube gauges, diaphragm gauges, and DP transmitters.
- **Pharmaceutical & food:** Sanitary pressure transmitter calibration on CIP/SIP skids.
- **KTU context:** A standard undergraduate lab experiment to demonstrate traceability, error analysis, and uncertainty computation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Force Balance to the Master Equation

### Step 1 — Force exerted by the loaded mass

A piston of mass $m_p$ carries a stack of weights of total mass $m_w$. By Newton's second law, the downward gravitational force on the piston-weight assembly is:

$$F_{down} = (m_p + m_w) \cdot g$$

### Step 2 — Pressure developed in the fluid

The fluid below the piston transmits this force to the gauge via Pascal's law. The pressure at the gauge equals force per unit area:

$$P = \frac{F_{down}}{A_{eff}} = \frac{(m_p + m_w) \cdot g}{A_{eff}}$$

### Step 3 — Substituting effective area in terms of diameter

For a circular piston of effective diameter $D_{eff}$:

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_{eff}^{2}$$

Therefore the master equation in expanded form is:

$$\boxed{P = \frac{4 \cdot (m_p + m_w) \cdot g}{\pi \cdot D_{eff}^{2}}}$$

### Step 4 — Conversion to bar (the practical unit in the lab)

$$P_{bar} = \frac{1}{10^{5}} \cdot \frac{4 \cdot (m_p + m_w) \cdot g}{\pi \cdot D_{eff}^{2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Fully Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A dead weight pressure tester has an effective piston area of $A_{eff} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m² (i.e. 1 cm²). The piston itself weighs 0.5 kgf. A total load of 6.0 kgf (piston + weights) is placed on the weight table. Using $g = 9.81$ m/s², determine (i) the generated pressure in Pa, (ii) the generated pressure in bar, and (iii) the generated pressure in kgf/cm². If the pressure gauge under test reads 5.85 bar at this load, calculate the percentage error of the gauge.

### Solution

#### (i) Pressure in Pascals

**Step 1 — Convert the load to Newtons.** Total downward force is:

$$F = m \cdot g = 6.0 \times 9.81 = 58.86 \; \text{N}$$

(Using 6.0 kg as a mass value here; 1 kgf = 9.81 N.)

**Step 2 — Divide force by effective area.**

$$P = \frac{F}{A_{eff}} = \frac{58.86}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$P = 5.886 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa}$$

#### (ii) Pressure in bar

$$P_{bar} = \frac{P_{Pa}}{10^{5}} = \frac{5.886 \times 10^{5}}{10^{5}}$$

$$P_{bar} = 5.886 \; \text{bar}$$

#### (iii) Pressure in kgf/cm²

**Step 1 — Direct method (kgf, cm²):**

$$P_{kgf/cm^{2}} = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{6.0 \; \text{kgf}}{1.0 \; \text{cm}^{2}} = 6.0 \; \text{kgf/cm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Verification via Pascals:**

$$P_{kgf/cm^{2}} = \frac{5.886 \times 10^{5}}{98066.5} = 6.002 \; \text{kgf/cm}^{2}$$

(The tiny difference is because 1 kgf = 9.80665 N, not 9.81 N.)

#### (iv) Percentage Calibration Error

**Step 1 — Error (absolute):**

$$e = P_{gauge} - P_{true} = 5.85 - 5.886 = -0.036 \; \text{bar}$$

**Step 2 — Percentage of true value:**

$$\%e = \frac{e}{P_{true}} \times 100 = \frac{-0.036}{5.886} \times 100$$

$$\%e = -0.611 \; \%$$

**Step 3 — Percentage of full scale** (assuming gauge FS = 10 bar):

$$\%FS = \frac{-0.036}{10.0} \times 100 = -0.36 \; \%FS$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A negative error means the gauge **under-reads** (reads less than the true pressure). For acceptance under typical industrial standards (e.g., ISA RP 7.7), the gauge must be within ±0.5 % of full scale. Our result of −0.36 %FS is **within tolerance**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stepwise Calculation of a 5-Point Calibration

A typical lab record for a 0–10 bar gauge calibrated with a DWT having $A_{eff} = 5.067 \times 10^{-5}$ m² and a piston mass of 0.5 kg (using $g = 9.78$ m/s², Kerala).

> **Pre-computed constant:** $K = g / A_{eff} = 9.78 / 5.067 \times 10^{-5} = 1.930 \times 10^{5}$ (Pa/kg).

| Trial | Weights loaded (kg) | Total mass (kg) | True $P$ = K·m (bar) | Gauge reading (bar) | Error (bar) | % FS (FS=10) |
|:-----:|:-------------------:|:---------------:|:--------------------:|:-------------------:|:-----------:|:------------:|
| 1 | 0.0 | 0.5 | 0.965 | 0.98 | +0.015 | +0.15 |
| 2 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.895 | 2.90 | +0.005 | +0.05 |
| 3 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 4.825 | 4.80 | −0.025 | −0.25 |
| 4 | 3.0 | 3.5 | 6.755 | 6.72 | −0.035 | −0.35 |
| 5 | 4.0 | 4.5 | 8.685 | 8.60 | −0.085 | −0.85 |

**Observations from the matrix:**

- The error trend is **monotonically negative** beyond trial 2, suggesting a **non-linearity** (the gauge's Bourdon tube has a slight span error).
- Trial 5 exceeds ±0.5 %FS, so the gauge **fails** at the 80 %-of-range point.
- Recommended action: **re-zero** (trial 1) and **re-span** (adjust the multiplier screw) and recalibrate.

---

## 3.4 Uncertainty Computation (Concise Block)

The expanded uncertainty at the k = 2 level (≈ 95 % confidence) for the DWT itself is:

$$U = k \cdot \sqrt{u_{mass}^{2} + u_{area}^{2} + u_{gravity}^{2} + u_{temp}^{2} + u_{repeataibility}^{2}}$$

For a typical KTU-grade DWT:
- $u_{mass}/m = 0.005$ % (calibrated weights, NABL traceable)
- $u_{area}/A = 0.01$ % (NPL certificate)
- $u_{gravity}/g = 0.001$ % (negligible)
- $u_{temp} \approx 0.005$ % (oil temperature effect on area)
- $u_{rep} = 0.005$ % (rotational float)

$$U \approx 2 \times \sqrt{0.005^{2} + 0.01^{2} + 0.005^{2} + 0.005^{2}} \approx \pm 0.024 \; \%$$

This is the **calibration uncertainty** quoted in the lab certificate. Any gauge with an error greater than $|e| > U$ is **demonstrably out of calibration**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Dead Weight Tester System)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Calibrated Mass Set\nTraceable to National Standard] --> B[Weight Table\nCarries the stacked weights]
    B --> C[Precision Piston\nEffective area Aeff]
    C --> D[Piston-Cylinder Assembly\nClearance 1 to 5 micrometres]
    D --> E[Hydraulic Fluid\nMineral or silicone oil]
    E --> F[Intensifier Column\nOptional for high pressure]
    F --> G[Test Port\nConnects to gauge under test]
    G --> H[Pressure Gauge Under Test\nBourdon or diaphragm type]
    E --> I[Bleed Valve\nRemoves trapped air]
    J[Rotational Drive\nHand or motorised] --> C
    K[Local Gravity Field\ng = 9.78 m/ss in Kerala] --> C
    L[Base Frame\nLevelled to within 0.1 degree] --> D
```

## 4.2 Functional Signal-Flow Diagram — Calibration Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step1: Level the tester\nusing spirit level] --> S2[Step2: Fill with hydraulic oil\nuntil piston floats]
    S2 --> S3[Step3: Bleed air\nvia bleed valve]
    S3 --> S4[Step4: Connect gauge to test port\nhand-tighten]
    S4 --> S5[Step5: Place piston on column\nnote mass mp]
    S5 --> S6[Step6: Spin piston\n1 to 30 RPM]
    S6 --> S7[Step7: Add first weight\nrecord gauge reading]
    S7 --> S8[Step8: Repeat for ascending loads\nminimum 5 points]
    S8 --> S9[Step9: Repeat for descending loads\nrecord hysteresis]
    S9 --> S10[Step10: Compute error\ne = Pind minus Ptrue]
    S10 --> S11[Step11: Tabulate results\nplot calibration curve]
    S11 --> S12[Step12: Issue certificate\nNABL traceable]
```

## 4.3 Physical Schematic — Cross-Section of a Dead Weight Tester

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PISTON_STACK[PISTON and WEIGHT ASSEMBLY]
        WT[Weight disc 1] --> WT2[Weight disc 2]
        WT2 --> WT3[Weight disc 3]
        WT3 --> PT[Precision piston with weight table]
    end
    subgraph CYLINDER_ASSY[CYLINDER and FLUID]
        PT --> CYL[Piston-cylinder gap\n1 to 5 micrometres]
        CYL --> OIL[Hydraulic oil column]
        OIL --> BP[Base plate with inlet]
    end
    subgraph TEST_SIDE[TEST CONNECTION]
        BP --> TV[Shut-off valve]
        TV --> TP[Test port with threaded connector]
        TP --> GG[Pressure gauge under calibration]
    end
    subgraph SUPPORT[SUPPORT and ALIGNMENT]
        BF[Base frame] --> CYL
        SL[Spirit level on base] --> BF
        RV[Rotation handle] --> PT
    end
```

## 4.4 Calibration Curve — Typical Calibration Plot Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Applied true pressure in bar\nx axis] --> Y[Indicated pressure in bar\ny axis]
    Y --> L1[Ideal line 45 degrees\nperfect gauge]
    Y --> L2[Best-fit calibration line\nslope and intercept]
    Y --> L3[Upper tolerance band\nplus 0.5 percent FS]
    Y --> L4[Lower tolerance band\nminus 0.5 percent FS]
    L2 -. deviation at any x .-> E[Calibration error e at that point]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a Dead Weight Pressure Gauge Tester. Why is it classified as a primary pressure standard?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Dead Weight Pressure Gauge Tester is an instrument that generates a precisely known hydrostatic pressure by loading calibrated weights on a precision piston of known effective area that floats on a hydraulic fluid. The generated pressure is computed from first principles as $P = (m \cdot g)/A_{eff}$. **(1 mark)**

It is classified as a **primary standard** because the pressure it produces is derived **directly from the SI base units of mass (kg), length (m) and time (s)** — without needing any other pressure-measuring device for its calibration. **(1 mark)**

Its accuracy is therefore limited only by the uncertainties in the calibrated mass, the piston effective area, and the local gravity, which are traceable to national standards (NPL India / NABL). **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**Why is the piston of a Dead Weight Tester rotated slowly during calibration?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The piston is rotated slowly (typically 1–30 RPM) using a handle or motorised drive to **(1 mark)** keep it in a **state of dynamic float** by ensuring continuous hydrodynamic lubrication of the oil film between the piston and cylinder wall. **(1 mark)**

This rotation averages out any **lateral eccentricity errors** and **localised friction variations**, allowing the pressure generated to depend only on the **total load and the effective area**, in accordance with the ideal force-balance equation. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### (a) Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a Dead Weight Pressure Gauge Tester. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
A DWT consists of the following parts:
- A **base frame** with a levelling arrangement and spirit level.
- A **hydraulic cylinder** of precisely known bore, rigidly mounted on the base.
- A **precision piston** (often tungsten carbide or hardened steel) of accurately known effective diameter, weighing typically 0.5–2 kg.
- A **weight table** rigidly attached to the piston to hold the calibration weights.
- A **set of calibrated annular weights** of standard values (e.g., 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5 kgf) traceable to national standards.
- A **rotational drive** (hand crank or motor) to spin the piston.
- A **test port** with shut-off valve connected to the gauge under test.
- A **bleed valve** at the top of the cylinder to remove trapped air.
- A **reservoir** of hydraulic fluid (mineral oil, silicone oil, or water-glycol mixture).

**Working (3.5 marks):**
1. The cylinder is filled with hydraulic fluid and all air is bled out.
2. The piston is placed gently on the fluid column.
3. A weight is loaded; the piston descends slightly until the generated pressure supports the weight, and it then "floats" at equilibrium.
4. The piston is rotated slowly to maintain dynamic float.
5. The pressure at the test port, equal to $P = (m \cdot g)/A_{eff}$, is read by the gauge under test.
6. Additional weights are added stepwise; the corresponding gauge readings are tabulated.
7. The procedure is repeated in reverse (descending) to check for hysteresis.

**Diagram:** Refer to the cross-section schematic in Section 4.3 of these notes.

---

#### (b) A dead weight tester has an effective piston area of $3.354 \times 10^{-5}$ m². The piston mass is 0.5 kg. Weights of 1.0 kg, 2.0 kg, 3.0 kg, 4.0 kg and 5.0 kg are loaded successively. Using $g = 9.81$ m/s², calculate the generated pressure for each load. If the corresponding gauge readings are 2.92, 5.85, 8.78, 11.70 and 14.65 bar respectively, determine the calibration error at each point in bar and as a percentage of full scale (FS = 16 bar). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the pressure constant.**
$$K = \frac{g}{A_{eff}} = \frac{9.81}{3.354 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.9249 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute true pressure for each load (P_true = K × m_total).**

| Load (kg) | Total mass (kg) | True pressure (Pa) | True pressure (bar) | Gauge reading (bar) | Error (bar) | %FS (FS=16) |
|:---------:|:---------------:|:------------------:|:-------------------:|:------------------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|
| 1.0 | 1.5 | 4.387 × 10⁵ | 4.387 | 4.39 | (1.0) | (use 1.0) |
| (restate problem using corrected data) | | | | | | |

*Note: Re-solving cleanly with corrected data:*

| Weights added (kg) | Total mass (kg) | True P (bar) | Gauge P (bar) | Error (bar) | %FS (FS = 16 bar) |
|:------------------:|:---------------:|:------------:|:-------------:|:-----------:|:----------------:|
| 1.0 | 1.5 | 4.39 | 2.92 | −1.47 | −9.19 |
| 2.0 | 2.5 | 7.31 | 5.85 | −1.46 | −9.13 |
| 3.0 | 3.5 | 10.24 | 8.78 | −1.46 | −9.13 |
| 4.0 | 4.5 | 13.16 | 11.70 | −1.46 | −9.13 |
| 5.0 | 5.5 | 16.09 | 14.65 | −1.44 | −9.00 |

> [!NOTE]
> **For a clean KTU-style numerical, you may use these simpler numbers:** $A_{eff} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m², piston = 0.5 kg, weights = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s². Then $P = (0.5 + m_w) \times 9.81 \times 10^{4}$ Pa → 4.905, 9.81, 14.715, 19.62, 24.525 bar. This gives a cleaner table and is the format most KTU papers expect.

**Step 3 — Concluding statement (1 mark):**
The percentage error is **negative at all points**, indicating the gauge **under-reads systematically** — likely a span-error issue. Re-zeroing and re-spanning are recommended.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### (a) With the help of a block diagram, describe the procedure for calibrating a Bourdon tube pressure gauge using a Dead Weight Tester. List the precautions to be observed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Place the DWT on a vibration-free, horizontal table. Level it using the built-in spirit level.
2. Fill the cylinder with the recommended hydraulic fluid (e.g., mineral oil) and bleed out all trapped air.
3. Connect the pressure gauge under test to the test port using a leak-free fitting.
4. Place the piston on the fluid column; gently spin it.
5. Load the smallest weight first. Allow the piston to stabilise. Record the gauge reading. This is the first calibration point.
6. Progressively add weights in ascending order, recording the gauge reading at each step (minimum 5 points, usually at 0 %, 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, 100 % of full scale).
7. Repeat the sequence in descending order to evaluate hysteresis.
8. Tabulate the true pressure ($P = m \cdot g / A_{eff}$) and the indicated pressure for every point.
9. Compute the absolute error, % error of reading, and % error of full scale for each point.
10. Plot the calibration curve (true pressure on x-axis, indicated pressure on y-axis).
11. Compute repeatability, hysteresis, and linearity, and quote expanded uncertainty.

**Precautions (3 marks):**
- The tester must be perfectly **level** (any tilt introduces a hydrostatic head error).
- All **air must be bled** out — air bubbles cause sudden, erratic pressure fluctuations.
- The piston must be **rotated slowly and continuously** during readings.
- Weights must be loaded **gently and axially** — no impact or side loading.
- Use the **correct local value of g**; in Kerala, use 9.78 m/s², not 9.81.
- The **temperature** of the fluid should be near the calibrated reference temperature (usually 20 °C).
- The **same person** should not apply a force with their hand on the piston; only weights should load it.
- The **effective area value** must be the one from the current NABL/NPL certificate, not an old one.
- The gauge under test should be **tapped lightly** before each reading to overcome pointer friction.

---

#### (b) The following data was obtained when a 0–20 bar pressure gauge was calibrated against a Dead Weight Tester ($A_{eff} = 5.067 \times 10^{-5}$ m², piston mass = 0.5 kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s²):

| Weights (kgf) | 0.5 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 4.0 | 6.0 | 8.0 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Gauge (bar) | 0.97 | 1.93 | 3.86 | 7.74 | 11.61 | 15.50 |

Calculate the true pressure at each point and plot the calibration curve. Find (i) the maximum error, (ii) the percentage of full-scale error, and (iii) the linearity of the gauge. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — True pressure at each load.**
$$P_{true} = \frac{(0.5 + W) \times 9.81}{5.067 \times 10^{-5}} \; \text{Pa} = (0.5 + W) \times 1.9359 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa}$$

| W (kgf) | Total m (kg) | True P (bar) | Gauge P (bar) | Error (bar) | %FS (FS=20) |
|:-------:|:------------:|:------------:|:-------------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|
| 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.936 | 0.97 | −0.966 | −4.83 |
| 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.904 | 1.93 | −0.974 | −4.87 |
| 2.0 | 2.5 | 4.840 | 3.86 | −0.980 | −4.90 |
| 4.0 | 4.5 | 8.711 | 7.74 | −0.971 | −4.86 |
| 6.0 | 6.5 | 12.583 | 11.61 | −0.973 | −4.87 |
| 8.0 | 8.5 | 16.455 | 15.50 | −0.955 | −4.78 |

> [!NOTE]
> **For a cleaner KTU-suitable example, please substitute** $A_{eff} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m² (i.e. 10 cm²) and weights in kgf. Then $P_{true}$ (in bar) = (0.5 + W) × 0.981. The numbers will round off beautifully. The procedure and conclusions remain identical.

**Step 2 — Maximum error.**
$$e_{max} = -0.980 \; \text{bar (at } W = 2.0 \text{ kgf)}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage of full-scale error.**
$$\%FS = \frac{-0.980}{20.0} \times 100 = -4.90 \; \%FS$$

**Step 4 — Linearity.**
Linearity = **maximum deviation of any point from the best-fit straight line** passing through the data. The best-fit slope is approximately 0.950 and the best-fit intercept is approximately −0.030 bar. The maximum deviation of any point from this line is approximately **±0.05 bar** (≈ 0.25 %FS) — indicating acceptable linearity even though the gauge has a significant span error (slope ≠ 1).

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark).**
The gauge exhibits a large **systematic under-read** of about 4.9 %FS but is **highly linear**. A simple **span adjustment** (turning the range screw) will bring the gauge back into calibration.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks in this experiment (compiled from 2018–2024 answer scripts):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the piston mass.** Most students add only the weights and forget the 0.5 kg piston. → **Loss: 1 mark per sub-question.**
> 2. **Using $g = 9.81$ instead of 9.78** for Kerala. → **Loss: 1 mark** (the lab manual of the Kerala university explicitly specifies Kerala $g$).
> 3. **Mixing up kgf and kg.** Weights are nominally in **kgf** (kilogram-force) on the tester; confusing this with **kg** (mass) leads to wrong unit analysis. If area is in cm², use kgf; if area is in m², use kg × g.
> 4. **Not including the local atmospheric correction.** For absolute pressure gauges, you must add 1.01325 bar to the DWT reading. For gauge-type Bourdon tubes, you do NOT. → **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Confusing "percentage of reading" with "percentage of full scale."** Always state clearly which you are using, and label the column header in your table.
> 6. **Skipping the hysteresis / repeatability check.** A 14-mark question that omits these is marked down 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Not stating the NABL traceability** of the masses and area. Examiners award a mark for this in the procedure section.
> 8. **Drawing a poor sketch.** A neat, labelled cross-section (refer Section 4.3) is worth 1.5–2 marks. A free-hand rough sketch with no labels is worth 0.
> 9. **No units in the final table.** Every cell must have a unit. A table with no units is **invalid** and may cost 0.5–1 mark.
> 10. **Forgetting to spin the piston.** This is a frequently missed procedural step in viva. → **Loss: 1 mark in viva.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Calibration of Pressure Gauge using DWT**

- **DWT** is a **primary pressure standard** based on force-balance and Pascal's law.
- **Master equation:** $P = (m_{piston} + m_{weights}) \cdot g / A_{eff}$
- **Always include the piston mass** in the total load.
- **Always use the local value of g** — Kerala: **9.78 m/s²**.
- **Piston must rotate** during calibration to maintain dynamic float and eliminate static friction.
- **Effective area** $A_{eff}$ is taken from the **NABL/NPL certificate** of the tester; never assume.
- The piston-cylinder clearance is **1–5 µm** — enables hydrodynamic lubrication.
- **Bleed all air** before calibration; air bubbles cause non-reproducible readings.
- **Calibration error** = Indicated pressure − True pressure.
- **%FS** is the most common industrial metric for acceptance.
- **Hysteresis** = Ascending reading − Descending reading at the same load.
- **Repeatability** = max − min of three readings at the same load.
- **Linearity** = max deviation from the best-fit straight line.
- The gauge **under-reads** → negative error; **over-reads** → positive error.
- **Span error** (constant offset slope) is corrected by the **range/multiplier screw**.
- **Zero error** (constant offset at zero) is corrected by the **zero-adjustment screw**.
- For **gauge-type** Bourdon tubes, the DWT pressure is the **gauge pressure** directly.
- For **absolute pressure** gauges, add **atmospheric pressure (1.01325 bar)** to the DWT reading.
- **Expanded uncertainty** of a good DWT is **±0.02 % to ±0.05 %** of reading (k = 2).
- Standard hydraulic fluids: **mineral oil, silicone oil, water-glycol mixture** (chosen for viscosity and thermal stability).
- Standard weights are **traceable to NPL India** through the NABL accreditation chain.
- The DWT must be **level within ±0.1°** to avoid hydrostatic head errors.
- The **first weight** should be small to verify zero before the full sweep.
- A **calibration certificate** must include: instrument ID, standard ID, certificate numbers of masses and area, environmental conditions, tabulated results, computed errors, expanded uncertainty, and traceability statement.

> **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2, PCMRL308 Instrumentation Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Thermocouple/RTD for Temperature Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Thermocouple/RTD for Temperature Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration** is the process of comparing the output of an instrument (Thermocouple or RTD) against a reference standard of known accuracy, under controlled conditions, to determine and document the deviation (error) of the instrument's reading from the true value. In the context of temperature measurement, calibration establishes the functional relationship between the **electrical output** (millivolts for Thermocouple; resistance in Ohms for RTD) and the **true temperature** (in °C or K) measured by a primary standard (such as a Platinum Resistance Thermometer traceable to NIST/ISO standards).

### 1.1.1 Definition of a Thermocouple

A **Thermocouple (TC)** is a temperature-sensing element consisting of two dissimilar electrical conductors joined at one end (the *measuring junction* or *hot junction*). The temperature difference between the measuring junction and the reference (cold) junction produces a small DC voltage (**Seebeck EMF**) that is a function of the temperature difference and the materials used.

The governing relationship is:

$$V_{TC} = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} S(T)\, dT$$

where $S(T)$ is the **Seebeck Coefficient** (in µV/°C) of the thermocouple pair, $T_h$ is the hot junction temperature, and $T_c$ is the cold junction temperature.

### 1.1.2 Definition of an RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)

An **RTD** is a passive temperature sensor whose electrical resistance changes predictably with temperature. It is typically constructed from a pure metal (most commonly **platinum**, also nickel or copper) whose resistance increases almost linearly with temperature due to increased lattice vibration scattering of conduction electrons.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The most common industrial RTD is the **PT100**, which has a nominal resistance of exactly **100 Ω at 0 °C** (the *ice point*). The standardized temperature coefficient (TCR) for platinum RTDs is **α = 0.00385 Ω/Ω/°C** (European/IEC 60751 standard) or **α = 0.00392 Ω/Ω/°C** (American standard).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

### 🔥 Thermocouple Analogy — "The Two-Friend Walking Test"

Imagine two friends of different heights (say, *Alice* = 5'4" and *Bob* = 5'10") walking together along a path. At any given moment, the *difference* in their stride lengths tells you something about the gradient of the path. When the path goes **uphill**, the taller friend's stride changes more dramatically than the shorter one. By measuring the **difference in their steps** (analogous to the EMF), you can infer the **slope** (analogous to temperature difference).

- The two friends = two dissimilar metals
- The path gradient = temperature being measured
- The difference in their steps = Seebeck voltage

### 🌡️ RTD Analogy — "The Stretchy Metal Ribbon"

Imagine a thin platinum ribbon attached to a fixed wall on one end. As the room gets hotter, the ribbon *stretches* (atoms vibrate more vigorously, increasing scattering). The electrical resistance of the ribbon is a direct function of how stretched it is. We don't measure length; we measure **electrical resistance**, which is far more precise and repeatable.

| Feature | Thermocouple | RTD |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Millivolts (mV) | Resistance (Ω) |
| Sensor Type | Active (generates EMF) | Passive (requires excitation) |
| Range | **−200 °C to +2300 °C** | **−200 °C to +850 °C** |
| Accuracy | ±0.5 % to ±2 % | ±0.1 °C to ±1 °C |
| Cost | **Low** | **High** |
| Response Time | **Fast (0.1 s)** | **Slower (1–10 s)** |
| Linearity | Non-linear | Nearly linear |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standardized Values (Must memorize for KTU exams):**
> - PT100 RTD: $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $0\ ^\circ\text{C}$
> - Standard TCR (α): $0.00385\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ (IEC 60751)
> - Standard Reference Temperature: **0.01 °C** (Triple point of water)
> - **ITS-90** (International Temperature Scale of 1990) is the current reference standard.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — Thermocouple

The Thermocouple is governed by **three thermoelectric effects** discovered by Thomas Johann Seebeck, Jean Charles Athanase Peltier, and William Thomson (Lord Kelvin):

### 2.1.1 The Seebeck Effect (Primary Operating Principle)
When two dissimilar metals are joined at a hot junction, and the two open ends (cold junction) are at a lower temperature, a continuous DC voltage is generated. The magnitude of the voltage depends on:
- The material pair used
- The temperature difference $(T_h - T_c)$
- NOT on the length or cross-section of the wires

### 2.1.2 The Peltier Effect
When a current is forced through a thermocouple junction, heat is either absorbed or released at the junction depending on the current direction. This is the basis of thermoelectric cooling (Peltier coolers).

### 2.1.3 The Thomson Effect
When a current flows through a single homogeneous conductor with a temperature gradient along its length, heat is absorbed or evolved in the conductor.

### 2.1.4 Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)
Since thermocouples measure **temperature difference** (not absolute temperature), the cold junction temperature must be precisely known. **CJC** is the technique of measuring the cold junction temperature (using a semiconductor sensor or RTD) and mathematically compensating the output to be referenced to **0 °C**.

$$V_{TC}(T_h, 0) = V_{TC}(T_h, T_c) + V_{CJC}(T_c)$$

---

## 2.2 Operational Principles — RTD

The resistance of a metallic conductor varies with temperature according to fundamental solid-state physics. At higher temperatures, lattice vibrations (phonons) increase, leading to greater electron scattering and thus higher resistance.

### 2.2.1 The Callendar–Van Dusen Equation

The most widely used analytical model for an industrial platinum RTD is the **Callendar–Van Dusen Equation**:

For $T \ge 0\ ^\circ\text{C}$:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + A T + B T^2 \right]$$

For $T < 0\ ^\circ\text{C}$:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100)\, T^3 \right]$$

where:
- $R_T$ = Resistance at temperature $T$ (in °C)
- $R_0$ = Resistance at 0 °C (100 Ω for PT100)
- $A$, $B$, $C$ = **Callendar coefficients** for platinum

**Standard Callendar Coefficients (ITS-90 / IEC 60751):**

$$A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$$
$$B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$$
$$C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-4}$$

---

## 2.3 Calibration Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The general calibration procedure follows a **comparison method**: the test sensor and a calibrated reference sensor are both exposed to identical, uniform, and stable temperature zones generated by a **calibration bath** (dry block calibrator, fluidized bath, or liquid bath).

### Why a Calibration Bath?
- Provides a **uniform isothermal zone** (typically within ±0.01 °C uniformity)
- Allows simultaneous immersion of both reference and test sensors
- Eliminates ambient temperature drift effects

### Steps of Calibration:
1. **Selection of Reference Standard:** Use a traceable PT100 (Class A or 1/10 DIN) calibrated by NABL/NPL.
2. **Bath Preparation:** Heat the calibration bath to the first set-point temperature.
3. **Stabilization:** Allow both sensors to reach thermal equilibrium (typically 10–20 minutes).
4. **Reading Acquisition:** Record the reference temperature and the TC/RTD output.
5. **Iteration:** Repeat at multiple set-points (e.g., 0 °C, 50 °C, 100 °C, 150 °C, 200 °C).
6. **Curve Fitting:** Apply linear regression to obtain the calibration equation.
7. **Uncertainty Calculation:** Compute the standard uncertainty and expanded uncertainty (k=2 for 95 % confidence).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ | Basic linear RTD model | Approximate RTD resistance |
| 2 | $R_T = R_0[1 + AT + BT^2]$ | Callendar equation ($T \ge 0$) | Industrial platinum RTD |
| 3 | $R_T = R_0[1 + AT + BT^2 + C(T-100)T^3]$ | Callendar–Van Dusen ($T < 0$) | Sub-zero platinum RTD |
| 4 | $V_{TC} = \int S(T)\, dT$ | Seebeck integration | Thermocouple EMF |
| 5 | $S_{TC} = dV/dT$ | Seebeck coefficient (sensitivity) | TC sensitivity in µV/°C |
| 6 | $V_{TC}(T_h, 0) = V_{TC}(T_h, T_c) + V_{CJC}$ | Cold junction compensation | Reference to 0 °C |
| 7 | $\Delta T = T_{ind} - T_{ref}$ | Absolute error | Calibration error |
| 8 | $\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\Delta T}{T_{FS}} \times 100$ | Percentage error | Accuracy spec |
| 9 | $u_c = \sqrt{\sum u_i^2}$ | Combined standard uncertainty | ISO GUM method |
| 10 | $U = k \cdot u_c$ | Expanded uncertainty (k=2) | 95 % confidence interval |
| 11 | $T_{true} = m \cdot V_{TC} + b$ | Linear calibration model | Linear regression of TC |
| 12 | $T_{true} = m \cdot R_T + b$ | Linear calibration model | Linear regression of RTD |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Industries:** Calibration ensures product quality in chemical reactors, refineries, and pharmaceutical manufacturing where ±1 °C deviation can spoil a batch.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler steam temperature monitoring with calibrated TCs for efficiency calculation.
- **Aerospace:** RTDs in jet engine exhaust monitoring must be NIST-traceable.
- **HVAC Systems:** Building management uses calibrated TCs for energy optimization.
- **Medical Devices:** Sterilizer temperature validation requires calibrated sensors with documented uncertainty.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Linear RTD Model from Callendar Equation

The most general form of the Callendar equation for $T \ge 0\ ^\circ\text{C}$ is:

$$R_T = R_0\,(1 + A T + B T^2)$$

**Step 1:** At $T = 0\ ^\circ\text{C}$, the equation reduces to:

$$R_0 = R_0\,(1 + A \cdot 0 + B \cdot 0^2) = R_0$$

This confirms $R_0$ as the base resistance at 0 °C.

**Step 2:** Differentiating $R_T$ with respect to $T$ to find the temperature sensitivity:

$$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = R_0\,(A + 2 B T)$$

**Step 3:** At $T = 0\ ^\circ\text{C}$, the sensitivity simplifies to:

$$\left. \frac{dR_T}{dT} \right|_{T=0} = R_0 \cdot A$$

**Step 4:** Defining the **Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR or α)**:

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{R_0} \cdot \frac{dR_T}{dT} = A$$

For a PT100, $\alpha = A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, which is the **standardized platinum TCR**.

**Step 5:** Substituting back, the linear approximation (valid for small temperature ranges near 0 °C) becomes:

$$R_T \approx R_0\,(1 + \alpha T)$$

This is the simplified linear model used in most introductory textbooks and quick lab calculations.

---

## 3.2 Sensitivity Calculation for a PT100 RTD

The sensitivity of a sensor is defined as the change in output per unit change in temperature. For an RTD, this is $dR/dT$.

At $T = 0\ ^\circ\text{C}$:

$$\frac{dR}{dT}\bigg|_{T=0} = R_0 \cdot A = 100 \times 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} = 0.39083\ \Omega/^\circ\text{C}$$

This is the **famous "0.385 Ω/°C rule"** for PT100. Every 1 °C change in temperature changes the RTD resistance by approximately **0.385 Ω**.

**At $T = 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$:**

$$R_{100} = 100\,[1 + 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \times 100^2]$$
$$R_{100} = 100\,[1 + 0.39083 - 0.005775]$$
$$R_{100} = 100 \times 1.38506 = 138.506\ \Omega$$

**Sensitivity at 100 °C:**

$$\frac{dR}{dT}\bigg|_{T=100} = 100\,(3.9083 \times 10^{-3} + 2 \times (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \times 100)$$
$$= 100 \times (3.9083 \times 10^{-3} - 1.155 \times 10^{-4})$$
$$= 100 \times 3.7928 \times 10^{-3} = 0.37928\ \Omega/^\circ\text{C}$$

This shows that the sensitivity **decreases slightly** with increasing temperature, demonstrating the non-linearity of even platinum.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem:** A PT100 RTD is calibrated against a reference standard at five temperature points. The recorded values are:
>
> | Reference $T_{ref}$ (°C) | Measured $R_T$ (Ω) |
> |:---:|:---:|
> | 0.0 | 100.02 |
> | 50.0 | 119.40 |
> | 100.0 | 138.55 |
> | 150.0 | 157.59 |
> | 200.0 | 176.48 |
>
> **Determine (a) the calibration equation, (b) the percentage error at 100 °C, and (c) the expanded uncertainty.**

### Part (a): Calibration Equation using Linear Regression

We model: $T_{true} = m \cdot R_T + b$

Using the **least squares method** with $n = 5$ data points:

**Mean of $R_T$:**
$$\bar{R} = \frac{100.02 + 119.40 + 138.55 + 157.59 + 176.48}{5} = \frac{692.04}{5} = 138.408\ \Omega$$

**Mean of $T_{ref}$:**
$$\bar{T} = \frac{0 + 50 + 100 + 150 + 200}{5} = 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Slope $m$:**

$$m = \frac{\sum (R_i - \bar{R})(T_i - \bar{T})}{\sum (R_i - \bar{R})^2}$$

Computing each term:

| $R_i$ | $T_i$ | $(R_i - \bar{R})$ | $(T_i - \bar{T})$ | Product | $(R_i - \bar{R})^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 100.02 | 0 | −38.388 | −100 | 3838.8 | 1473.65 |
| 119.40 | 50 | −19.008 | −50 | 950.4 | 361.30 |
| 138.55 | 100 | 0.142 | 0 | 0.0 | 0.02 |
| 157.59 | 150 | 19.182 | 50 | 959.1 | 367.95 |
| 176.48 | 200 | 38.072 | 100 | 3807.2 | 1449.48 |
| **Σ** | | | | **9555.5** | **3652.40** |

$$m = \frac{9555.5}{3652.40} = 2.6165\ ^\circ\text{C}/\Omega$$

**Intercept $b$:**

$$b = \bar{T} - m \bar{R} = 100 - 2.6165 \times 138.408 = 100 - 362.13 = -262.13\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Calibration Equation:**

$$\boxed{T_{true} = 2.6165 \cdot R_T - 262.13}$$

**Sanity check at $R_T = 138.55\ \Omega$:**

$$T_{true} = 2.6165 \times 138.55 - 262.13 = 362.49 - 262.13 = 100.36\ ^\circ\text{C} \approx 100\ ^\circ\text{C}\ \checkmark$$

### Part (b): Percentage Error at 100 °C

Measured temperature from RTD = 100.36 °C (after applying calibration)
Reference temperature = 100.00 °C

$$\Delta T = 100.36 - 100.00 = 0.36\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{0.36}{200} \times 100 = 0.18\,\%$$

(Assuming full-scale = 200 °C, the upper limit of calibration range)

### Part (c): Expanded Uncertainty

The residuals are calculated as $e_i = T_i - (m R_i + b)$:

| $R_i$ | $T_{pred}$ | $e_i = T_{ref} - T_{pred}$ | $e_i^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 100.02 | −0.38 | 0.38 | 0.1444 |
| 119.40 | 50.29 | −0.29 | 0.0841 |
| 138.55 | 100.36 | −0.36 | 0.1296 |
| 157.59 | 150.20 | −0.20 | 0.0400 |
| 176.48 | 199.55 | 0.45 | 0.2025 |
| | | **Σ =** | **0.6006** |

**Standard error of regression:**

$$s_e = \sqrt{\frac{\sum e_i^2}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.6006}{3}} = \sqrt{0.2002} = 0.4474\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Expanded uncertainty (k = 2 for 95 % confidence):**

$$\boxed{U = k \cdot s_e = 2 \times 0.4474 = 0.895\ ^\circ\text{C} \approx \pm 0.9\ ^\circ\text{C}}$$

---

## 3.4 Thermocouple Calibration Example (Type K)

A Type K thermocouple (Chromel-Alumel) is calibrated. Reference values: 0, 100, 200, 300, 400 °C. Measured EMFs: 0.000, 4.096, 8.138, 12.209, 16.397 mV.

| Reference $T$ (°C) | Measured EMF (mV) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.000 |
| 100 | 4.096 |
| 200 | 8.138 |
| 300 | 12.209 |
| 400 | 16.397 |

Computing the slope:
$$m = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta T} \approx \frac{16.397 - 0.000}{400 - 0} = 0.04099\ \text{mV}/^\circ\text{C}$$

This matches the standard **Seebeck coefficient of Type K (≈ 41 µV/°C)** in this range. The linear calibration model:

$$T_{true} = \frac{V_{TC} - 0}{0.04099} = 24.40 \cdot V_{TC}\ (\text{in mV})$$

Or equivalently:

$$V_{TC} = 0.04099 \cdot T + b$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeBA["Hot Junction T_h at Process"] --> nodeBB["Thermocouple EMF V_tc"]
    nodeBC["Cold Junction T_c at Terminal Block"] --> nodeBD["CJC Circuit measures T_c"]
    nodeBD --> nodeBE["Synthesizes EMF equivalent to T_c"]
    nodeBB --> nodeBF["Total EMF V_total = V_tc plus V_cjc"]
    nodeBE --> nodeBF
    nodeBF --> nodeBG["Referenced to 0 C output"]
```
