<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

### **Q1. Define Seebeck Effect. List the three laws of thermocouples.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Seebeck Effect** is the generation of an electromotive force (EMF) in a closed circuit consisting of two dissimilar metals when their junctions are maintained at different temperatures. The magnitude of the EMF depends on the materials and the temperature difference, but **not** on the length or cross-sectional area of the conductors.

**Three Laws of Thermocouples (Tait's Laws):**

1. **Law of Homogeneous Circuit:** An EMF is generated only by a temperature gradient; a homogeneous wire in a uniform temperature field produces no net EMF, regardless of cross-section.
2. **Law of Intermediate Metals:** Inserting a third metal into the circuit does not affect the EMF as long as its two junctions are at the same temperature.
3. **Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** The EMF corresponding to temperatures $T_1$ and $T_3$ equals the sum of EMFs for $T_1 \to T_2$ and $T_2 \to T_3$ (where $T_1 < T_2 < T_3$).

---

### **Q2. What is Cold Junction Compensation? Why is it necessary in a thermocouple measurement system?**

**Model Answer:**

**Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)** is the technique of measuring the temperature at the cold (reference) junction of a thermocouple and mathematically adding the equivalent EMF so that the final output is referenced to **0 °C**.

**Necessity:**
- A thermocouple measures the **temperature difference** $(T_h - T_c)$, not absolute $T_h$.
- If $T_c$ drifts due to ambient temperature changes, the indicated $T_h$ will be wrong even if the hot junction temperature is stable.
- CJC ensures accurate absolute temperature indication regardless of the cold junction temperature.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — University ESE Module]**
> *Internal choice is mandatory. Answer ANY ONE full question from each slot.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the procedure for calibration of a Resistance Temperature Detector (PT100) using a temperature bath. List the apparatus and precautions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PT100 RTD was calibrated and the following data was obtained:

| Reference Temp (°C) | 0 | 50 | 100 | 150 | 200 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RTD Resistance (Ω) | 100.00 | 119.40 | 138.51 | 157.32 | 175.86 |

Determine the **slope**, **intercept**, **percentage error at 100 °C**, and the **expanded uncertainty** using a coverage factor of k = 2.

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Procedure & Block Diagram:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Temperature calibration bath (oil/water/salt bath or dry block calibrator)
2. Reference standard PRT (PT100, Class A or better, NABL-traceable)
3. Test PT100 RTD
4. Precision resistance measurement bridge or digital multimeter (4-wire)
5. Temperature controller with PID
6. Stirrer for bath uniformity
7. Ice flask (for 0 °C reference point) — optional
8. Connecting leads (4-wire for RTD to eliminate lead resistance error)

**Procedure:**
1. Switch on the calibration bath and set the controller to the first set-point temperature (e.g., 0 °C using ice bath or 50 °C).
2. Insert both the reference PRT and the test PT100 to the same immersion depth (at least 15 cm for liquid baths).
3. Ensure good thermal contact — use a small amount of heat-conducting paste if needed.
4. Wait for **thermal equilibrium** (typically 10–20 minutes; verify by stable reading on the reference).
5. Record the reference temperature $T_{ref}$ and the test RTD resistance $R_{test}$ simultaneously.
6. Repeat steps 1–5 for at least 5 set-points covering the desired calibration range (e.g., 0, 50, 100, 150, 200 °C).
7. Plot the calibration curve (Temperature vs Resistance).
8. Apply linear regression to obtain the calibration equation: $T = m R + b$.
9. Compute the residuals and expanded uncertainty.
10. Generate the calibration certificate with traceability information.

**Precautions:**
- Avoid contamination of the bath fluid.
- Ensure proper immersion depth to avoid stem conduction errors.
- Use **4-wire (Kelvin) connection** for RTD to eliminate lead resistance.
- Wait for true thermal equilibrium; do not record during transient phase.
- Maintain ambient temperature stability (avoid drafts on cold junctions).
- Do not handle sensors with bare hands when near ambient — use clean gloves.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the means.**

$$\bar{R} = \frac{100.00 + 119.40 + 138.51 + 157.32 + 175.86}{5} = \frac{691.09}{5} = 138.218\ \Omega$$

$$\bar{T} = \frac{0 + 50 + 100 + 150 + 200}{5} = 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2: Compute the slope.**

| $R_i$ | $T_i$ | $R_i - \bar{R}$ | $T_i - \bar{T}$ | $(R_i - \bar{R})(T_i - \bar{T})$ | $(R_i - \bar{R})^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 100.00 | 0 | −38.218 | −100 | 3821.8 | 1460.62 |
| 119.40 | 50 | −18.818 | −50 | 940.9 | 354.12 |
| 138.51 | 100 | 0.292 | 0 | 0.0 | 0.0853 |
| 157.32 | 150 | 19.102 | 50 | 955.1 | 364.89 |
| 175.86 | 200 | 37.642 | 100 | 3764.2 | 1416.86 |
| | | | **Σ** | **9482.0** | **3596.54** |

$$m = \frac{9482.0}{3596.54} = 2.6370\ ^\circ\text{C}/\Omega$$

**Step 3: Compute the intercept.**

$$b = \bar{T} - m \bar{R} = 100 - 2.6370 \times 138.218 = 100 - 364.48 = -264.48\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Calibration Equation:** $\boxed{T = 2.6370 \cdot R - 264.48}$

**Step 4: Compute % error at 100 °C.**

Predicted temperature at $R = 138.51$:

$$T_{pred} = 2.6370 \times 138.51 - 264.48 = 365.25 - 264.48 = 100.77\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\Delta T = 100.77 - 100.00 = 0.77\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{0.77}{200} \times 100 = 0.385\,\%$$

**Step 5: Compute residuals and uncertainty.**

| $R_i$ | $T_{pred}$ | $e_i = T_{ref} - T_{pred}$ | $e_i^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 100.00 | −0.70 | 0.70 | 0.4900 |
| 119.40 | 50.34 | −0.34 | 0.1156 |
| 138.51 | 100.77 | −0.77 | 0.5929 |
| 157.32 | 150.43 | −0.43 | 0.1849 |
| 175.86 | 199.30 | 0.70 | 0.4900 |
| | | **Σ** | **1.8734** |

Standard error of regression:

$$s_e = \sqrt{\frac{1.8734}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.8734}{3}} = \sqrt{0.6245} = 0.7903\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Expanded uncertainty (k = 2):**

$$\boxed{U = 2 \times 0.7903 = 1.58\ ^\circ\text{C}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Seebeck Effect** in detail. With a labelled diagram, describe the construction and working of a Thermocouple. Compare Type K, Type J, and Type T thermocouples (range, materials, sensitivity, applications).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Type K thermocouple is calibrated with the following readings. Compute the slope, intercept, and the temperature corresponding to a measured EMF of **10.250 mV**.

| Reference Temp (°C) | 0 | 100 | 200 | 300 | 400 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| EMF (mV) | 0.000 | 4.096 | 8.138 | 12.209 | 16.397 |

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Seebeck Effect, Construction, and Comparison:**

**Seebeck Effect — Detailed Explanation:**

When two dissimilar metallic conductors A and B are joined at one end to form a measuring (hot) junction, and the other two ends (cold junction) are maintained at a different temperature, an EMF is generated that drives a current through the closed circuit. The magnitude of the EMF depends on the temperature difference and the thermoelectric properties of the two materials.

The Seebeck EMF for a thermocouple pair is given by:

$$V = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} [S_B(T) - S_A(T)]\, dT$$

where $S_A$ and $S_B$ are the **absolute Seebeck coefficients** of metals A and B respectively.

**Construction:**
- **Hot (Measuring) Junction:** Formed by welding, brazing, or twisting the two dissimilar wires. Common welding methods include **capacitor discharge welding** and **laser welding**.
- **Thermocouple Wires:** Insulated with fiberglass, PTFE, or ceramic beading depending on the temperature.
- **Cold (Reference) Junction:** Located at the instrument terminals, where the thermocouple wires connect to the copper lead wires of the measuring instrument.
- **Sheath (for industrial probes):** A stainless steel or Inconel sheath protects the junction, often filled with MgO insulation for vibration resistance.

**Comparison Table — Type K, J, T:**

| Parameter | Type K | Type J | Type T |
|---|---|---|---|
| Materials (+) | Chromel (Ni-Cr) | Iron (Fe) | Copper (Cu) |
| Materials (−) | Alumel (Ni-Al) | Constantan (Cu-Ni) | Constantan (Cu-Ni) |
| Range (°C) | **−200 to +1260** | **0 to +760** | **−200 to +370** |
| Sensitivity (µV/°C) | **~41** | **~50** | **~40** |
| Atmosphere | Oxidizing | Reducing, vacuum | Moist, reducing |
| Application | Most general-purpose | Older furnaces | Cryogenics, food |
| Cost | Moderate | Low | Moderate |

**(b) Numerical Solution for Type K Calibration:**

**Step 1: Compute means.**

$$\bar{V} = \frac{0 + 4.096 + 8.138 + 12.209 + 16.397}{5} = \frac{40.840}{5} = 8.168\ \text{mV}$$

$$\bar{T} = 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2: Compute slope $m = dT/dV$:**

| $V_i$ | $T_i$ | $V_i - \bar{V}$ | $T_i - \bar{T}$ | $(V_i - \bar{V})(T_i - \bar{T})$ | $(V_i - \bar{V})^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0.000 | 0 | −8.168 | −100 | 816.8 | 66.72 |
| 4.096 | 50 | −4.072 | −50 | 203.6 | 16.58 |
| 8.138 | 100 | −0.030 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.0009 |
| 12.209 | 150 | 4.041 | 50 | 202.05 | 16.33 |
| 16.397 | 200 | 8.229 | 100 | 822.9 | 67.72 |
| | | | **Σ** | **2045.35** | **167.35** |

$$m = \frac{2045.35}{167.35} = 22.22\ ^\circ\text{C}/\text{mV}$$

$$b = \bar{T} - m \bar{V} = 100 - 22.22 \times 8.168 = 100 - 181.50 = -81.50\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Calibration Equation:** $\boxed{T = 22.22 \cdot V - 81.50}$

**Step 3: Temperature at V = 10.250 mV:**

$$T = 22.22 \times 10.250 - 81.50 = 227.76 - 81.50 = \boxed{146.26\ ^\circ\text{C}}$$

**Sanity check:** Type K's standard table gives EMF at 146 °C ≈ 6.13 mV cold-junction referenced. The reversed relationship gives $V/m \approx 1/24.4 \approx 0.041$ mV/°C, confirming consistency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Warnings:**
> 1. **Forgetting CJC:** When solving thermocouple problems, students often assume the cold junction is at 0 °C without stating the assumption. Always **explicitly mention** CJC status.
> 2. **Sign Convention Confusion:** The Seebeck voltage polarity depends on the material pairing. KTU examiners award partial credit for **correctly labeling which wire is positive (+) and which is negative (−)** in diagrams.
> 3. **Unit Errors in RTD Sensitivity:** The value **0.385 Ω/°C is for PT100 only.** For PT1000, it is 3.85 Ω/°C. Always state the sensor model.
> 4. **Uncertainty Calculation:** The expanded uncertainty uses **k = 2 for 95 % confidence.** Using k = 1 or 3 will lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Linear Regression Form:** State clearly whether $T = mR + b$ or $R = mT + b$ — examiners look for consistency throughout your solution.
> 6. **Forgetting to Convert mV to µV:** Type K EMF is typically in mV but the Seebeck coefficient is in µV/°C. Mixing units is the most common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist for Calibration of Thermocouple/RTD:**

- [ ] **Calibration** is comparison of test sensor against a traceable reference standard at multiple set-points.
- [ ] **Thermocouple** is an **active** sensor based on the **Seebeck Effect** (two dissimilar metals, EMF output in mV).
- [ ] **RTD** is a **passive** sensor (PT100 most common) with resistance output in Ω, based on the temperature dependence of metal resistance.
- [ ] **PT100:** $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at 0 °C; standard TCR $\alpha = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ (IEC 60751).
- [ ] **Callendar–Van Dusen Equation:** $R_T = R_0[1 + AT + BT^2]$ for $T \ge 0$ and an extra cubic term for $T < 0$.
- [ ] **Three Laws of Thermocouples:** Homogeneous Circuit, Intermediate Metals, Intermediate Temperatures.
- [ ] **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)** is essential for accurate thermocouple absolute temperature reading.
- [ ] **Type K** is the most common general-purpose TC; **Type J** is for reducing atmospheres; **Type T** is for cryogenics.
- [ ] **Sensitivity of PT100:** ~0.385 Ω/°C (this is a high-yield numerical for KTU).
- [ ] **Calibration Procedure:** Bath → Stabilize → Record → Repeat at 5+ set-points → Regression → Uncertainty.
- [ ] **Linear Regression** formula: $m = \Sigma[(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})] / \Sigma[(x_i - \bar{x})^2]$
- [ ] **Standard Error of Regression:** $s_e = \sqrt{\Sigma e_i^2 / (n-2)}$
- [ ] **Expanded Uncertainty:** $U = k \cdot s_e$ with **k = 2 for 95 % confidence**.
- [ ] **4-wire (Kelvin) connection** is required for accurate RTD measurement to eliminate lead resistance.
- [ ] **Reference standard** should be at least **4× more accurate** than the test sensor.
- [ ] **ITS-90** is the current international temperature scale used for traceability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Strain Gauge Pressure Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Strain Gauge Pressure Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Strain Gauge Pressure Cell** is a primary pressure transducer that converts an applied fluid pressure into a proportional electrical signal by exploiting the **piezoresistive effect** of metallic or semiconductor strain gauges bonded onto an elastic sensing element (typically a circular diaphragm or a cylindrical bellows).

> [!NOTE]
> **Calibration** is the experimental procedure of comparing the transducer output against a traceable reference standard (such as a **Dead Weight Tester**) over its full operating range, both in increasing and decreasing directions, to determine its accuracy, linearity, hysteresis, and repeatability characteristics.

The complete transducer assembly (mechanical element + strain gauges + Wheatstone bridge + housing) is referred to as the **Pressure Cell**, and it is the workhorse sensor used in industrial process control, hydraulic systems, aerospace, and biomedical instrumentation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a mechanical bathroom weighing scale. When you step on it, an internal **spring deflects** and a pointer moves across a dial. You trust the reading because the scale was originally tested against standard masses (1 kg, 5 kg, 50 kg). That comparison process IS calibration.

Now replace the spring with a thin metal disc (diaphragm), the pointer with a **Wheatstone bridge**, and the standard masses with the precisely machined piston-and-weight assembly of a **Dead Weight Tester**. The strain gauge pressure cell works on the exact same principle: pressure deflects a diaphragm, the deflection stretches/compresses bonded strain gauges, their resistance changes, and the bridge converts this tiny resistance change into a measurable voltage.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Gauge Factor (GF)** of metallic foil gauges ≈ **2.0 to 2.2**
- **Gauge Factor (GF)** of semiconductor gauges ≈ **100 to 200**
- **Poisson's ratio (ν)** for steel ≈ **0.30**
- **Young's Modulus (E)** for steel ≈ **2 × 10¹¹ N/m²**
- **Standard Excitation Voltage (V_in)** = **5 V DC** or **10 V DC**
- **Bridge Output** is typically in **mV/V** (millivolts per volt of excitation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The strain gauge pressure cell experiment is a **CO (Course Outcome) mapping** cornerstone. Students must demonstrate **CO3: Calibration of sensors** and **CO4: Error analysis** in the laboratory record. The experiment carries **15 marks** in the continuous evaluation (10 record + 5 viva) and is a frequent **ESE (End Semester Examination)** lab viva topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principle of Operation — Structured Logic Flow

The strain gauge pressure cell operates through a five-stage physical-to-electrical conversion chain:

1. **Mechanical Input Stage:** The applied fluid pressure **P** acts on the diaphragm surface, generating distributed mechanical stress.
2. **Mechanical-to-Strain Conversion:** The diaphragm deflects. The strain **ε** at the surface is related to pressure by the elasticity equations of a clamped circular plate.
3. **Strain-to-Resistance Conversion (Piezoresistive Effect):** The bonded strain gauges experience strain **ε**, causing a fractional change in resistance given by the **Gauge Factor (GF)**.
4. **Resistance-to-Voltage Conversion:** The strain gauges are wired in a **Wheatstone bridge**, which translates ΔR into a differential output voltage **V_o**.
5. **Signal Conditioning:** The mV-level output is amplified, linearized, and displayed as a calibrated pressure reading.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Configuration

- **Why a Wheatstone bridge?** A single gauge output is too small (μV level) and is severely affected by temperature. A bridge configuration **nulls common-mode signals**, provides **temperature compensation**, and amplifies the differential output.
- **Why four active gauges?** In a full-bridge configuration with two gauges in tension and two in compression, the output is multiplied by a factor of **(1 + ν)**, where ν is Poisson's ratio. This increases sensitivity by approximately 30% for steel.
- **Why a dead weight tester as standard?** It is a **primary standard** based directly on SI base units (Force = mass × g, Pressure = Force/Area). No intermediate calibration is needed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE and are commonly tested in numerical/model questions.

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gauge Factor | $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L} = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | Dimensionless | **GF ≈ 2.0** for constantan |
| Fractional Resistance Change | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Dimensionless | Piezoresistive relation |
| Diaphragm Strain (edge max.) | $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{3 P D^{2} (1 - \nu^{2})}{8 E t^{2}}$ | m/m | D = diameter, t = thickness |
| Quarter-Bridge Output | $V_{o} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}$ | Volts | One active arm |
| Full-Bridge Output (axial gauges) | $V_{o} = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{2}$ | Volts | Two gauges active |
| Full-Bridge Output (Poisson config.) | $V_{o} = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon (1 + \nu)}{4}$ | Volts | Two tension + two compression |
| Dead Weight Pressure | $P = \dfrac{m g}{A}$ | Pa or N/m² | Primary standard |
| Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{V_{o}}{P}$ | V/Pa | Slope of calibration curve |
| Linearity Error (%) | $L = \dfrac{\vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert_{max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | % | Best-fit straight line |
| Hysteresis Error (%) | $H = \dfrac{\vert V_{up} - V_{down} \vert_{max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | % | Loading vs unloading |
| Repeatability Error (%) | $R = \dfrac{\vert V_{i} - \bar{V} \vert_{max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | % | Standard deviation related |
| Combined Accuracy | $A = \sqrt{L^{2} + H^{2} + R^{2}}$ | % | RSS method |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft altimeters and hydraulic pressure monitors use strain gauge cells for reliability under vibration.
- **Process Industries:** SCADA and DCS systems in oil refineries, chemical plants, and power stations rely on strain gauge pressure transmitters (e.g., Rosemount 3051).
- **Biomedical:** Invasive blood pressure monitoring in ICUs uses miniaturized strain gauge pressure transducers.
- **Automotive:** Fuel injection pressure, brake line pressure, and tire pressure monitoring systems (TPMS).
- **Civil Engineering:** Structural health monitoring of bridges, dams, and tunnels using embedded pressure cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Voltage of a Wheatstone Bridge

**Starting Configuration:** A four-arm Wheatstone bridge excited by a stable DC source $V_{in}$, with all four arms containing active strain gauges.

Let $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$ be the nominal resistances of the four arms. The output voltage measured between the mid-points of the two branches is given by the standard voltage divider analysis.

The voltage at the left mid-point (between $R_1$ and $R_4$) measured with respect to ground:

$$V_{L} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_{4}}{R_{1} + R_{4}}$$

The voltage at the right mid-point (between $R_2$ and $R_3$) measured with respect to ground:

$$V_{R} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_{3}}{R_{2} + R_{3}}$$

The differential output voltage is:

$$V_{o} = V_{L} - V_{R} = V_{in} \left[ \dfrac{R_{4}}{R_{1} + R_{4}} - \dfrac{R_{3}}{R_{2} + R_{3}} \right]$$

Now apply a strain $\varepsilon$ to the gauges. Two gauges ($R_1$ and $R_3$) are in tension and two ($R_2$ and $R_4$) are in compression (Poisson configuration on a diaphragm).

The new resistances become:

$$R_{1} = R + \Delta R_{1}, \quad R_{3} = R + \Delta R_{3}$$
$$R_{2} = R - \Delta R_{2}, \quad R_{4} = R - \Delta R_{4}$$

Using the piezoresistive relation $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$, and substituting into the bridge equation, after algebraic simplification (assuming $\Delta R \ll R$, which is valid for small strains):

$$V_{o} \approx \dfrac{V_{in}}{4} \left[ \dfrac{\Delta R_{1}}{R_{1}} - \dfrac{\Delta R_{2}}{R_{2}} + \dfrac{\Delta R_{3}}{R_{3}} - \dfrac{\Delta R_{4}}{R_{4}} \right]$$

For Poisson arrangement on a diaphragm, the lateral (transverse) strain equals $-\nu \varepsilon$. The compression gauges therefore see a fractional resistance change of $-\nu \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ (in magnitude). Substituting:

$$V_{o} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{4} \left[ GF \cdot \varepsilon - (-GF \cdot (-\nu \varepsilon)) + GF \cdot \varepsilon - (-GF \cdot (-\nu \varepsilon)) \right]$$

$$V_{o} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{4} \left[ GF \cdot \varepsilon (1 + \nu) + GF \cdot \varepsilon (1 + \nu) \right] / 2$$

After the two tension gauges contribute $GF \cdot \varepsilon$ each, and the two Poisson compression gauges each contribute $\nu \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ in the opposite sense, the net is:

$$V_{o} = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (1 + \nu)}{4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The factor $(1 + \nu)$ arises because the compression gauges, lying on the orthogonal axis, experience a transverse strain of $-\nu \varepsilon$. For steel with $\nu = 0.30$, this factor is **1.30**, meaning the bridge output is 30% higher than a single axial gauge would provide.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure-to-Strain Conversion for a Circular Diaphragm

Consider a thin circular diaphragm of **diameter $D$** and **thickness $t$**, rigidly clamped at its circumference, subjected to a uniform pressure $P$ on one face.

The classical thin-plate theory (valid for $t \ll D$, typically $D/t > 20$) gives the maximum radial bending stress at the clamped edge as:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3 P D^{2} (1 - \nu^{2})}{8 t^{2}}$$

Using Hooke's law for uniaxial stress $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$, the maximum strain at the diaphragm edge is:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{E} = \dfrac{3 P D^{2} (1 - \nu^{2})}{8 E t^{2}}$$

This shows that strain is **directly proportional to applied pressure $P$**, which is the mathematical foundation for the **linear calibration curve** of the pressure cell.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Calibration Curve Linear Regression (Least Squares Method)

Given $n$ calibration data points $(x_i, y_i)$, where $x_i$ is the input (pressure) and $y_i$ is the output (voltage), the best-fit straight line $y = a + bx$ has coefficients:

$$b = \dfrac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^{2} - \left( \sum x_i \right)^{2}}$$

$$a = \dfrac{\sum y_i - b \sum x_i}{n}$$

These are computed in the lab and the constants $a$ (offset) and $b$ (sensitivity in V/Pa) characterize the cell.

### 3.4 Analytical Error Matrix (KTU Frequently Tested)

For each calibration point, the **deviation from best-fit line** is calculated as:

$$\Delta V_i = \vert V_{measured,i} - V_{ideal,i} \vert$$

The three KTU-required error components are then:

$$\text{Linearity } (\%) = \dfrac{\max(\Delta V_i)}{V_{FS}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Hysteresis } (\%) = \dfrac{\max \vert V_{up} - V_{down} \vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Repeatability } (\%) = \dfrac{\max \vert V_i - \bar{V} \vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Accuracy } (\%) = \sqrt{L^{2} + H^{2} + R^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The combined accuracy is computed using the **Root Sum Square (RSS)** method because the three errors are statistically independent. They cannot simply be added arithmetically.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Functional Block Architecture of the Calibration Setup

This block diagram maps the entire experimental flow from the pressure standard to the data acquisition system.

This diagram enumerates the lab procedure recommended for the KTU 2024 record submission.

    step1[Step 1 Inspect Equipment and Identify Components]

    step9[Step 9 Repeat Calibration Two More Times for Repeatability]
    step10[Step 10 Plot Calibration Graph Pressure Versus Voltage]
    step11[Step 11 Compute Linearity Hysteresis Repeatability]

    step1 --> step2
