> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Note for Students:** When drawing the Wheatstone bridge in your lab record, always mark the **polarity of output terminals (+ and −)**. The KTU examiner specifically checks for the **direction of strain** (tension/compression) at the gauge locations and the corresponding **sign convention** in the bridge arms. A reversed polarity gives a **negative output**, which is the most common error students make.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

Define **Gauge Factor (GF)** of a strain gauge. A strain gauge has a nominal resistance of **120 Ω** and a gauge factor of **2.1**. If it is subjected to a strain of **1000 microstrain**, calculate the change in resistance.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Gauge Factor is defined as the ratio of fractional change in resistance to the fractional change in length (strain). It is a dimensionless quantity that characterizes the sensitivity of a strain gauge.

$$GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

**Given:** $R = 120\ \Omega$, $GF = 2.1$, $\varepsilon = 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}$

**Calculation:**

$$\Delta R = GF \cdot R \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 120 \times 10^{-3} = 0.252\ \Omega$$

**Result:** The change in resistance is **0.252 Ω**.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

What is a **Dead Weight Tester (DWT)**? Why is it used as a primary standard for pressure calibration of the strain gauge pressure cell?

**Model Answer:**

A **Dead Weight Tester (DWT)** is a primary pressure standard consisting of a precision-machined piston-cylinder assembly on which accurately known masses are placed. The pressure generated is computed directly from fundamental SI units:

$$P = \dfrac{W}{A} = \dfrac{m \cdot g}{A}$$

where $W$ is the weight (force in N), $m$ is the total mass in kg, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$, and $A$ is the effective piston area in $\text{m}^{2}$.

**Reasons for using DWT as a primary standard:**

1. **Traceability to SI units** — Pressure is derived directly from mass, length, and time.
2. **High accuracy** — Typical accuracy is **±0.05%** of reading.
3. **Wide range** — By changing piston area and masses, pressures from **1 kPa to 100 MPa** can be generated.
4. **No intermediate calibration required** — Unlike secondary standards, no prior calibration chain is needed.
5. **Stability** — Long-term stability with proper maintenance exceeds decades.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part B question offers an internal choice between two alternatives. Both questions below are equivalent in marks and cognitive depth.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q.A.(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **principle of operation of a strain gauge pressure cell**. Discuss the role of the diaphragm and the bonding material. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of Operation:**
The strain gauge pressure cell converts fluid pressure into an electrical signal via the piezoresistive effect. When a fluid pressure **P** is applied through the inlet port onto one side of an elastic **circular diaphragm** (made of stainless steel or phosphor bronze), the diaphragm deflects. This deflection produces **mechanical strain** on the surface where the strain gauges are bonded.

**The bonded strain gauges** (metallic foil type) experience this strain, and their resistance changes according to:

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

The four strain gauges are wired as a **Wheatstone bridge** and excited by a stable DC voltage. The differential output voltage is proportional to the applied pressure.

**Role of the Diaphragm:**
- Acts as the **primary sensing element** that converts pressure to mechanical strain.
- Material must have **high yield strength, low hysteresis, and good linearity** (typically 17-4 PH stainless steel).
- The thickness **t** must be chosen such that maximum strain at the operating pressure remains within the **elastic limit** (typically 0.1% to 0.3% strain).
- Diameter **D** and thickness **t** are designed using: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{3 P D^{2} (1 - \nu^{2})}{8 E t^{2}}$

**Role of the Bonding Material:**
- The bonding agent (typically **epoxy resin** like M-Bond 610 or cyanoacrylate) must transfer strain from the diaphragm to the gauge with **100% fidelity**.
- It must have **negligible creep**, good shear strength, and stable operation over the temperature range.
- Improper bonding causes **hysteresis, drift, and loss of sensitivity**.

---

**Q.A.(b)** With the help of a circuit diagram, derive the **output voltage equation of a full Wheatstone bridge** with all four arms active. State the advantage of the Poisson configuration. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Circuit:** Four strain gauges form the four arms of a Wheatstone bridge excited by $V_{in}$.

**Derivation:** (Already shown in detail in Section 3.1 of these notes.)

The general expression for the differential output is:

$$V_{o} = V_{in} \left[ \dfrac{R_{4}}{R_{1} + R_{4}} - \dfrac{R_{3}}{R_{2} + R_{3}} \right]$$

For small $\Delta R$ and all four arms active, with two gauges in tension and two in compression (Poisson configuration):

$$V_{o} = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (1 + \nu)}{4}$$

**Advantages of the Poisson Configuration:**

1. **Increased Sensitivity:** Output is multiplied by $(1 + \nu) \approx 1.3$ for steel — a 30% gain over axial-only configuration.
2. **Built-in Temperature Compensation:** All four gauges are bonded to the same diaphragm, so temperature-induced resistance changes cancel out at the bridge null.
3. **Common-Mode Rejection:** Bridge inherently rejects any common-mode excitation noise.
4. **Linear Output:** The $(1 + \nu)$ factor is constant; thus linearity is preserved.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Q.B.(a)** Explain the **complete procedure for calibrating a strain gauge pressure cell** using a dead weight tester. Include the calibration graph, calculation of linearity, hysteresis, and repeatability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus Required:** Dead Weight Tester, Strain Gauge Pressure Cell, Regulated DC Power Supply (5V/10V), Digital Voltmeter (DVM), Connecting cables, Stop watch.

**Procedure:**

1. **Inspection:** Check the DWT for cleanliness, free movement of piston, and absence of leakage.
2. **Connection:** Connect the pressure cell inlet to the DWT outlet using a flexible high-pressure hose. Bleed air to remove any trapped air bubbles.
3. **Excitation:** Apply a stable DC excitation voltage (e.g., 5 V) to the bridge. Allow a **warm-up time of 5 minutes** for thermal stabilization.
4. **Zero Adjustment:** With zero pressure applied, adjust the bridge balance potentiometer until the DVM reads **0.000 mV**.
5. **Loading Cycle (Increasing Pressure):** Place the first calibrated weight on the DWT piston. Allow the pressure to stabilize (about 30 seconds). Record the DVM reading in mV. Repeat for **5 to 10 pressure increments** up to the full scale.
6. **Unloading Cycle (Decreasing Pressure):** Remove weights one by one in the reverse order. Record DVM readings at each step.
7. **Repeat Runs:** Repeat the entire loading-unloading cycle **two more times** to assess repeatability.
8. **Data Tabulation:** Compile a table with columns: Pressure (bar), Output Up (mV), Output Down (mV), Mean (mV).
9. **Graph Plotting:** Plot **Pressure (X-axis) vs Mean Output Voltage (Y-axis)** on a graph sheet. Draw the best-fit straight line through the data points.
10. **Error Computation:** Calculate Linearity (L%), Hysteresis (H%), and Repeatability (R%) using the formulas in Section 2.3.
11. **Combined Accuracy:** Compute $A = \sqrt{L^{2} + H^{2} + R^{2}}\%$.

**Typical Result:** A well-calibrated industrial-grade strain gauge pressure cell shows accuracy between **±0.25% to ±0.5% of full scale**.

---

**Q.B.(b)** During calibration of a strain gauge pressure cell with a full-scale range of **10 bar**, the following readings were obtained at three repeated trials (increasing pressure only, in mV):

| Pressure (bar) | Trial 1 (mV) | Trial 2 (mV) | Trial 3 (mV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 4.12 | 4.18 | 4.15 |
| 4 | 8.20 | 8.25 | 8.22 |
| 6 | 12.35 | 12.40 | 12.38 |
| 8 | 16.50 | 16.55 | 16.52 |
| 10 | 20.60 | 20.68 | 20.65 |

Also given: ideal straight-line output equation is **$V = 2.06 \cdot P$** (mV). Calculate the **repeatability** and **combined accuracy** if the linearity error is **0.45%** and hysteresis is **0.30%**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate mean output at each pressure.**

At $P = 2$ bar: $\bar{V} = (4.12 + 4.18 + 4.15) / 3 = 4.15$ mV
At $P = 4$ bar: $\bar{V} = (8.20 + 8.25 + 8.22) / 3 = 8.223$ mV
At $P = 6$ bar: $\bar{V} = (12.35 + 12.40 + 12.38) / 3 = 12.377$ mV
At $P = 8$ bar: $\bar{V} = (16.50 + 16.55 + 16.52) / 3 = 16.523$ mV
At $P = 10$ bar: $\bar{V} = (20.60 + 20.68 + 20.65) / 3 = 20.643$ mV

**Step 2: Compute repeatability at each point.**

Repeatability is the maximum deviation of any reading from the mean, expressed as a percentage of full scale ($V_{FS} = 20.643$ mV at 10 bar).

At $P = 2$ bar: max deviation = $\vert 4.18 - 4.15 \vert = 0.03$ mV
At $P = 4$ bar: max deviation = $\vert 8.25 - 8.223 \vert = 0.027$ mV
At $P = 6$ bar: max deviation = $\vert 12.40 - 12.377 \vert = 0.023$ mV
At $P = 8$ bar: max deviation = $\vert 16.55 - 16.523 \vert = 0.027$ mV
At $P = 10$ bar: max deviation = $\vert 20.68 - 20.643 \vert = 0.037$ mV

Maximum deviation across all points = **0.037 mV** (at 10 bar).

**Step 3: Repeatability percentage.**

$$R\% = \dfrac{0.037}{20.643} \times 100 = 0.179\%$$

**Step 4: Combined Accuracy (RSS method).**

Given: $L = 0.45\%$, $H = 0.30\%$, $R = 0.179\%$

$$A = \sqrt{L^{2} + H^{2} + R^{2}} = \sqrt{(0.45)^{2} + (0.30)^{2} + (0.179)^{2}}$$

$$A = \sqrt{0.2025 + 0.09 + 0.0320} = \sqrt{0.3245}$$

$$A \approx 0.57\%$$

**Final Result:** The strain gauge pressure cell has a **combined accuracy of approximately ±0.57% of full scale**, which is acceptable for general industrial applications (Class 1.0 instrumentation).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Bridge Output:** Students often forget to apply the **negative sign** for compression gauges in the Poisson configuration. The formula is **$V_o = V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon (1 + \nu) / 4$**, NOT with $(1 - \nu)$. Verify the direction of strain before writing the bridge equation.
>
> 2. **Units of Gauge Factor:** Gauge Factor is **dimensionless**. Do not write units like m/Ω. KTU examiners specifically deduct 0.5 marks for this.
>
> 3. **Full-Scale Value:** In accuracy calculations, $V_{FS}$ is the output at **maximum rated pressure**, NOT at the maximum measured deviation. Many students mistakenly use the wrong denominator.
>
> 4. **Hysteresis Requires Loading AND Unloading:** Repeating only the loading cycle does NOT give hysteresis. You **must** record both directions and compute $\vert V_{up} - V_{down} \vert$.
>
> 5. **Dead Weight Pressure Units:** $P = mg/A$ gives Pascals (N/m²). Convert to **bar** ($1\ \text{bar} = 10^{5}$ Pa) before plotting if the calibration is in bar units. Mixing units is the **most common reason for losing 2 marks** in numericals.
>
> 6. **Bonding Material Naming:** Examiners expect the exact name — **"M-Bond 610"** or **"epoxy resin"** — not vague terms like "glue" or "adhesive."

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- The **strain gauge pressure cell** is a **piezoresistive transducer** that converts pressure into voltage via the intermediary of **mechanical strain**.
- **Gauge Factor** is the most critical parameter of the strain gauge: $GF = (\Delta R/R)/\varepsilon$.
- **Wheatstone bridge** is mandatory for measurement; it provides **temperature compensation**, **common-mode rejection**, and **signal amplification**.
- The **Poisson configuration** with four active gauges gives a $(1 + \nu) \approx 1.3$ sensitivity boost for steel.
- The **Dead Weight Tester** is the **primary standard** for pressure calibration in this experiment.
- The **best-fit straight line** is computed using the **least-squares method**.
- Three errors must be computed: **Linearity, Hysteresis, Repeatability** — and combined via **RSS method**.
- A typical industrial strain gauge cell achieves **±0.25% to ±0.5%** accuracy.
- **Diaphragm material** choice (stainless steel, phosphor bronze) directly affects linearity, hysteresis, and fatigue life.
- The output voltage is typically **mV level** — always amplify before ADC conversion.
- **Warm-up time** of 5 minutes is essential before any calibration reading.
- The **calibration graph** must include the best-fit line, the actual data points, and a clearly labeled axes with units.
- **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** This experiment directly addresses **CO3 (Calibration of transducers)** and **CO4 (Error analysis and statistical interpretation)**.
- Remember the **11 high-yield formulas** from Section 2.3 — they form the basis of any numerical problem in the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Vibration Using Piezoelectric Accelerometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of Vibration Using Piezoelectric Accelerometers # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Piezoelectric Accelerometer** is a passive, electro-mechanical vibration transducer that converts the *mechanical kinetic energy of a vibrating surface* into a measurable *electrical charge* (or voltage) by exploiting the **direct piezoelectric effect** of certain anisotropic crystals such as Quartz, Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT), Barium Titanate, or Tourmaline. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCMRL308 — Instrumentation Laboratory), the experiment is defined as the experimental determination of the *amplitude, frequency, and acceleration* of a vibrating test specimen (typically a cantilever beam, motor housing, or shake table) using a piezoelectric accelerometer coupled to a **charge amplifier** and a digital storage oscilloscope / FFT analyzer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 Scheme – PCMRL308):** > The experiment is mapped to **Course Outcome CO2** ("Design and analyse measurement systems involving transducers and signal conditioning circuits") and **CO4** ("Conduct experiments, analyse data, and interpret results using modern instrumentation tools"). The cognitive level as per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy is **Apply / Analyse (Level 3-4)**. > [!NOTE] > **Piezoelectric Effect – Historical Context:** > The direct piezoelectric effect was discovered by **Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880**. The word *piezo* is derived from the Greek word *piezein*, meaning "to press" or "to squeeze", which describes the mechanical compression that produces the electrical output. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tiny Spring-Mass Thinker" Imagine you have glued a small steel ball (called the **seismic mass** or **proof mass**) on top of a piece of soft cheese (representing the **piezoelectric crystal**), and the cheese is mounted on a metal plate (the **base**). Now shake the metal plate rapidly up and down (this is the **vibration** you want to measure). Because of Newton's First Law (inertia), the steel ball "wants" to stay in its original position. So, the cheese gets *squeezed and stretched* rhythmically as the plate moves while the ball lags behind. The cheese has a magical property: *whenever it is squeezed, it generates a tiny voltage across its surfaces.* The harder it is squeezed, the bigger the voltage. By measuring this voltage, you can find out exactly how hard the plate was shaken — which is, by definition, its **acceleration**. > [!TIP] > **Key Realisation:** The accelerometer does *not* measure displacement directly, nor velocity. It measures the **acceleration** of the surface, from which the velocity and displacement can be mathematically reconstructed via single and double integration respectively. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | Piezoelectric charge constant | $d$ | $\approx 2.3 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/N}$ (Quartz) | | Seismic (proof) mass | $m$ | $5\text{–}50\ \text{g}$ | | Base strain sensitivity | — | $\leq 0.001\ \text{g/g}$ | | Standard gravitational acceleration | $g$ | $\mathbf{9.80665\ m/s^2}$ | | Charge sensitivity (typical) | $S_q$ | $1\text{–}100\ \text{pC/g}$ | | Voltage sensitivity (typical) | $S_v$ | $10\text{–}1000\ \text{mV/g}$ | | Operating frequency range | $f$ | $\mathbf{0.1\ Hz\ to\ 20\ kHz}$ | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" Behind Each Step The piezoelectric accelerometer is fundamentally a **force-measuring device** that infers acceleration through Newton's Second Law ($F = ma$). The "Why" behind every component is structured below: ### Step 1 — Mechanical Coupling of Base Motion The base of the accelerometer is rigidly attached to the vibrating surface. The **base motion** $x_b(t)$ is, by assumption, identical to the surface motion of the structure under test. ### Step 2 — Inertial Force Generation by Seismic Mass The seismic mass $m$ resists motion change due to its **inertia**. When the base accelerates with $a_b(t) = \ddot{x}_b(t)$, the relative displacement between mass and base produces an **inertial reaction force**: $$F_{\text{inertial}} = -m \cdot a_b(t)$$ This is the *only physical quantity* the piezoelectric element senses. ### Step 3 — Direct Piezoelectric Conversion The piezoelectric crystal, sandwiched between the mass and the base, experiences this force $F$. By the direct piezoelectric effect: $$q(t) = d \cdot F(t) = d \cdot m \cdot a_b(t)$$ The generated **charge is linearly proportional to the applied force**, and hence to the acceleration. ### Step 4 — Signal Conditioning via Charge Amplifier The piezoelectric element behaves as a charge source in parallel with a small capacitance $C_p$ (typically 500 pF – 2 nF) and a very high leakage resistance $R_p$ ($\geq 10^{12}\ \Omega$). Because of this high output impedance, a **charge amplifier** is mandatory to convert the charge into a usable low-impedance voltage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Insight:** > A piezoelectric accelerometer **cannot measure static (DC) acceleration** (e.g., Earth's gravity). This is because the generated charge leaks away through the finite insulation resistance over time. The minimum measurable frequency is set by the discharge time constant $\tau = R_p C_p$, typically yielding a low-frequency cut-off of $0.1\text{–}5\ \text{Hz}$. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination Ready) | # | Equation / Formula | Description / Use | |:--|:--|:--| | 1 | $q = d \cdot F$ | Direct piezoelectric charge generation (Coulomb's law of piezo) | | 2 | $F = m \cdot a$ | Newton's second law applied to seismic mass | | 3 | $q = d \cdot m \cdot a$ | Combined output charge vs. acceleration | | 4 | $V_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{q}{C_f} = \dfrac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_f}$ | Charge-amplifier output voltage | | 5 | $S_q = \dfrac{q}{a} = d \cdot m$ | Charge sensitivity (units: $\text{pC/g}$ or $\text{pC/(m/s}^2)$) | | 6 | $S_v = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{a} = \dfrac{d \cdot m}{C_f}$ | Voltage sensitivity (units: $\text{mV/g}$) | | 7 | $a = \omega^2 \cdot x_0 = (2\pi f)^2 \cdot x_0$ | Kinematic relation between acceleration and displacement amplitude | | 8 | $x_0 = \dfrac{a_0}{(2\pi f)^2}$ | Displacement amplitude computed from acceleration | | 9 | $v_0 = \dfrac{a_0}{2\pi f}$ | Velocity amplitude computed from acceleration | | 10 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural (resonant) frequency of the seismic system | | 11 | $\xi = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | Damping ratio of the accelerometer | | 12 | $f_{\text{use}} \leq 0.3 \cdot f_n$ | Upper useful frequency limit (to keep error $\leq 5\%$) | | 13 | $1\ g = 9.80665\ \text{m/s}^2$ | Standard gravity conversion | | 14 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio in decibels | | 15 | $f_{\text{low}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$ | Low-frequency cut-off of the charge amplifier | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility — Why this matters beyond the exam:** > Piezoelectric accelerometers are the *workhorse* of **Machine Condition Monitoring (MCM)** in Industry 4.0 factories. They are used for: > - Predictive maintenance of rotating machinery (motors, turbines, gearboxes) > - Modal analysis and structural health monitoring (SHM) of bridges and aircraft wings > - Seismic instrumentation in civil engineering > - Shock testing of defence and automotive components > - NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) testing in automotive R&D --- ## 2.3 Frequency Response — The "How" Behind Accurate Measurement The accelerometer behaves as a **second-order underdamped mechanical system** with transfer function (acceleration base to output charge): $$\dfrac{Q(s)}{A_b(s)} = \dfrac{d \cdot m \cdot \omega_n^2}{s^2 + 2\xi\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}$$ | Region | Behaviour | Measurement Quality | |:--|:--|:--| | $f \ll f_n$ | Magnitude $\approx$ constant (flat) | **Excellent** (use this range) | | $f \approx f_n$ | Resonance peak ($Q$ amplification) | **Avoid** — non-linear error | | $f \gg f_n$ | Roll-off at $-40\ \text{dB/decade}$ | **Unusable** — mass decouples from base | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The student must always state the **rule of thumb** — *operate the accelerometer at frequencies below 1/3 of its resonant frequency* to maintain a flat response within $\pm 5\%$ amplitude error. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Charge Output of a Piezoelectric Accelerometer **Statement to prove:** The charge generated by the piezoelectric crystal is directly proportional to the base acceleration, and the proportionality constant is the **charge sensitivity** $S_q = d \cdot m$. **Given data / assumptions:** - Piezoelectric material has charge constant $d$ (in C/N). - Seismic mass is $m$ (in kg). - The base of the accelerometer undergoes acceleration $a(t)$ (in m/s²). - The crystal is rigid enough to transmit the full inertial force without loss. **Step-by-step derivation:** We begin with the constitutive equation of the direct piezoelectric effect, which linearly couples mechanical stress $T$ to generated electric displacement $D$: $$D = d \cdot T$$ Here, the stress acting on the crystal is the inertial force per unit area. For a crystal of area $A_e$: $$T = \dfrac{F}{A_e} = \dfrac{m \cdot a}{A_e}$$ The total charge generated is the electric displacement integrated over the electrode area: $$q = D \cdot A_e$$ Substituting the expression for $D$: $$q = (d \cdot T) \cdot A_e = d \cdot \left(\dfrac{m \cdot a}{A_e}\right) \cdot A_e$$ The electrode area $A_e$ cancels out: $$q = d \cdot m \cdot a$$ Rearranging to express acceleration in terms of measured charge: $$a = \dfrac{q}{d \cdot m} = \dfrac{q}{S_q}$$ where the **charge sensitivity** is defined as: $$S_q \triangleq d \cdot m \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{C}}{\text{m/s}^2}\right]$$ > **Result:** The acceleration is recovered from the measured charge by simply dividing by the constant $S_q$. This is the foundation of the entire measurement system. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Output Voltage of a Charge Amplifier **Statement to prove:** The output voltage of an ideal charge amplifier is $V_{\text{out}} = -q / C_f$, independent of the piezoelectric capacitance or cable capacitance. **Circuit topology:** An operational amplifier in inverting configuration with a feedback capacitor $C_f$ (and parallel feedback resistor $R_f$ to set the DC bias path), whose input is connected to the piezoelectric sensor. **Step-by-step derivation:** The op-amp is assumed ideal: - Input impedance is infinite (no current flows into the inverting input). - The two input terminals are at the same potential (virtual short), so $V_- = 0\ \text{V}$ (virtual ground). The current flowing from the piezoelectric source $q$ into the inverting node must, by Kirchhoff's Current Law, all flow through the feedback capacitor $C_f$: $$i_{\text{in}} = i_{C_f}$$ The input current from the charge source is the time derivative of charge: $$i_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{dq}{dt}$$ The current through the capacitor $C_f$ is: $$i_{C_f} = C_f \cdot \dfrac{d(V_- - V_{\text{out}})}{dt} = -C_f \cdot \dfrac{dV_{\text{out}}}{dt}$$ Equating both currents: $$\dfrac{dq}{dt} = -C_f \cdot \dfrac{dV_{\text{out}}}{dt}$$ Integrating both sides with respect to time (the constants of integration vanish because both charge and voltage are zero in steady state): $$q(t) = -C_f \cdot V_{\text{out}}(t)$$ Therefore: $$V_{\text{out}}(t) = -\dfrac{q(t)}{C_f} = -\dfrac{d \cdot m \cdot a(t)}{C_f}$$ > **Result:** The output voltage is **inverted** in sign and scaled by the feedback capacitance $C_f$. Crucially, it is **independent of the source capacitance** $C_p$ or any cable capacitance $C_c$, which is the key advantage of the charge-mode amplifier over the voltage-mode amplifier. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kinematic Reconstruction of Displacement from Acceleration **Statement to prove:** If the acceleration is $a(t) = a_0 \sin(\omega t)$, then the displacement amplitude is $x_0 = a_0 / \omega^2$. **Step-by-step derivation:** The velocity is the first integral of acceleration: $$v(t) = \int a(t)\,dt = \int a_0 \sin(\omega t)\,dt = -\dfrac{a_0}{\omega}\cos(\omega t) + C_1$$ Assuming zero mean velocity (no DC drift), the integration constant is zero. The velocity amplitude is: $$v_0 = \dfrac{a_0}{\omega}$$ The displacement is the second integral of acceleration: $$x(t) = \int v(t)\,dt = \int -\dfrac{a_0}{\omega}\cos(\omega t)\,dt = -\dfrac{a_0}{\omega^2}\sin(\omega t) + C_2$$ Again, with zero mean displacement: $$x_0 = \dfrac{a_0}{\omega^2} = \dfrac{a_0}{(2\pi f)^2}$$ > **Result:** This kinematic identity allows the experimenter to recover displacement and velocity signals from a single acceleration measurement — a critical capability validated during lab viva voce. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical Problem) **Problem Statement:** A piezoelectric accelerometer has a seismic mass of $m = 10\ \text{g}$ and a quartz crystal with $d = 2.3\ \text{pC/N}$. It is connected to a charge amplifier with $C_f = 1\ \text{nF}$. The accelerometer output is observed on an oscilloscope as a sine wave of peak-to-peak voltage $V_{pp} = 4.0\ \text{V}$ at a frequency of $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$. Calculate: 1. The peak acceleration $a_0$ (in $\text{m/s}^2$ and in $g$). 2. The peak displacement amplitude $x_0$ (in $\text{mm}$). 3. The peak velocity amplitude $v_0$ (in $\text{m/s}$). **Solution:** **Given data:** - $m = 10\ \text{g} = 0.010\ \text{kg}$ - $d = 2.3 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/N}$ - $C_f = 1\ \text{nF} = 1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F}$ - $V_{pp} = 4.0\ \text{V} \Rightarrow V_{\text{peak}} = V_0 = 2.0\ \text{V}$ - $f = 50\ \text{Hz} \Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi f = 314.159\ \text{rad/s}$ **Step 1 — Peak acceleration:** The charge sensitivity is: $$S_q = d \cdot m = (2.3 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (0.010) = 2.3 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{C·s}^2/\text{m}$$ The peak charge generated is: $$q_0 = C_f \cdot V_0 = (1 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (2.0) = 2.0 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{C}$$ The peak acceleration is: $$a_0 = \dfrac{q_0}{S_q} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-9}}{2.3 \times 10^{-14}} = 86{,}956.5\ \text{m/s}^2$$ Converting to $g$: $$a_0 = \dfrac{86{,}956.5}{9.80665} \approx 8867.7\ g$$ **Step 2 — Peak displacement amplitude:** $$x_0 = \dfrac{a_0}{\omega^2} = \dfrac{86{,}956.5}{(314.159)^2} = \dfrac{86{,}956.5}{98{,}696.0} \approx 0.881\ \text{m} = 881\ \text{mm}$$ **Step 3 — Peak velocity amplitude:** $$v_0 = \dfrac{a_0}{\omega} = \dfrac{86{,}956.5}{314.159} \approx 276.8\ \text{m/s}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation for KTU Lab Record:** > The experimentally observed vibration on a shake table or cantilever beam will typically be in the range $1\text{–}20\ g$ and $0.1\text{–}5\ \text{mm}$ displacement. The numerical example above uses exaggerated values for pedagogical clarity. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Measurement Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Vibrating Test Specimen
Shake Table or Motor] -->|Mechanical Vibration| B[Piezoelectric Accelerometer
Seismic Mass + PZT Crystal] B -->|Charge q = d m a| C[Low-Noise Coaxial Cable
with Shielding] C -->|High Impedance Charge Signal| D[Charge Amplifier
Op-Amp with C_f] D -->|Low Impedance V_out = -q / C_f| E[Signal Conditioning
Anti-Aliasing Filter] E -->|Filtered Analog Signal| F[Data Acquisition
Oscilloscope or DAQ] F -->|FFT or Time Domain| G[PC with LabVIEW
or MATLAB] G -->|Numerical Integration| H[Reconstructed Velocity
and Displacement] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The signal flow is strictly one-directional: *Mechanical vibration $\rightarrow$ Charge generation $\rightarrow$ Charge-to-Voltage conversion $\rightarrow$ Filtering $\rightarrow$ Digitisation $\rightarrow$ Numerical integration for velocity/displacement.* --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Construction of a Shear-Type Accelerometer ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A[Accelerometer Housing] M1[Top Mounting Stud] PZT[Piezoelectric Disc 1
Poled +] M2[Central Seismic Mass
Post between two PZTs] PZT2[Piezoelectric Disc 2
Poled -] BASE[Base Plate with Mounting Hole] end M1 --> PZT PZT --> M2 M2 --> PZT2 PZT2 --> BASE OUT1[Output Terminal +] -.-> PZT OUT2[Output Terminal -] -.-> PZT2 ``` > **Design Note:** The *shear-type* configuration (shown above) is the industry standard because it is **insensitive to base strain and thermal transients**, which is why KTU examiners prefer it as the default reference design. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Circuit Schematic — Charge Amplifier (Using Mermaid Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR QS[Charge Source q from
Piezoelectric Sensor] -->|Input Node| SUM((Virtual
Ground)) SUM -->|Inverting Input| OP[Op-Amp
TL072 or AD549] OP -->|Output| VOUT[V_out = -q / C_f] SUM --- CF[Feedback Capacitor C_f] CF --- VOUT SUM --- RF[Feedback Resistor R_f
Sets DC Bias Path] RF --- VOUT OP -->|Non-Inverting Input| GND[Ground 0 V] VOUT --> RL[Load Resistor R_L] RL --> GND ``` > **Operational Notes:** > 1. The op-amp must be a **FET-input type** (e.g., AD549, OPA128) with input bias current $\leq 50\ \text{fA}$ to avoid discharging the piezo source. > 2. $R_f$ is typically $10\ \text{M}\Omega$ to $100\ \text{G}\Omega$, setting the low-frequency cut-off $f_{\text{low}} = 1 / (2\pi R_f C_f)$. --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Frequency Response Plot (Block Diagram Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LOW[Low Frequency Region f less than f_n] FL1[Flat Response 0 dB Gain] end subgraph MID[Resonance Region f approx f_n] FL2[Sharp Peak Q factor
Typically Q = 5 to 30] end subgraph HIGH[High Frequency Region f greater than f_n] FL3[Roll-off minus 40 dB per decade
Unusable Region] end LOW --> MID --> HIGH ``` > **Engineering Practice:** Always choose an accelerometer whose **resonant frequency is at least 3× to 5× higher** than the highest frequency component present in the vibration being measured. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Q: Define the direct piezoelectric effect. Name any two piezoelectric materials commonly used in accelerometers.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. *Definition (2 marks):* The **direct piezoelectric effect** is the phenomenon whereby certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (such as quartz, Rochelle salt, tourmaline, and PZT) develop an **electric charge across their faces when subjected to mechanical stress**. The generated charge is linearly proportional to the applied force, with the constant of proportionality being the *piezoelectric charge constant $d$*, given by the constitutive relation $q = d \cdot F$. 2. *Materials (1 mark):* The two most commonly used piezoelectric materials in commercial accelerometers are **(i) Quartz (SiO₂)** — preferred for its excellent temperature stability and linearity, and **(ii) Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)** — preferred for its very high charge sensitivity. --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – December 2023]` **Q: Why is a charge amplifier preferred over a voltage amplifier for piezoelectric accelerometer signal conditioning?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. *Source characteristic (1 mark):* A piezoelectric accelerometer is a **charge source**, not a voltage source. Its output charge is independent of the connected capacitance, but the voltage $V = q / C$ is inversely proportional to it. 2. *Cable sensitivity (1 mark):* A voltage amplifier's output depends on the **cable capacitance** $C_c$, which varies with cable length and type, introducing calibration errors. 3. *Charge amplifier advantage (1 mark):* A charge amplifier, built around an inverting op-amp with feedback capacitor $C_f$, gives $V_{\text{out}} = -q / C_f$, which is **completely independent of source and cable capacitance**, providing a stable, calibration-free output that can drive long cables. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Option A `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a piezoelectric accelerometer. Derive the relation between output charge and base acceleration. **Model Answer:** **Construction (3 marks):** A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of the following components, arranged in a sealed metal housing: - **Base plate** with a mounting stud for attachment to the vibrating surface. - **Piezoelectric element** — a disc of PZT or quartz, sandwiched between two electrodes. - **Seismic (proof) mass** — a heavy tungsten or brass block loaded on top of the crystal to provide the inertia. - **Output terminals** — connected to the electrodes, brought out via a coaxial connector. - **Pre-load sleeve** — applies a constant compressive force to hold the crystal firmly. **Working (2 marks):** When the base vibrates with acceleration $a$, the seismic mass $m$ tends to remain stationary due to inertia. The relative motion compresses and decompresses the piezoelectric crystal, generating an alternating charge proportional to the inertial force $F = m a$. **Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, combining the piezoelectric constitutive law $q = d F$ with Newton's second law $F = m a$: $$q = d \cdot m \cdot a$$ The proportionality constant $S_q = d \cdot m$ is called the **charge sensitivity** of the accelerometer (typically expressed in $\text{pC/g}$). --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A piezoelectric accelerometer has a charge sensitivity of $S_q = 50\ \text{pC/g}$. It is connected to a charge amplifier with $C_f = 2.2\ \text{nF}$. The output of the charge amplifier is observed on an oscilloscope as a sine wave with $V_{pp} = 880\ \text{mV}$ at $f = 200\ \text{Hz}$. Compute (i) peak acceleration in $g$ and $\text{m/s}^2$, (ii) peak velocity in $\text{m/s}$, (iii) peak displacement in $\text{mm}$. **Model Answer:** **Given:** - $S_q = 50\ \text{pC/g} = 50 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/g}$ - $C_f = 2.2\ \text{nF} = 2.2 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F}$ - $V_{pp} = 880\ \text{mV} = 0.880\ \text{V} \Rightarrow V_0 = 0.440\ \text{V}$ - $f = 200\ \text{Hz} \Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi f = 1256.64\ \text{rad/s}$ **(i) Peak acceleration (3 marks):** The peak charge generated is: $$q_0 = C_f \cdot V_0 = (2.2 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (0.440) = 9.68 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{C}$$ Converting to picocoulombs: $$q_0 = 968\ \text{pC}$$ Dividing by charge sensitivity: $$a_0 = \dfrac{q_0}{S_q} = \dfrac{968\ \text{pC}}{50\ \text{pC/g}} = 19.36\ g$$ Converting to SI: $$a_0 = 19.36 \times 9.80665 = 189.86\ \text{m/s}^2$$ **(ii) Peak velocity (2 marks):** $$v_0 = \dfrac{a_0}{\omega} = \dfrac{189.86}{1256.64} = 0.1511\ \text{m/s} = 151.1\ \text{mm/s}$$ **(iii) Peak displacement (2 marks):** $$x_0 = \dfrac{a_0}{\omega^2} = \dfrac{189.86}{(1256.64)^2} = \dfrac{189.86}{1{,}579{,}136.7} = 1.203 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 0.1203\ \text{mm}$$ --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Option B (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam – December 2023, Module 2]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a circuit diagram the operation of a charge amplifier used with piezoelectric sensors. Derive the expression for its output voltage. Why must the op-amp used be of the FET-input type? **Model Answer:** **Circuit operation (2 marks):** The charge amplifier is built around an inverting op-amp with a feedback capacitor $C_f$ (typically 100 pF to 10 nF) in parallel with a high-value feedback resistor $R_f$ (10 MΩ to 100 GΩ). The piezoelectric sensor is connected between the inverting input (virtual ground) and the system ground. The non-inverting input is grounded. **Derivation of output voltage (3 marks):** As shown in Section 3.2, applying Kirchhoff's Current Law at the inverting node (which is a virtual ground, $V_- = 0$) and using the op-amp's infinite input impedance: $$\dfrac{dq}{dt} = -C_f \cdot \dfrac{dV_{\text{out}}}{dt} \Rightarrow V_{\text{out}} = -\dfrac{q}{C_f}$$ The role of $R_f$ is to provide a DC bias path; it sets the low-frequency cut-off at $f_{\text{low}} = 1 / (2\pi R_f C_f)$. **FET-input requirement (2 marks):** The piezoelectric source has an extremely high source impedance (of the order of $10^{12}\ \Omega$). A standard bipolar op-amp has an input bias current of several nanoamperes, which would discharge the piezoelectric capacitance in milliseconds, causing signal drift and amplitude error. A **FET-input op-amp** (such as AD549, OPA128, or TL072) has an input bias current in the femtoampere range ($\leq 50\ \text{fA}$), which is negligible compared to the source current, preserving the charge signal faithfully. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A cantilever beam made of mild steel is excited by an electromagnetic shaker at its free end. A piezoelectric accelerometer mounted at the free end gives a peak-to-peak output of $V_{pp} = 1.6\ \text{V}$ through a charge amplifier with $C_f = 1\ \text{nF}$. The accelerometer sensitivity is $S_q = 100\ \text{pC/g}$. Compute (i) the peak acceleration, (ii) the peak velocity, and (iii) the peak displacement. Take the excitation frequency as $f = 100\ \text{Hz}$. If the accelerometer is replaced by another one with twice the charge sensitivity, what is the new output voltage for the same vibration level? **Model Answer:** **Given:** - $V_{pp} = 1.6\ \text{V} \Rightarrow V_0 = 0.8\ \text{V}$ - $C_f = 1\ \text{nF} = 1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F}$ - $S_q = 100\ \text{pC/g}$ - $f = 100\ \text{Hz} \Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi f = 628.32\ \text{rad/s}$ **(i) Peak acceleration (2 marks):** $$q_0 = C_f \cdot V_0 = (1 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (0.8) = 8 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{C} = 800\ \text{pC}$$ $$a_0 = \dfrac{q_0}{S_q} = \dfrac{800\ \text{pC}}{100\ \text{pC/g}} = 8\ g = 8 \times 9.80665 = 78.45\ \text{m/s}^2$$ **(ii) Peak velocity (2 marks):** $$v_0 = \dfrac{a_0}{\omega} = \dfrac{78.45}{628.32} = 0.1249\ \text{m/s} = 124.9\ \text{mm/s}$$ **(iii) Peak displacement (2 marks):** $$x_0 = \dfrac{a_0}{\omega^2} = \dfrac{78.45}{(628.32)^2} = \dfrac{78.45}{394{,}784} = 1.987 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} \approx 0.199\ \text{mm}$$ **New output voltage (1 mark):** If the charge sensitivity doubles to $S_q' = 200\ \text{pC/g}$, the charge generated for the *same* acceleration doubles ($q_0' = 1600\ \text{pC}$), and therefore the output voltage doubles: $$V_{pp}' = 2 \times V_{pp} = 3.2\ \text{V}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Students frequently forget to convert $g$ to $\text{m/s}^2$ (multiply by $9.80665$) or Hz to rad/s (multiply by $2\pi$). Always show the unit conversion explicitly. > 2. **Peak vs. RMS confusion:** When the oscilloscope shows $V_{pp}$ (peak-to-peak), divide by 2 to get $V_0$ (peak). Do **not** confuse with $V_{\text{rms}} = V_0 / \sqrt{2}$. > 3. **Sign of output:** The charge amplifier inverts the signal. Mention this in viva if asked, but it does not affect amplitude calculations. > 4. **Kinematic relation memory:** Many students write $x = a \cdot \omega$ instead of $x = a / \omega^2$. Memorise the *inversely proportional* relationship with the square of frequency. > 5. **Stating limitations:** Always mention the two key limitations in the viva: (a) cannot measure DC/static acceleration, (b) requires low-noise cable and FET-input amplifier. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Block — Print This Section Before the Exam** - **Working principle:** Direct piezoelectric effect — charge $q = d \cdot F$ generated by stress on a non-centrosymmetric crystal. - **Newton's second law link:** $F = m a$, so $q = d \cdot m \cdot a$, giving the **charge sensitivity** $S_q = d \cdot m$. - **Three core elements:** Seismic mass (provides inertia) + Piezoelectric crystal (provides transduction) + Base (couples to test surface). - **Two main configurations:** *Compression type* (high sensitivity, susceptible to base strain) and *Shear type* (industry standard, immune to base strain). - **Signal conditioner is mandatory:** Piezo accelerometers need a **charge amplifier** with $V_{\text{out}} = -q / C_f$; op-amp must be **FET-input** for high input impedance. - **Frequency response:** Operate below $0.3 \cdot f_n$ for $\pm 5\%$ accuracy; avoid the resonance region; the response rolls off at $-40\ \text{dB/decade}$ above $f_n$. - **Kinematic identities:** $v_0 = a_0 / \omega$ and $x_0 = a_0 / \omega^2$ — *integration in time domain corresponds to division by $j\omega$ in frequency domain*. - **Cannot measure static acceleration:** DC response is blocked by the charge leakage; low-frequency cut-off is set by $\tau = R_f C_f$. - **Standard mounting methods (in increasing frequency range):** Wax / adhesive / magnetic / probe tip / **stud mounting** (best). - **Lab procedure (PCMRL308):** Mount accelerometer on cantilever/shake table → excite with known frequency → read $V_{pp}$ on DSO → compute $q_0 = C_f \cdot V_0$ → compute $a_0 = q_0 / S_q$ → reconstruct $v_0$ and $x_0$ → tabulate and plot $a_0$ vs. $f$ response. - **Common crystals and their typical $d$ values:** Quartz $d \approx 2.3\ \text{pC/N}$ (stable, low sensitivity); PZT $d \approx 200\text{–}500\ \text{pC/N}$ (high sensitivity, temperature sensitive). - **Key examiner keywords to use in answers:** *Seismic mass, Inertial force, Charge sensitivity, Voltage sensitivity, Charge amplifier, Virtual ground, FET-input, Resonant frequency, Useful frequency range, Base strain, Charge leakage, $g$-calibration.* - **Gravitational constant to remember by heart:** $1\ g = 9.80665\ \text{m/s}^2$ — any value not equal to this is a "wrong unit" trap. - **Numerical sanity check:** A typical $5\ g$ vibration at $100\ \text{Hz}$ gives a displacement of about $0.124\ \text{mm}$ — use this to validate computed answers.

topics.title: Measurement of Rotation Speed Using Tachometer, Tacho Generator, and Stroboscopic Tachometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Rotation Speed Using Tachometer, Tacho Generator, and Stroboscopic Tachometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rotational Speed — The Engineering Quantity Under Measurement

**Rotational Speed (N)** is the number of revolutions a rotating shaft completes per unit time, universally expressed in **revolutions per minute (rpm)** or **revolutions per second (rps)**. In the SI framework, the angular equivalent is **angular velocity (ω)** in **rad/s**, related by the conversion factor **2π/60**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Speed of rotation* of a shaft is one of the most frequently measured non-electrical quantities in industry. It is measured using **mechanical tachometers**, **electrical tachogenerators**, and **optical/stroboscopic tachometers** — each exploiting a different physical principle.

## 1.2 The Three Methods — At a Glance

| Method | Energy Domain Exploited | Output Quantity | Contact with Shaft? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Tachometer** | Mechanical (centrifugal force) | Mechanical pointer deflection | Yes (contact type) |
| **Tacho Generator** | Electromechanical (Faraday's Law) | DC/AC voltage | No (coupled) |
| **Stroboscopic Tachometer** | Optical (persistence of vision) | Frequency of flashes | No (optical) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Speedometer" Family

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Three Ways to Time a Swinging Pendulum:**
> 1. **Tachometer (Mechanical)** → Like *counting pendulum swings with a stopwatch and gear train*. Crude but tactile.
> 2. **Tacho Generator (Electrical)** → Like *attaching a tiny dynamo to the pendulum*; the faster it swings, the more voltage it generates. You read the voltmeter.
> 3. **Stroboscopic (Optical)** → Like *shining a flickering light*; when the flicker matches the swing rhythm, the pendulum appears frozen in mid-air. You read the flicker rate.
>
> All three answer the same question — *"How fast is it spinning?"* — but with progressively less physical disturbance to the rotating system.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Units Used

- **Conversion factor:** 1 rps = **60 rpm** = **2π rad/s**
- **Standard unit of rotational speed:** **rpm** (industry), **rad/s** (SI)
- **Tacho generator constant (K):** V/(rad/s) or V/(rpm)
- **Stroboscope calibration:** flashes per second = Hz

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Mechanical (Centrifugal) Tachometer

### Principle
A centrifugal tachometer uses the **centrifugal force** generated on flyweights attached to a shaft. As rotational speed increases, the flyweights swing outward against a spring, and this displacement is converted to a pointer reading on a calibrated dial.

### Components
- **Rotating spindle** coupled to the test shaft
- **Two flyweights** pivoted on links
- **Restoring spring**
- **Gear train** and **revolution counter**
- **Pointer–scale assembly**

### Working Logic
1. Shaft rotation → flyweights experience centrifugal force **F_c = m·r·ω²**
2. Flyweights move outward, compressing the spring
3. Linear displacement is mechanically amplified via levers/gears
4. Pointer deflects on a pre-calibrated scale showing **N (rpm)**
5. Integrated gear train drives a **revolution counter** (for total turns over a timed interval)

### Advantages & Limitations
> [!NOTE]
> **Pros:** Simple, self-contained, no power needed.
> **Cons:** Contact-based → adds loading error; mechanical wear; lower accuracy; not suitable for high speeds (>10,000 rpm).

---

## 2.2 Method 2 — Tacho Generator (DC & AC)

### Principle
A tacho generator is essentially a **small permanent-magnet generator** whose output voltage is **directly proportional to the rotational speed** of its armature. It obeys the fundamental **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Equation:**
> $$E_g = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega \approx K_g \cdot N$$
> where $E_g$ is generated EMF (V), $\phi$ is flux per pole (Wb), $\omega$ is angular velocity (rad/s), and $K_g$ is the generator constant (V/rpm).

### 2.2.1 DC Tacho Generator
- **Construction:** Permanent magnet stator + rotating armature + commutator + brushes
- **Output:** DC voltage, **linear** with speed
- **Polarity:** Indicates **direction** of rotation
- **Ripple:** Commutator introduces small AC ripple (filtered by capacitor across output)

### 2.2.2 AC Tacho Generator (Induction Type)
- **Construction:** Two stator windings (one excited by AC reference, one output) on a ferromagnetic rotor (cup or drag-cup type)
- **Output:** AC voltage at **supply frequency**, with **amplitude proportional to speed**
- **Direction sensing:** Phase reversal of output voltage indicates reversal

### Working Logic (DC Type)
1. Shaft rotates armature in a constant magnetic field $\phi$
2. EMF induced: $E_g = \dfrac{P \cdot \phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$ (P=poles, Z=conductors, A=parallel paths)
3. Voltage read by a calibrated voltmeter → directly indicates **N in rpm**
4. For higher accuracy: VTVM or digital voltmeter across output

### Advantages & Limitations
> [!NOTE]
> **Pros:** No contact loading on shaft, continuous analog output, ideal for **closed-loop speed control** (servo systems), high accuracy (~0.1%).
> **Cons:** Brush wear (DC type), temperature drift of magnets, residual voltage at zero speed (dead zone).

---

## 2.3 Method 3 — Stroboscopic Tachometer

### Principle
A stroboscope produces **calibrated flashes of light** at a known frequency. When the flash frequency equals (or is a submultiple of) the rotational frequency, the rotating object appears **stationary** due to the **persistence of vision** of the human eye (~1/16 s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Equation (Multiple-Blade Target):**
> $$N = \frac{60 \cdot f}{n}$$
> where $N$ is shaft speed in rpm, $f$ is flash frequency in Hz, and $n$ is the number of identical marks/blades on the rotating member.

### Sub-Multiples & Super-Multiples
- If object appears stationary at frequency $f$, the **true speed** could be:
  $$N = \frac{60 \cdot f}{n} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{2 \cdot 60 \cdot f}{n} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{3 \cdot 60 \cdot f}{n} \dots$$
- **Disambiguation trick:** Double the flash frequency. If pattern still appears stationary, you are seeing the fundamental (true speed). If the pattern shifts, you were at a submultiple.

### Working Logic
1. Attach a reference mark (or a multi-blade disc) to the shaft
2. Illuminate the shaft with the stroboscope
3. Vary flash frequency $f$ until the mark **appears stationary**
4. Read $f$ from the calibrated dial
5. Compute $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{n}$

### Advantages & Limitations
> [!NOTE]
> **Pros:** Completely **non-contact**, suitable for very high speeds, can measure inaccessible shafts, no loading effect.
> **Cons:** Ambiguity with sub-multiples, requires a visible reference mark, fluorescent/ambient lighting may interfere.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Method | Governing Formula | Variables & Units | Validity / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugal Force | $F_c = m r \omega^{2}$ | $F_c$ (N), $m$ (kg), $r$ (m), $\omega$ (rad/s) | All mechanical tachometers |
| 2 | Centrifugal Displacement | $x = \dfrac{m r \omega^{2}}{k}$ | $x$ (m), $k$ (spring const., N/m) | Linear approximation |
| 3 | Speed Conversion | $N = \dfrac{60 \, \omega}{2 \pi}$ | $N$ (rpm), $\omega$ (rad/s) | Universal |
| 4 | DC Tacho EMF | $E_g = K_g \cdot N$ | $E_g$ (V), $K_g$ (V/rpm) | Linear region |
| 5 | DC Tacho EMF (full) | $E_g = \dfrac{P \phi Z N}{60 A}$ | $P$ (poles), $\phi$ (Wb), $Z$ (conductors), $A$ (paths) | Exact form |
| 6 | Tacho Constant | $K_g = \dfrac{E_g}{N}$ | V/rpm | Calibration slope |
| 7 | Stroboscope | $N = \dfrac{60 f}{n}$ | $f$ (Hz), $n$ (blades) | Single-image illusion |
| 8 | Wheel/Slot Counter | $N = \dfrac{\text{counts} \times 60}{n_{slot} \cdot t}$ | $t$ (s) | Optical tachometer |
| 9 | Frequency from Photo-sensor | $f_{out} = \dfrac{N \cdot n_{slot}}{60}$ | Hz | Pulse output |
| 10 | Range of Tachometer | $N_{max} = f_{max} \cdot 60 / n$ | rpm | Stroboscope limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $N$ (rpm) with $\omega$ (rad/s) and forget the **60** in the conversion. Always write $N \text{ (rpm)} = \dfrac{60 \, \omega \text{ (rad/s)}}{2\pi}$ on the answer sheet.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Servo & CNC machines:** DC tacho as speed feedback element in closed-loop control.
- **Automotive dashboards:** Tacho generators for analog rpm gauges.
- **Turbine & motor testing:** Stroboscopes for non-contact monitoring of fans, propellers, gear teeth.
- **Industrial quality control:** Photoelectric tachometers for conveyor roller verification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Generated EMF and Speed (DC Tacho Generator)

**Statement to prove:** The EMF generated in a DC tacho generator armature is **linearly proportional to the rotational speed N (rpm)**.

### Step 1 — EMF Induced per Conductor
From Faraday's Law, a single conductor of length $l$ moving with linear velocity $v$ perpendicular to flux density $B$:

$$\varepsilon_{cond} = B \, l \, v$$

### Step 2 — Express Linear Velocity in Terms of Rotor Speed
If the conductor lies on a rotor of diameter $D$ rotating at $N$ rpm:

$$v = \pi D \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$$

### Step 3 — Total Number of Conductors in Series per Parallel Path
For a wave-wound armature: $A = 2$, and conductors per path $= \dfrac{Z}{2}$.
For a lap-wound armature: $A = P$, and conductors per path $= \dfrac{Z}{P}$.

Total EMF for $P$ poles, flux per pole $\phi$, with average flux density $B = \dfrac{P \phi}{\pi D l}$:

$$E_g = \dfrac{P \, \phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A}$$

### Step 4 — Grouping Constants
Define the **generator constant**:

$$K_g = \dfrac{P \, \phi \, Z}{60 \, A} \quad \text{(V/rpm)}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{E_g = K_g \cdot N}$$

### Step 5 — Solving for Speed (Practical Use in Lab)
If voltmeter reads $V$ across the tacho output (assuming no-load or compensated loading):

$$N = \dfrac{V}{K_g} \quad \text{(rpm)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (Lab-Style):**
> Given $P = 4$, $\phi = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb, $Z = 500$ (wave wound, $A = 2$), find $K_g$:
> $$K_g = \dfrac{4 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 500}{60 \times 2} = \dfrac{10}{120} = 0.0833 \text{ V/rpm}$$
> If voltmeter reads **5.0 V**, then:
> $$N = \dfrac{5.0}{0.0833} \approx 60 \text{ rpm}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stroboscopic Speed Equation

**Statement to prove:** A rotating object with $n$ identical marks appears stationary when the stroboscope flashes at frequency $f = \dfrac{n N}{60}$.

### Step 1 — Angular Position After One Flash Interval
Let the shaft rotate at $N$ rpm → angular velocity:

$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \text{ rad/s}$$

Time between two successive flashes at frequency $f$:

$$T = \dfrac{1}{f} \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 2 — Angle Rotated in Time T
$$\theta = \omega \cdot T = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \cdot \dfrac{1}{f}$$

### Step 3 — Condition for Stationary Appearance
The next identical mark must occupy the position the previous mark vacated. With $n$ marks equally spaced, the angular spacing between adjacent marks is:

$$\Delta\theta = \dfrac{2 \pi}{n}$$

For the image to appear stationary:

$$\theta = \Delta\theta = \dfrac{2 \pi}{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60 f} = \dfrac{2 \pi}{n}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for f
$$f = \dfrac{n N}{60} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

### Step 5 — Invert to Find N from Observed f
$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{60 f}{n} \text{ rpm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization for sub-multiples:**
> Stationary appearance is also observed at $f = \dfrac{m \cdot n N}{60}$ for $m = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$
> The **lowest** such $f$ that produces a stationary image gives the **true** speed. Higher-order stationary patterns occur at integer multiples.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Centrifugal Tachometer Deflection

### Step 1 — Centrifugal Force on a Flyweight
$$F_c = m r \omega^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Spring Deflection
Equating to spring force $F_s = k x$:

$$k x = m r \omega^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \dfrac{m r}{k} \omega^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to rpm
Using $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$:

$$x = \dfrac{m r}{k} \cdot \left( \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \right)^{2} = \underbrace{\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} m r}{3600 \, k}}_{K_{m}} \cdot N^{2}$$

$$\boxed{x = K_{m} \cdot N^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The deflection is **quadratic** in speed. Therefore, the dial scale is **non-linear** — compressed at low speeds, expanded at high speeds. This non-linearity is a major reason contact tachometers are being replaced by electrical/optical methods in modern labs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Mechanical (Centrifugal) | DC Tacho Generator | Stroboscopic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Output Signal** | Mechanical pointer / counter | DC Voltage $E_g$ | Flash frequency $f$ |
| **Speed Range** | 30 – 10,000 rpm | 0 – 10,000 rpm (typical) | 100 – 100,000+ rpm |
| **Accuracy** | ±2% | ±0.1% to ±0.5% | ±0.05% (calibration dependent) |
| **Contact?** | Yes (loading error) | No (coupled, not loaded) | No (optical) |
| **Direction Info?** | No (magnitude only) | Yes (voltage polarity) | No (frozen image) |
| **Display** | Pointer dial | Voltmeter (calibrated) | Frequency dial |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium-High |
| **Application** | Hand-held field use | Servo feedback loops | Lab, high-speed testing |
| **Main Error Source** | Mechanical wear, friction | Brush drop, temp drift, ripple | Sub-multiple ambiguity, ambient light |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
