# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Examination Mark Distribution Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU 2024 Scheme lab course pattern, the **End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** of PCMRL308 carries **100 marks**, of which the **viva voce + record + written theory** component is approximately **40 marks**. The questions below are designed to mimic the KTU past-year style and weightage.

---

## 5.2 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Least Count. Calculate the least count of a vernier caliper with 1 mm main scale divisions and 20 vernier scale divisions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Least count is the smallest dimension that can be measured accurately on the instrument scale. It is also called the resolution of the instrument.

**Calculation (2 Marks):**
Given: MSD = 1 mm, n = 20

$$
LC = \frac{MSD}{n} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer: 0.05 mm**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the principle of a micrometer? Why is the ratchet stop used instead of the thimble for final adjustment?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Principle (1.5 Marks):**
The micrometer works on the **precision screw and nut principle**. The spindle moves by the **pitch (0.5 mm)** of the thread per complete rotation. The circular thimble scale with 50 divisions converts this rotation into a linear reading of **0.01 mm per division**.

**Ratchet Stop Reason (1.5 Marks):**
The ratchet stop provides a **uniform measuring pressure** (approximately 5–10 N) on the workpiece, preventing **over-tightening**, which would cause:
- **Spindle wear**
- **Workpiece deformation**
- **Inconsistent readings between operators**

The ratchet **slips** once the correct pressure is reached, ensuring **repeatability** and **accuracy**.

---

## 5.3 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option A
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PCMRL308]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a vernier caliper**. State its least count and the types of measurements it can perform.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following readings were taken on a vernier caliper (LC = 0.02 mm):
MSR = 45 mm, VC = 18, Zero error = −0.04 mm.
Calculate the **corrected reading**. Also, list **any four** common **sources of error** in vernier caliper measurements.

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**Construction (3 Marks):**
A vernier caliper consists of:
- A **main scale** graduated in mm (typically 0–150 mm or 0–200 mm range).
- A **movable jaw** carrying the **vernier scale** (with 50 divisions for 0.02 mm LC).
- A **fixed jaw** mounted on one end of the beam.
- A **depth rod** sliding at the right end.
- **Locking screws** to fix the movable jaw.
- **Knife edges** for internal measurements.

**Working (3 Marks):**
The vernier scale **slides** along the main scale. The zero of the vernier acts as the **pointer**. The difference between **one MSD (1 mm)** and **one VSD (0.98 mm)** gives the least count of **0.02 mm**. The instrument can measure:
- **Outside dimensions** (OD, length, width)
- **Inside dimensions** (ID, slot width)
- **Depths** (of holes, slots)
- **Steps and shoulders**

**Least Count (1 Mark):**

$$
LC = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{50} = 0.02 \text{ mm}
$$

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** MSR = 45 mm, VC = 18, LC = 0.02 mm, Zero error = −0.04 mm

**Step 1 — Observed Reading (2 Marks):**

$$
R_{obs} = 45 + (18 \times 0.02) = 45 + 0.36 = 45.36 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Correction (2 Marks):**
Since the zero error is **negative**, the instrument reads *less* than actual. We **add** the absolute value:

$$
R_{corrected} = 45.36 + 0.04 = 45.40 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Four Sources of Error (3 Marks):**

| # | Source of Error | Effect |
|---|----------------|--------|
| 1 | **Parallax error** while reading scales | Incorrect MSR or VC |
| 2 | **Zero error** not checked before use | Systematic offset in all readings |
| 3 | **Excessive jaw pressure** on workpiece | Workpiece deformation; reading too small |
| 4 | **Dirt / oil on jaws** | Foreign material adds to reading |
| 5 | **Worn or damaged jaws** | Loose fit, inconsistent contact |

**Final Answer: Corrected reading = 45.40 mm**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option B (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle, construction, and least count of an outside micrometer**. Why is the spindle thread pitch chosen as 0.5 mm and not 1 mm?

**(b) [7 Marks]** An outside micrometer (LC = 0.01 mm) shows a main scale reading of **18 mm** (with the 0.5 mm mark visible below the datum), a thimble reading of **42**, and a zero error of **+0.03 mm**. Compute the **actual diameter** of the workpiece.

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**Principle (2 Marks):**
The outside micrometer works on the **uniform screw principle**. When the thimble rotates by one full turn, the spindle advances by a distance equal to the **pitch of the thread (0.5 mm)**. The 50 divisions on the thimble thus allow a reading of **0.01 mm** per division.

**Construction (3 Marks):**
Main parts:
- **C-shaped frame** (provides rigidity and acts as a heat sink from the hand).
- **Anvil** (fixed face) and **Spindle** (moving face).
- **Sleeve / Barrel** with linear scale (mm and 0.5 mm marks).
- **Thimble** with 50 circular divisions.
- **Ratchet stop** and **lock nut**.

**Least Count and Pitch Choice (2 Marks):**

$$
LC = \frac{0.5}{50} = 0.01 \text{ mm}
$$

The pitch is chosen as **0.5 mm (and not 1 mm)** so that:
- The thimble can be divided into **50 readable divisions** (giving 0.01 mm).
- If 1 mm pitch were used, dividing into 50 parts would give 0.02 mm, which is coarser — defeating the purpose of a precision instrument.
- A **0.5 mm pitch** also keeps the thimble diameter **practically small** (typically 14–18 mm) while providing high resolution.

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** MSR = 18 mm + 0.5 mm = **18.5 mm**, HSR = 42, LC = 0.01 mm, Zero error = +0.03 mm

**Step 1 — Thimble Contribution (2 Marks):**

$$
HSR \times LC = 42 \times 0.01 = 0.42 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Observed Reading (2 Marks):**

$$
R_{obs} = 18.5 + 0.42 = 18.92 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Zero Error Correction (2 Marks):**
Zero error is **positive**, so the instrument reads *more* than actual. We **subtract**:

$$
R_{actual} = 18.92 - 0.03 = 18.89 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Statement (1 Mark):**

The actual diameter of the workpiece is **18.89 mm**.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.5 mm mark on the micrometer sleeve.** Many students read only "18 mm" and miss the lower scale showing 0.5 mm, leading to a **loss of 2–3 marks**. Always check **both** the upper (1 mm) and lower (0.5 mm) marks on the sleeve.
>
> 2. **Wrong sign convention for zero error.** A common mistake: students "add" a positive zero error or "subtract" a negative one. The correct rule is:
>    - **Positive zero error → Subtract from observed reading**
>    - **Negative zero error → Add to observed reading**
>
> 3. **Forgetting to draw a neat labeled diagram.** KTU lab records and theory papers **mandate** a labeled diagram carrying at least 6 parts. Missing this costs **1–2 marks** in viva and theory.
>
> 4. **Mixing up units.** Some students write "0.02 cm" instead of "0.02 mm" for least count. The metric standard is **always in mm**.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning the ratchet stop's role.** Examiners in viva specifically ask why the ratchet is used. A vague answer like "for tightening" loses marks; the correct answer involves **uniform pressure and avoiding over-tightening**.
>
> 6. **Using the thimble instead of the ratchet for final approach.** This is a **practical error**; if demonstrated in lab, the marks for "proper procedure" will be deducted.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- **Vernier Caliper** — LC = **0.02 mm** (1 mm / 50 divisions). Measures OD, ID, and depth.
- **Outside Micrometer** — LC = **0.01 mm** (0.5 mm pitch / 50 thimble divisions). Measures **external** dimensions only.
- **Inside Micrometer** — LC = **0.01 mm**; uses **extension rods** (25, 50, 75, 100 mm) for **internal** dimensions.
- **Vernier Depth Gauge** — LC = **0.02 mm**; measures **depths of holes and slots**.
- **Vernier Height Gauge** — LC = **0.02 mm**; measures **heights** and is used for **scribing and layout** on a surface plate.
- **Universal Formula for Total Reading (Vernier):**

$$
R = MSR + (VC \times LC)
$$

- **Universal Formula for Total Reading (Micrometer):**

$$
R = MSR + (HSR \times LC) - (\text{zero error with sign correction})
$$

- **Zero Error Rule:** Always check the zero **with the jaws closed (vernier)** or **anvil-spindle touching (micrometer)** *before* taking any reading.
- **Standard Reference Temperature:** **20 °C** is the international standard for all linear measurements.
- **Ratchet Stop Rule:** Never use the thimble directly for final clamping on a micrometer; always use the ratchet for **uniform pressure**.
- **SI Unit:** All linear measuring instruments in the KTU syllabus are graduated in **millimetres (mm)**.
- **Range vs. Resolution:** A vernier caliper has **larger range** (0–300 mm) but **coarser resolution**; a micrometer has **smaller range** (0–25 mm per head) but **finer resolution** (0.01 mm).
- **Thermal Expansion:** Steel expands by **11.5 × 10⁻⁶ / °C** — for high-precision work, both instrument and workpiece must be at 20 °C.
- **Calibration:** Vernier calipers are calibrated using **slip gauges** or **caliper checker**; micrometers using **optical flats or setting standards**.
- **Inside Micrometer Rule:** Sum of the **extension rod length + head reading** gives the **internal diameter** of the bore.
- **Vernier Height Gauge Rule:** The base must rest on a **flat surface plate** — never on a regular table, as the datum will be incorrect.
- **Always hold the micrometer by the **insulated frame** to prevent hand heat from warming the spindle and causing reading errors (thermal expansion of the frame by just 1 °C in 25 mm causes ≈ 0.0003 mm error, which is significant for sub-micron work).**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and Use of Angular Measuring Devices: Sine Bar, Clinometer Angle Gauge, Bevel Protractor, Profile Projector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Use of Angular Measuring Devices: Sine Bar, Clinometer Angle Gauge, Bevel Protractor, Profile Projector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Angular Measurement in Engineering Metrology

**Angular measurement** is the process of determining the angular orientation or angular deviation of a workpiece surface, feature, or axis with respect to a reference datum. In KTU 2024 Scheme Metrology & Instrumentation Lab (PCMRL308), angular measurement is treated as a foundational sub-discipline of **Dimensional Metrology**, sitting alongside linear and geometric form measurements. The four standard laboratory instruments used to perform angular measurement in the KTU curriculum are the **Sine Bar**, **Clinometer Angle Gauge**, **Bevel Protractor**, and **Profile Projector** — each occupying a distinct precision tier.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *Angular measuring devices are precision instruments used to set or measure the included angle between two surfaces of a workpiece, using either mechanical, trigonometric, or optical magnification principles. The selection of an instrument depends on the order of accuracy required (typically 1° to 1' arc) and the size of the workpiece.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Incline and the Wedge"**
> Imagine you are trying to measure the tilt of a ramp by placing a marble on it. A **Bevel Protractor** is like a physical protractor you would hold against the ramp — quick, but the reading depends on how well your eye aligns the lines. A **Sine Bar** is like raising one end of the ramp with a stack of books until a marble barely rolls — by knowing the **length of the ramp** and the **height of the books**, trigonometry (sin θ = height / length) gives the exact tilt. A **Clinometer** is the spirit-level-with-a-dial cousin used in civil engineering on slopes. A **Profile Projector** is like a movie projector — it shines light *through* a thin silhouette of the part and throws an enlarged outline on a screen, where the outline's angle can be measured against a protractor drawn on the screen.

## 1.3 Classification of the Four Devices (KTU Board Tabular Snapshot)

| # | Instrument | Operating Principle | Typical Accuracy | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sine Bar** | Trigonometric (sin θ = h / L) | ± 1' to ± 5' | Setting/measuring tapers, angles of small components |
| 2 | **Clinometer Angle Gauge** | Gravity-referenced bubble/dial | ± 10' to ± 1° | Slope of large castings, machine beds, ship hulls |
| 3 | **Bevel Protractor** | Mechanical Vernier scale | **5' (five minutes)** | Workshop inspection of bevels, chamfers, drill points |
| 4 | **Profile Projector** | Optical magnification (shadowgraph) | ± 0.01 mm at 10× | Complex 2-D profiles, gears, threads, press tools |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The **Profile Projector is non-contact** (it uses light projection) whereas the **Sine Bar, Clinometer, and Bevel Protractor are contact-type** instruments. This is a frequently-asked 3-mark KTU question.

---

## 1.4 Significance in KTU 2024 Scheme Outcomes

The KTU 2024 Course Outcome (CO) mapping for this experiment typically reads as:

- **CO2 (Apply):** Apply angular measurement devices to determine unknown angles of given workpieces.
- **CO3 (Analyze):** Compare the precision, range, and applicability of sine bar, bevel protractor, and profile projector.
- **CO4 (Instrumentation Practice):** Demonstrate the correct procedure of setting a known angle using slip gauges and verifying the same with a bevel protractor.

This experiment is therefore both **procedural** (you must perform the steps in the lab) and **analytical** (you must justify which instrument to choose for a given tolerance).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SINE BAR — Working Principle

A **sine bar** is a hardened, precision-ground steel bar with two identical cylindrical rollers of equal diameter (typically **∅ 20 mm** in Indian labs) rigidly mounted on its top surface. The **center-to-center distance (L)** between the two rollers is fixed and is the most critical dimensional parameter of the bar.

**Standard KTU-Lab Sine Bar Lengths:** L = **100 mm**, **200 mm**, or **300 mm**.

### 2.1.1 The Geometry of the Sine Bar

When a sine bar is placed on a flat datum surface (surface plate) and one roller is elevated by a stack of **slip gauges** (also called **Johansson gauges**) of total height **h**, the bar tilts through an angle **θ** with respect to the surface plate. The geometry of the right-triangle formed yields:

$$\sin(\theta) = \frac{h}{L}$$

$$h = L \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{L}\right)$$

The workpiece (whose angle is to be set or measured) is clamped to the sine bar using a work-holding strap. The **upper surface of the workpiece must become parallel to the surface plate** — this is the condition of "perfect setting," verified by a sensitive **dial indicator or spirit level**.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. Identify the **nominal angle θ** of the workpiece.
2. Compute the **required slip-gauge height** h = L × sin θ.
3. Build a slip-gauge stack of exactly height h.
4. Place the stack under the *elevated* roller.
5. Clamp the workpiece onto the sine bar.
6. Verify parallelism between the workpiece top and the surface plate using a **dial indicator** (run-out ≤ **0.002 mm** is desirable).
7. To **measure** an unknown angle, iterate: try values of h until parallelism is achieved, then compute θ = sin⁻¹ (h / L).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two rollers and not three?** Two rollers fix the bar in a unique tilted plane (2 contact points define a line, the 3rd reference being the work surface). A third roller would over-constrain the geometry and introduce stacking error.

## 2.2 The CLINOMETER ANGLE GAUGE

A **Clinometer** is a portable instrument that determines angles by referencing the **direction of gravity**. It is essentially a precision **bubble level** coupled to a **graduated dial or digital encoder**. As the instrument is tilted, the bubble (or pendulum mass) shifts, and this displacement is converted into an angular reading.

### 2.2.1 Principle
- A sealed **spirit vial** (curved glass tube partially filled with a low-viscosity liquid such as **ethyl alcohol or diethyl phthalate**) is mounted on a pivoted arm.
- As the base of the clinometer is laid on the inclined surface, gravity keeps the bubble (or pendulum) vertical while the body of the instrument tilts.
- The angular displacement is read on the **graduated dial** through a gear train, or displayed on a **digital LCD**.

### 2.2.2 Range and Resolution
- Typical range: **0° to 90°** in two quadrants, or **± 90°** from horizontal.
- Resolution: **10 arc-minutes** (mechanical type) or **0.1°** (digital type).

### 2.2.3 Engineering Application
Clinometers are widely used to check:
- The **flatness and twist** of large machine tool beds.
- The **slope** of ship hulls and rail tracks.
- The **plumb** of structural columns and the **level** of pipelines.

## 2.3 The BEVEL PROTRACTOR (Universal Vernier Bevel Protractor)

The **Bevel Protractor** is the most widely used workshop instrument for direct angular measurement. It consists of:

1. A **circular main scale** (graduated 0°–90° in four quadrants, 0°–360° total).
2. A **vernier scale** (the KTU-laboratory version has 12 divisions spanning 11° — see derivation in §3).
3. A **movable blade** carrying the vernier.
4. A **fixed stock** (datum) and **adjustable blade**.
5. A **clamping lever** to lock the reading.
6. An **acute-angle attachment** for measuring angles > 90° and up to 360°.

### 2.3.1 Reading Procedure (KTU Standard)
1. Note the **main-scale reading** in degrees (the last full degree passed by the vernier zero).
2. Slide along the vernier scale until a vernier line perfectly coincides with a main-scale line.
3. Multiply the coinciding vernier line number by the **least count (LC)**.
4. **Total reading** = Main scale reading + (Vernier coincidence × LC).

### 2.3.2 Least Count Derivation (Universal Bevel Protractor)
- Main scale smallest division = **1° = 60'**.
- Vernier scale: 12 divisions occupy 11° (i.e., 11 main-scale divisions).
- Therefore, 1 vernier division = **11° / 12** = 55 minutes of arc.
- Least Count = 1 main-scale division − 1 vernier-scale division = 60' − 55' = **5' (five minutes of arc)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** Always state the LC of the instrument **before** quoting a reading. A reading of *"23°30'"* without stating the LC of the bevel protractor loses one full mark.

## 2.4 The PROFILE PROJECTOR (Optical Comparator / Shadowgraph)

The **Profile Projector** is a non-contact, optical-measurement instrument that projects a **magnified silhouette (shadow)** of the workpiece onto a ground-glass screen. The magnified image is then measured against a pre-drawn scale or template on the screen.

### 2.4.1 Optical Components (in order of light travel)

1. **Light Source** — halogen or mercury-vapor lamp, 100 W typical.
2. **Condenser Lens** — collimates the light into a parallel beam.
3. **Objective Lens** — forms the magnified image at its focal plane.
4. **Projection Screen** — ground-glass screen with protractor scale and digital readout overlay.
5. **Work Stage** — X–Y table with **micrometers** (0.005 mm LC) for precise positioning.
6. **Lens Turret** — interchangeable objectives giving discrete magnifications.

### 2.4.2 Standard Magnifications
The most common magnifications offered on a KTU-lab profile projector are:

- **10×** (1 inch / 25.4 mm focal-length objective)
- **20×** (½ inch / 12.7 mm)
- **50×** (¼ inch / 6.35 mm)
- **100×** (1/10 inch / 2.54 mm)

### 2.4.3 Magnification Equation

$$M = \frac{\text{Image size on screen}}{\text{Actual size of workpiece}} = \frac{v}{u}$$

where **v** = distance from objective to screen, **u** = distance from objective to workpiece. For a fixed optical bench, **M is a fixed design constant** for each objective (10, 20, 50, 100).

### 2.4.4 Effective Screen Measurement
If a dimension *D* appears on the screen as *D′*, the actual dimension is:

$$D = \frac{D'}{M}$$

### 2.4.5 Engineering Utility
Profile projectors are essential for inspecting:
- **Gears** (tooth profile, pressure angle, addendum, dedendum).
- **Screw threads** (form, pitch, crest, root).
- **Press tools, punches, and dies**.
- **Cams, contours, and 2-D outlines** that are difficult to access with a vernier.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you will need for KTU 2024 University Examination questions on this experiment. Memorize the symbols, not just the equations.

| # | Instrument | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Typical Numerical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sine Bar — Setting | $h = L \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | h = slip-gauge height (mm), L = roller center distance (mm), θ = angle (°) | h ≤ L (sin θ ≤ 1) |
| 2 | Sine Bar — Measurement | $\theta = \sin^{-1}(h \div L)$ | h = trial slip-gauge height, L = bar length | θ ∈ [0°, 90°] |
| 3 | Sine Bar — Slip-gauge stack (compound) | $h = h_1 + h_2 + \dots$ | Slip gauges wrung in series | h accuracy ±0.001 mm |
| 4 | Clinometer | $\theta = \tan^{-1}(\Delta H \div L_b)$ | ΔH = bubble displacement, $L_b$ = vial radius | Resolution 0.1° to 10' |
| 5 | Bevel Protractor LC | $\text{LC} = \text{MSD} - \text{VSD} = 1° - \tfrac{11°}{12} = 5'$ | MSD = 60', VSD = 55' | **LC = 5'** |
| 6 | Bevel Protractor Reading | $R = \text{MSR} + (n \times \text{LC})$ | n = vernier line coinciding, MSR = main scale reading | Range 0° to 360° |
| 7 | Profile Projector — Mag. | $M = \dfrac{\text{Image size on screen}}{\text{Object size}}$ | M = 10, 20, 50, 100 | Image size = M × Object size |
| 8 | Profile Projector — Dimension | $D = \dfrac{D'}{M}$ | D = true size, D' = screen measurement | mm / inch |
| 9 | Profile Projector — Angle (from screen protractor) | $\theta = \dfrac{\theta_{\text{screen}}}{M}$ *for tangential length* OR measured directly on screen goniometer | θ unchanged by M (angles are scale-invariant) | 0° to 360° |
| 10 | Slip-Gauge Wringing | Wringing builds additively with no gap | Steel gauge blocks | Grade K = ±0.0002 mm |

### 2.5.1 Real-World Utility of the Sine Bar Equation

The equation **h = L sin θ** is the same trigonometric identity that governs the resolution of **incline lifts, ramp gradients in highway engineering (1 in 20 gradients), and the micrometer screw on a sine table**. In metrology, the sine bar converts a *linear* slip-gauge measurement into an *angular* setting — exploiting the fact that slip gauges (linear) are far easier to make accurately than graduated circles (angular).

### 2.5.2 Why Profile Projectors are Non-Contact — Engineering Reason

Instruments that touch the workpiece (sine bar, bevel protractor) suffer from **stylus pressure error** and **probe-geometry error** (e.g., a 0.5 mm tip cannot enter a 0.4 mm slot). Profile projectors overcome both limitations by shining light *through or around* the part, making them indispensable for inspecting **delicate, soft, or intricately contoured components** such as small injection-moulded plastic gears.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Sine-Bar Trigonometric Identity

**Statement to prove:**

> When a sine bar of roller center-distance L is elevated at one end by a slip-gauge stack of height h, the bar's tilt angle θ measured from the surface plate satisfies sin θ = h / L.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Construction of the Right Triangle

Consider a sine bar resting on a perfectly flat **surface plate** (a precision granite or cast-iron reference). The bar has two identical cylindrical rollers of equal diameter, mounted with their centers separated by a fixed distance **L**. One roller (the left one) sits directly on the surface plate. The other roller (the right one) is raised by a stack of slip gauges of total height **h**, measured from the surface plate to the **bottom** of the right roller.

Because both rollers are identical cylinders rolling on two flat planes (the surface plate and the top of the slip-gauge stack), the line joining the two roller **centers** is a straight line. The bar's top datum edge is parallel to this centerline (by design of the instrument).

### 3.1.2 Constructing the Right Triangle

Drop a vertical line from the right roller center to the horizontal plane passing through the left roller center. The resulting figure is a right-angled triangle with:

- **Hypotenuse** = the line joining the two roller centers, of length **L** (the sine bar's center distance).
- **Opposite side** (to angle θ) = the vertical rise of the right roller center above the left roller center. This vertical rise is **exactly equal to h**, because the right roller is a cylinder of diameter d, and its center sits at a height of (h + d/2) above the surface plate, while the left roller center is at a height of d/2 above the surface plate. Subtracting: rise = (h + d/2) − d/2 = **h**.
- **Adjacent side** = horizontal projection of the centerline.
- **Angle** at the left roller center, between the centerline and the horizontal = **θ**, the tilt of the sine bar.

### 3.1.3 Applying the Definition of Sine

By definition of the sine function in a right triangle:

$$\sin(\theta) = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{h}{L}$$

Multiplying both sides by L:

$$h = L \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

Solving for the angle:

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{L}\right)$$

This completes the derivation. **Q.E.D.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **What is "L" physically?** L is the *center-to-center* distance of the two rollers, NOT the overall length of the sine bar. For a 200 mm sine bar, L is stamped on the bar and is exactly **200 mm** (the overall length and center-distance coincide by design).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Least Count of the Universal Bevel Protractor

**Given data (from the KTU-lab instrument):**
- Main scale smallest division (MSD) = **1° = 60' (minutes of arc)**.
- Vernier scale has **12 vernier divisions** (VSD) that span **11 main-scale divisions** = 11°.

**Step 1 — Value of one vernier division:**

$$\text{VSD} = \frac{11°}{12} = \frac{11 \times 60'}{12} = \frac{660'}{12} = 55'$$

**Step 2 — Definition of least count:**

$$\text{LC} = \text{MSD} - \text{VSD}$$

$$\text{LC} = 60' - 55' = 5'$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The smallest angular increment that the bevel protractor can resolve is **5 minutes of arc** (5'). Since 1° = 60', 5' = **5/60 of a degree = 0.0833°**.

**Step 4 — Sanity check using uncertainty principle:**

> A KTU-lab bevel protractor with 5' LC corresponds to a *linear* resolution at the workpiece of approximately 1 µm per mm of blade length. For a 150 mm blade, this gives ~0.15 mm — adequate for workshop inspection but inferior to a sine bar (which is limited only by slip-gauge accuracy, ~0.001 mm).

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example 1 — Sine Bar Setting

**Problem:**
A KTU-lab **200 mm sine bar** is to be used to set a workpiece to an angle of **23° 18'**. Calculate the slip-gauge stack height h to be built.

**Solution:**

$$\theta = 23° 18' = 23 + \tfrac{18}{60} = 23.300°$$

Apply the sine-bar equation:

$$h = L \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

$$h = 200 \cdot \sin(23.300°)$$

Using a standard scientific calculator:

$$\sin(23.300°) = 0.39551$$

Therefore:

$$h = 200 \times 0.39551 = 79.102 \text{ mm}$$

**Slip-gauge selection (using a 103-piece KTU-lab set):**
A typical wrung stack to realize 79.102 mm is:

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= 1.002 \text{ mm} \\
  &\quad + 1.100 \text{ mm} \\
  &\quad + 7.000 \text{ mm} \\
  &\quad + 70.000 \text{ mm} \\
  &\quad = 79.102 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

(Students must always include a **wringing/wear block** of size 1.002 mm or use a wrung combination that is unique to the set in hand.)

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example 2 — Sine Bar Measurement of Unknown Angle

**Problem:**
An unknown angle of a beveled workpiece is measured with a **300 mm sine bar**. After trial-and-error, the slip-gauge stack that makes the top surface perfectly parallel to the surface plate (as verified by a dial indicator) measures **h = 47.85 mm**. Calculate the unknown angle.

**Solution:**

Apply the inverse sine-bar equation:

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{L}\right)$$

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{47.85}{300}\right)$$

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.1595)$$

$$\theta = 9.176°$$

Converting the decimal to degrees-minutes:

$$0.176° \times 60 = 10.56' \approx 11'$$

Therefore:

$$\theta \approx 9° 11'$$

**Verification by alternative formula:**

$$h = L \cdot \sin(\theta) = 300 \cdot \sin(9.176°) = 300 \times 0.15950 = 47.85 \text{ mm} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Example 3 — Bevel Protractor Reading

**Problem:**
On a universal bevel protractor (LC = 5'), the vernier zero is found past the **32°** mark on the main scale, and the **7th** vernier line coincides with a main-scale line. Calculate the angle.

**Solution:**

Apply the reading equation:

$$R = \text{MSR} + (n \times \text{LC})$$

$$R = 32° + (7 \times 5')$$

$$R = 32° + 35'$$

$$R = 32° 35'$$

Decimal conversion:

$$R = 32 + \tfrac{35}{60} = 32.583°$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Analytical Example 4 — Profile Projector Dimension

**Problem:**
A gear-tooth profile is projected on a profile-projector screen at a magnification of **50×**. The image of the tooth addendum on the screen measures **62.5 mm**. Calculate the actual addendum height of the gear tooth.

**Solution:**

Apply the dimension equation:

$$D = \frac{D'}{M}$$

$$D = \frac{62.5 \text{ mm}}{50}$$

$$D = 1.25 \text{ mm}$$

This is a typical addendum for a fine-pitch module-0.5 involute gear.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board "Compare-and-Contrast" Tabular)

The following matrix is the single most important analytical tool for KTU 14-mark questions asking *"Compare sine bar, bevel protractor, and profile projector."* You are strongly advised to reproduce it (with a sketch) in the exam hall.

| Parameter | Sine Bar | Clinometer Angle Gauge | Bevel Protractor | Profile Projector |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating Principle** | Trigonometric (sine rule) | Gravity (bubble / pendulum) | Mechanical vernier | Optical magnification |
| **Contact vs. Non-contact** | Contact | Contact | Contact | Non-contact |
| **Range** | 0° to 60° (practical) | 0° to 90° (or ± 90°) | 0° to 360° | Unlimited (image is projected) |
| **Least Count / Resolution** | Limited by slip gauges (~0.001 mm ≈ 1' for L = 200) | 10' (mech.) / 0.1° (digital) | **5'** | ± 0.01 mm at 10× |
| **Accuracy Tier** | High (workshop grade) | Low (field grade) | Medium | Very High |
| **Workpiece Size** | Small to medium | Large (castings, beds) | Small to medium | Small (must fit on stage) |
| **Typical KTU Use** | Setting/measuring known angles of jigs | Checking machine-bed twist | Quick inspection of chamfers | Inspecting gear & thread profiles |
| **Limitation** | Slip-gauge stack cumbersome | Affected by local gravity anomalies | Operator-skill dependent | Limited to 2-D profile, 2-D shadow |
| **Cost (Relative)** | ₹ 5 k – 20 k | ₹ 1 k – 5 k | ₹ 3 k – 10 k | ₹ 2 lakh – 10 lakh |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note on Bevel Protractor LC:** Some instruments in the market have **40 vernier divisions spanning 39°** — these give **LC = 1' (one minute)**. Always check the vernier plate on the instrument before quoting the LC in your exam answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid block diagrams** map the *functional architecture* and *optical light-path* of each instrument. They are designed to be **textbook-quality** and 100% safe to render in any Mermaid-compatible viewer (no reserved keywords, no markdown inside labels).

## 4.1 Sine Bar — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph dat[REFERENCE PLANE]
        SP[Surface Plate - Grade 0 Granite]
    end

    subgraph sb[SINE BAR ASSEMBLY]
        R1[Roller 1 - Cylinder Ø 20 mm]
        SG[Slip Gauge Stack - Height h]
        R2[Roller 2 - Cylinder Ø 20 mm]
        BD[Bar Datum Edge - Parallel to Roller Centers]
        CL[Workpiece Clamp Strap]
        WP[Workpiece - Angle θ to be Set]
    end

    subgraph ver[VERIFICATION SUBSYSTEM]
        DI[Dial Indicator on Stand]
        LV[Spirit Level - Optional]
    end

    SP --- R1
    SG --- R2
    R1 --- BD
    R2 --- BD
    BD --- WP
    CL --- WP
    DI --- WP
    LV --- WP
```

## 4.2 Bevel Protractor — Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph base[STATIC FRAME]
        STK[Fixed Stock - Datum Reference]
        MS[Main Scale - 0 to 90 deg, 4 Quadrants]
        AC[Acute-Angle Attachment - For Angles over 90 deg]
    end

    subgraph mov[MOVING ASSEMBLY]
        MB[Movable Blade]
        VS[Vernier Scale - 12 divisions, 11 deg span]
        CL[Clamping Lever]
        ADJ[Fine Adjustment Screw]
    end

    subgraph aux[AUXILIARY]
        PRO[Projector Lamp - Built-in]
        MAG[Magnifier Eyepiece - 5x]
    end

    STK --- MS
    MS --- VS
    MB --- VS
    MB --- ADJ
    VS --- MAG
    STK --- AC
    AC --- MB
    CL --- ADJ
    PRO --- MS
```

## 4.3 Profile Projector — Optical Light-Path Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph src[ILLUMINATION SUBSYSTEM]
        LS[Light Source - Halogen 100W]
        COND[Condenser Lens - Collimates Beam]
        FIL[Heat-Absorbing Filter]
    end

    subgraph stage[WORK STAGE]
        WSTG[X-Y Translation Table with Micrometers 0.005 mm LC]
        WPC[Workpiece - 2D Profile on Glass]
    end

    subgraph optics[MAGNIFICATION SUBSYSTEM]
        TUR[Lens Turret - 4 Objectives]
        OBJ1[10x Objective]
        OBJ2[20x Objective]
        OBJ3[50x Objective]
        OBJ4[100x Objective]
    end

    subgraph scr[VIEWING AND MEASUREMENT SUBSYSTEM]
        PRJ[Projection Screen - Ground Glass 300 mm Dia]
        PROT[Screen Protractor - 0 to 360 deg]
        DR[Digital Readout DRO - 0.001 mm]
    end

    LS --> COND --> FIL --> WSTG
    WSTG --> WPC --> TUR
    TUR --> OBJ1
    TUR --> OBJ2
    TUR --> OBJ3
    TUR --> OBJ4
    OBJ1 --> PRJ
    OBJ2 --> PRJ
    OBJ3 --> PRJ
    OBJ4 --> PRJ
    PRJ --> PROT
    PRJ --> DR
```

## 4.4 Clinometer Angle Gauge — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph basec[BASE ASSEMBLY]
        BPL[Base Plate - Magnetic or V-Groove]
        REF[Reference Datum Face]
    end

    subgraph pivotc[PIVOT AND LEVEL]
        PVT[Pivot Bearing - Low-Friction]
        BVL[Spirit Vial - Curved Glass Tube]
        PDM[Pendulum Mass - Gravity Reference]
    end

    subgraph readc[READING SUBSYSTEM]
        DIAL[Graduated Dial - 0 to 90 deg, 2 Sectors]
        GTR[Gear Train - Amplification]
        IND[Pointer or Digital LCD]
    end

    BPL --- REF
    REF --- PVT
    PVT --- DIAL
    BVL --- PVT
    PDM --- PVT
    PVT --- GTR
    GTR --- DIAL
    DIAL --- IND
```

## 4.5 Comparative Operational Flow — Setting an Angle on a Sine Bar

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start - Identify Target Angle theta] --> B[Compute h equals L times sin theta]
    B --> C[Select Slip Gauges from Set]
    C --> D[Wrung Stack on Surface Plate Under Roller 2]
    D --> E[Place Sine Bar with Roller 1 on Plate and Roller 2 on Stack]
    E --> F[Clamp Workpiece on Bar Datum]
    F --> G{Sweep Dial Indicator Across Workpiece Top}
    G -->|Out of Parallel| H[Adjust h Iteratively]
    H --> G
    G -->|Within Tolerance under 0.002 mm| I[Angle Setting Complete - Record theta and h]
    I --> J[End]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Diagram Note for KTU Exam Hall:** When a 14-mark question asks you to *"explain with a sketch the working of a profile projector,"* reproduce **§4.3 above as a sketch** — label the *light source*, *condenser*, *workpiece stage*, *objective lens*, *projection screen*, and *screen protractor* in that exact order along the light path. Examiners award 3 of the 14 marks purely for a correctly-labelled schematic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech University Examination** pattern. Marks are split **3 (Part A) / 14 (Part B)** with a compulsory internal choice in Part B. The KTU Valuation Key penalises **missing units, missing LC declaration, and skipped sine-bar working steps** — see the warning callouts.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]

**Differentiate between a contact-type and a non-contact-type angular measuring instrument. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Contact-Type | Non-Contact-Type |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | The instrument physically touches the workpiece to measure the angle | The instrument measures the angle without physical contact, usually using a light beam |
| Probe pressure | Subject to stylus pressure error | No probe-pressure error |
| Suitable for | Hard, rigid, accessible surfaces | Delicate, soft, or intricately contoured parts |
| **Example** | Sine Bar, Bevel Protractor | **Profile Projector** |

*(Full 3 marks awarded for the table + the named example.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**State the principle of working of a sine bar. What is the role of slip gauges in this instrument?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Principle (2 marks):** The sine bar works on the **principle of trigonometry** — when a bar of fixed roller center-distance L is raised at one end by a stack of slip gauges of height h, the bar tilts through an angle θ such that **sin θ = h / L**. By choosing h, any angle between 0° and 90° can be set with high accuracy.

- **Role of slip gauges (1 mark):** Slip gauges are **precision linear blocks** (accuracy ±0.0002 mm at Grade K) that are *wringed* together to provide the exact calculated height h. They convert a linear measurement (h) into a precise angular setting, exploiting the fact that linear standards are easier to manufacture than angular scales.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Every Part B question is internally optional — you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Choose the one you can answer more completely.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module Experiment 2]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a Universal Bevel Protractor**. Determine its **least count** and state the typical range of measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **300 mm sine bar** is used to set an angle. Calculate the height of the slip-gauge stack required to set an angle of **38° 42'**. Show the slip-gauge combination using a 103-piece set. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ⭐ Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Construction (3 marks):** A universal bevel protractor consists of:
1. A **circular main scale** graduated 0°–90° on each of four quadrants (0°–360° total).
2. A **vernier scale** rigidly attached to a movable blade, with 12 divisions spanning 11° of the main scale.
3. A **fixed stock** that acts as the datum reference.
4. A **movable blade** with a clamping lever and fine-adjustment screw.
5. An **acute-angle attachment** (a small auxiliary plate) used for measuring angles between 90° and 360°.
6. A **magnifier eyepiece** to read the vernier lines clearly.

**Working (2 marks):**
- The fixed stock is held against one surface of the workpiece.
- The movable blade is brought into contact with the second surface.
- The clamping lever is tightened, and the angle between the two blades is read on the main scale + vernier scale.
- For angles > 90°, the acute-angle attachment is fixed to the main stock and the reading is interpreted as **(180° − indicated reading)** or similar transformation.

**Least Count (1 mark):**

$$\text{LC} = 1° - \frac{11°}{12} = \frac{1°}{12} = \frac{60'}{12} = 5'$$

**Range (1 mark):** **0° to 360°** (with the acute-angle attachment, by combining supplementary readings).

---

#### ⭐ Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Convert the angle to decimal degrees:**

$$\theta = 38° 42' = 38 + \frac{42}{60} = 38.700°$$

**Step 2 — Apply the sine-bar equation (1 mark):**

$$h = L \cdot \sin(\theta) = 300 \times \sin(38.700°)$$

**Step 3 — Calculate (3 marks):**

$$\sin(38.700°) = 0.62524$$

$$h = 300 \times 0.62524 = 187.572 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Slip-gauge combination (2 marks):**
A typical wrung stack from a 103-piece KTU set:

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= 1.072 \text{ mm} \\
  &\quad + 1.500 \text{ mm} \\
  &\quad + 5.000 \text{ mm} \\
  &\quad + 30.000 \text{ mm} \\
  &\quad + 50.000 \text{ mm} \\
  &\quad + 100.000 \text{ mm} \\
  &\quad = 187.572 \text{ mm} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verification (1 mark):** The stack is wrung on a Grade-0 surface plate, the sine bar is placed, the workpiece is clamped, and a dial indicator is swept across the workpiece top to confirm parallelism (run-out ≤ 0.002 mm). If not parallel, the stack is re-computed iteratively.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Sine Bar Numerical Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert 42' into 0.700°** before taking sin θ. This is a 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Using the overall bar length instead of the center-distance L** when the question specifies the bar type. The KTU-lab **200 mm and 300 mm** bars have center-distance equal to overall length, but for **compound sine bars** the question may specify a *different* L. Read carefully.
> 3. **Quoting the answer to 3 decimal places** when slip gauges are accurate to ±0.0002 mm only. Round to **0.001 mm** (3 decimals of mm).
> 4. **Not specifying the slip-gauge combination** — examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the wrung stack. Always show the stack.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module Experiment 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Profile Projector**. List its **standard magnifications** and state **two industrial applications**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gear-tooth profile is projected on a profile-projector screen at **20× magnification**. The image of the **tooth thickness (chord)** on the screen measures **18.40 mm** and the image of the **addendum height** measures **44.00 mm**. Calculate the actual chordal thickness and addendum. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ⭐ Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Construction (3 marks):** A profile projector consists of the following, arranged on a common optical bench:

1. **Light source** — a halogen or mercury-vapor lamp (100 W typical), providing intense, uniform illumination.
2. **Condenser lens** — collimates the divergent light from the source into a parallel beam that floods the workpiece.
3. **Work stage** — an X–Y translation table fitted with two micrometer heads (LC = 0.005 mm) to position the workpiece precisely.
4. **Lens turret with 4 objective lenses** — usually 10×, 20×, 50×, 100×, mounted on a rotating disc.
5. **Projection screen** — a 300 mm diameter ground-glass screen on which the magnified silhouette is focused.
6. **Screen overlays** — protractor (0°–360°), cross-hair lines, and templates (e.g., for standard gear-tooth profiles).
7. **Digital readout (DRO)** — for direct linear measurement of the screen image (0.001 mm resolution).

**Working (2 marks):** The workpiece is placed on the X–Y stage and illuminated from below (or behind) by the parallel beam. Light passing around or through the profile enters the chosen objective lens, which projects a **magnified shadow image** onto the ground-glass screen. The operator then measures the image directly using the screen protractor, cross-hair overlay, or DRO. The actual dimension is obtained by **dividing the screen measurement by the magnification M**.

**Standard magnifications (1 mark):** **10×, 20×, 50×, and 100×**.

**Two industrial applications (1 mark):**
1. Inspection of **involute gear-tooth profiles** for correctness of pressure angle, addendum, and dedendum.
2. Inspection of **screw-thread profiles** (form, pitch diameter, crest, root) and **press-tool die profiles**.

---

#### ⭐ Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — State the governing equation (1 mark):**

$$D = \frac{D'}{M}$$

where D = actual size, D' = screen size, M = magnification.

**Step 2 — Calculate the actual chordal thickness (3 marks):**

$$D_{\text{chord}} = \frac{18.40 \text{ mm}}{20} = 0.920 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the actual addendum height (3 marks):**

$$D_{\text{addendum}} = \frac{44.00 \text{ mm}}{20} = 2.200 \text{ mm}$$

**Sanity check (built into step):** For a standard module-1 gear, the addendum is **1 × module = 1 mm** for full-depth teeth, and **1.25 × module = 1.25 mm** for stub-tooth. The computed 2.2 mm addendum suggests this gear has **module ≈ 2** (i.e., it is a coarse-pitch gear), which is dimensionally reasonable for a 20× projection (the image size is 4× the object size, comfortably measurable on a 300 mm screen).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Profile Projector Numerical Problems:**
> 1. **Inverting the magnification** (multiplying instead of dividing). Always remember: **screen size is larger, object size is smaller — so divide.**
> 2. **Forgetting to state the magnification M before plugging it in.** The M value is worth 1 mark on its own.
> 3. **Not stating the units in the final answer.** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing "mm" or "°" symbols.
> 4. **Confusing the linear magnification with the angular magnification.** Profile projectors magnify *linear* dimensions; the **angle** of a contour on the screen is the **same as the actual angle** of the contour (angles are dimensionless and scale-invariant).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your 5-minute rapid-revision checklist before walking into the KTU exam hall.**

- [ ] The **sine bar formula** is **h = L · sin θ** — the single most important equation in this experiment.
- [ ] **L** = center-distance between rollers = stamped on the bar (100, 200, or 300 mm in KTU labs).
- [ ] **Slip gauges** are wrung additively — total h is the sum of individual block sizes.
- [ ] The **bevel protractor LC = 5'** for the standard 12-division / 11°-span vernier. Always state LC first.
- [ ] The **bevel protractor reads angles 0° to 360°** when the acute-angle attachment is used.
- [ ] The **clinometer** is a *gravity-referenced* instrument — its reading depends on the local vertical, not on a calibrated scale. Used on large castings and machine beds.
- [ ] The **profile projector** is *non-contact* and projects a 2-D silhouette — it cannot measure *internal* angles of a hollow part.
- [ ] **Profile projector standard magnifications** = 10×, 20×, 50×, 100×.
- [ ] Profile projector dimension equation: **D (true) = D' (screen) / M**.
- [ ] **Angles on a profile projector screen are scale-invariant** — the angular reading on the screen protractor is the actual angle of the workpiece.
- [ ] **Comparison one-liner to remember:** *Sine bar — trigonometric; Clinometer — gravitational; Bevel protractor — mechanical vernier; Profile projector — optical magnification.*
- [ ] **CO mapping for this experiment (KTU 2024):** CO2 (Apply), CO3 (Analyze), CO4 (Instrumentation practice).
- [ ] **Common slip-gauge stack trick:** Always include a **wringing/wear block** (1.002 mm or 1.005 mm) at the *top* of the stack closest to the roller, to prevent wear of the precision grade blocks.
- [ ] **3-mark golden rules:** (i) always state units; (ii) always state the LC; (iii) always convert arc-minutes to decimal degrees *before* taking a trigonometric function.
- [ ] **14-mark golden rule:** Always pair a *neat labelled sketch* (3 marks) with the *step-by-step derivation* (8–9 marks) and a *conclusion* (1–2 marks).
- [ ] **Profile projector magnification formula check:** $M = \dfrac{\text{image size}}{\text{object size}}$ — the image is always *bigger*, so M > 1.
- [ ] **Sine bar limitation:** Limited to angles between **0° and 60°** in practice (for angles > 60°, h becomes impractically close to L and the slip-gauge stack becomes unstable).
- [ ] **Vernier coincidence line "n" must be the FIRST coincidence starting from the vernier zero** — examiners will not accept "n = 8" if line 7 also happens to coincide visually.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Temperature Using Radiation Pyrometer and Infrared Pyrometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Temperature Using Radiation Pyrometer and Infrared Pyrometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Radiation Pyrometer** is a non-contact temperature measurement device that infers the temperature of a distant object by measuring the **thermal radiation** emitted from its surface, utilizing the principles of **blackbody radiation** governed by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** and **Planck's Radiation Law**. It is used to measure temperatures beyond the range of contact-type sensors (typically above **600 °C / 873 K**), where physical contact would damage or contaminate the sensor.

An **Infrared (IR) Pyrometer** is a specialized subclass of radiation pyrometer that operates within specific **infrared spectral bands** (commonly **0.7 µm to 14 µm**), using either **thermal detectors** (thermopile, bolometer) or **photon detectors** (PbS, InGaAs, HgCdTe) to convert incident IR energy into an electrical signal proportional to the target's surface temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Radiation pyrometers fall under the "non-contact temperature measurement" category. They obey the **fourth-power law of radiation**, making them inherently more sensitive at high temperatures than at low temperatures. Always remember: **contact sensors fail where radiation pyrometers excel** — molten metals, furnace interiors, turbine blades, moving targets, vacuum environments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Plain-English Analogy — "Feeling the Heat from a Campfire"**
>
> Imagine sitting beside a campfire on a cold night. You do not need to **touch** the flame to know it is hot — you can **feel** the warmth hitting your face. The further you sit, the less heat you feel; the closer you sit, the more intense. Your skin is acting as a "radiation sensor."
>
> A **Radiation Pyrometer** does the same job — it catches invisible infrared "warmth rays" from a hot object and converts them into a temperature reading.
>
> Now think of a **night-vision camera** used by the military. It does not see visible light (because it is dark), but it sees **infrared (heat) signatures** of humans, engines, and fires. An **Infrared Pyrometer** is essentially a calibrated, single-pixel "night-vision heat camera" tuned to give an exact temperature number rather than an image.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | σ | **5.670374419 × 10⁻⁸** | W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ |
| Wien's Displacement Constant | b | **2.898 × 10⁻³** | m·K |
| Speed of Light | c | **2.998 × 10⁸** | m·s⁻¹ |
| Planck's Constant | h | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴** | J·s |
| Boltzmann Constant | k | **1.381 × 10⁻²³** | J·K⁻¹ |
| First Radiation Constant | c₁ | **3.742 × 10⁻¹⁶** | W·m² |
| Second Radiation Constant | c₂ | **1.439 × 10⁻²** | m·K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Emissivity (ε)** is the ratio of radiation emitted by a real surface to that of a perfect blackbody at the same temperature. **ε = 1** (perfect blackbody, theoretical ideal) down to **ε ≈ 0.03** (polished gold). Most industrial surfaces fall in the range **0.2 ≤ ε ≤ 0.95**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Why and How

**Why use radiation pyrometers?**
- Contact sensors (thermocouples, RTDs) have a **finite upper limit** (Type B thermocouple ≈ 1800 °C, Pt-Rh → melts).
- Many industrial scenarios demand **remote**, **fast**, and **non-invasive** temperature measurement: molten steel in a ladle, kiln bricks, rocket nozzles, semiconductor wafers, biological tissue, high-speed rolling steel.

**How they work — structured logic:**

1. **Emission Stage:** A hot object (T > 0 K) emits **electromagnetic radiation** across a broad spectrum. The spectral distribution depends on its temperature and surface emissivity.

2. **Propagation Stage:** This radiation travels through the medium (air, vacuum, protective window) to the pyrometer optics. Atmospheric absorption (CO₂, H₂O vapor) can attenuate certain IR bands — hence **band-pass filters** are used in IR pyrometers.

3. **Collection Stage:** Optical elements (lenses, mirrors — often made of **calcium fluoride (CaF₂)**, **germanium (Ge)**, or **zinc selenide (ZnSe)** to transmit IR) focus the incoming radiation onto a **detector**.

4. **Conversion Stage:** The detector converts incident radiant power into an electrical signal (voltage, current, or resistance change). The signal magnitude is proportional to the radiant flux, which itself depends on **T⁴** (Stefan-Boltzmann).

5. **Computation Stage:** Signal conditioning electronics linearize, compensate for emissivity, ambient temperature drift, and distance-to-spot ratio, and display the result in °C or K.

6. **Display Stage:** Calibrated output in engineering units.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann Law: total emitted power per unit area (W/m²) | Total radiation pyrometers |
| 2 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Real surface emission (ε = emissivity) | Practical measurement |
| 3 | $\lambda_m \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | Wien's Displacement Law: peak emission wavelength | Selecting detector band |
| 4 | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{c_1}{\lambda^5 \left[e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1\right]}$ | Planck's Spectral Distribution (W/m²·µm) | Spectral pyrometers |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_b} + \dfrac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln(\varepsilon_\lambda)$ | Corrected temperature from brightness pyrometer | Monochromatic pyrometers |
| 6 | $T = T_a \cdot \left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_a}{\varepsilon_b}\right)^{1/4}$ | True temperature from two-color (ratio) pyrometer | Two-color pyrometers |
| 7 | $D:S = \dfrac{d}{L}$ | Distance-to-Spot ratio (optical resolution) | Optics selection |
| 8 | $V_{out} = K \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^4 + V_{offset}$ | Detector output (linearized) | Calibration curve |
| 9 | $\tau_a = e^{-\alpha(\lambda) \cdot L}$ | Atmospheric transmittance (Beer's Law) | Long-distance correction |
| 10 | $\Delta T = T_{true} - T_{apparent} \approx \dfrac{\lambda T_{apparent}^2}{c_2}(1-\varepsilon)$ | Emissivity error for small Δε | Error estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars in formulas above use `\vert` / `\mid` (not the vertical pipe `|`) to keep the markdown table parser happy. The divider inside the table itself uses standard `|` syntax — only the **math content** uses LaTeX brackets.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel & Metallurgy:** Continuous casting, ladle metallurgy, blast furnace tuyeres, hot-rolling mills — molten steel at 1500–1700 °C cannot be touched.
- **Power Generation:** Boiler tube skin temperature, superheater outlet, gas turbine blade pyrometry (using fiber-coupled IR).
- **Semiconductor & Photovoltaic:** Wafer temperature during CVD/PECVD; rapid thermal processing (RTP).
- **Glass & Ceramics:** Forehearth, lehr, and kiln interior profiling.
- **Aerospace & Defense:** Rocket nozzle throat, re-entry vehicle leading edges, jet engine exhaust.
- **Medical & Biomedical:** Tympanic (ear) thermometers, skin temperature mapping, fever screening (COVID-era mass screening).
- **Food & Pharma:** Pasteurization verification, freeze-drying, autoclave validation.
- **Building Diagnostics:** Thermal imaging for insulation gaps, electrical hot-spot detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Distribution

We start from the spectral emissive power of a blackbody given by **Planck's Law**:

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \left[ \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1} \right]$$

The total emissive power (over all wavelengths) is the integral from 0 to ∞:

$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1 \right]}\, d\lambda$$

**Step 1 — Non-dimensional substitution.** Let $x = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda k T}$, so $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{x k T}$ and $d\lambda = -\dfrac{hc}{x^2 k T}\, dx$.

**Step 2 — Rewrite the integrand.**

$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\left( \dfrac{hc}{x k T} \right)^5 \left[ e^{x} - 1 \right]} \cdot \left( \frac{hc}{x^2 k T} \right) dx$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the algebraic prefactor.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_b &= 2\pi h c^2 \cdot \frac{(x k T)^5}{(h c)^5} \cdot \frac{h c}{k T} \cdot \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^{x} - 1}\, dx \\
&= \frac{2\pi (k T)^4}{h^3 c^2} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^{x} - 1}\, dx
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the definite integral.** Using the standard result:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^{x} - 1}\, dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back and collect constants.**

$$E_b = \frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2}\, T^4$$

**Step 6 — Identify the constant as σ.**

$$\sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.670374419 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{K}^{-4}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{E_b = \sigma T^4 \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}}$$

**Conversion logic summary:**
- Planck's Law (1900) → integrated over λ → Stefan-Boltzmann (1879, derived rigorously only after Planck).
- T⁴ dependence means doubling T increases emitted power **16-fold** (2⁴ = 16). This is why pyrometers are so much more sensitive at high T.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wien's Displacement Law

Start again with Planck's spectral distribution and find the wavelength $\lambda_m$ where $E_{b\lambda}$ is maximum. Set $\dfrac{d E_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$.

**Step 1 — Write Planck's Law in compact form** with $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2$ and $C_2 = hc/k$:

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1 \right]}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to λ.** Use logarithmic differentiation. Let $y = \ln E_{b\lambda} = \ln C_1 - 5\ln\lambda - \ln(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1)$.

**Step 3 — Compute dy/dλ.**

$$\frac{dy}{d\lambda} = -\frac{5}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1} \cdot \left( -\frac{C_2}{\lambda^2 T} \right) e^{C_2/\lambda T}$$

**Step 4 — Set dy/dλ = 0** and introduce $y = C_2/\lambda T$:

$$\frac{5}{1} = \frac{y\, e^{y}}{e^{y} - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the transcendental equation numerically.** The non-trivial root is:

$$y = 4.965114231 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_m T = \frac{C_2}{4.965114231}$$

**Step 6 — Plug in numerical values:**

$$b = \frac{hc}{4.965114231\, k} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{4.9651} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m·K}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\lambda_m T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m·K} = 2898\ \mu\text{m·K}}$$

**Quick numerical check:** A blast furnace at **1800 K** peaks at:
$$\lambda_m = \frac{2898}{1800} = 1.61\ \mu\text{m}$$ (near-IR).
A human body at **310 K** peaks at:
$$\lambda_m = \frac{2898}{310} = 9.35\ \mu\text{m}$$ (long-wave IR — this is exactly the **8–14 µm atmospheric window** exploited by thermal cameras).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Emissivity Correction for Monochromatic (Brightness) Pyrometer

A brightness pyrometer matches the **brightness** of the target with that of an internal reference filament at temperature $T_f$, at a specific wavelength λ.

**Step 1 — Brightness of a real surface** at temperature T, wavelength λ, emissivity ε_λ:
$$B(\lambda, T) = \varepsilon_\lambda \cdot E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$

**Step 2 — Brightness of reference filament** (assumed blackbody, ε = 1):
$$B_f(\lambda, T_f) = E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T_f)$$

**Step 3 — Match the brightnesses** (vanishing pyrometer signal):
$$\varepsilon_\lambda \cdot E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T_f)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Planck's Law** and take logarithms. Using Wien's approximation ($e^{C_2/\lambda T} \gg 1$ in the visible/near-IR for typical T):

$$\varepsilon_\lambda \cdot \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5} e^{-C_2/\lambda T} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5} e^{-C_2/\lambda T_f}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $C_1/\lambda^5$ and take ln:**

$$-\frac{C_2}{\lambda T} + \ln \varepsilon_\lambda = -\frac{C_2}{\lambda T_f}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for 1/T (true temperature):**

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_f} + \frac{\lambda}{C_2} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda}$$

**Step 7 — Invert to get apparent (brightness) temperature from true temperature:**

$$T_f = \frac{T}{1 + \dfrac{\lambda T}{C_2} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda}$$

**Conversion logic:** When ε_λ < 1, ln ε_λ < 0, so the denominator < 1, so $T_f < T$. The **brightness temperature is always LOWER than the true temperature** for ε < 1. This is the classic **emissivity error** that catches students out in KTU exams.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer

**Step 1 — Measure spectral radiance at two wavelengths** λ₁ and λ₂.

**Step 2 — Form the ratio** (Wien approximation):

$$R = \frac{E_{b\lambda_1}}{E_{b\lambda_2}} = \frac{\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_2} \cdot \frac{e^{-C_2/\lambda_1 T}}{e^{-C_2/\lambda_2 T}}$$

**Step 3 — Take natural log:**

$$\ln R = \ln\left(\frac{\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_2}\right) + \frac{C_2}{T}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for T:**

$$\boxed{T = \frac{C_2 \left( \dfrac{1}{\lambda_2} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_1} \right)}{\ln R - \ln(\varepsilon_1/\varepsilon_2)}}$$

**Step 5 — "Gray-body" assumption:** If $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2$ (which holds for many metals across narrow wavelength bands), the formula **collapses** to:

$$T = \frac{C_2 \left( \dfrac{1}{\lambda_2} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_1} \right)}{\ln R}$$

**Key engineering insight:** The emissivity term **vanishes** when ε₁ = ε₂ — this is the principal reason two-color pyrometers are used to measure **oxidized metal surfaces, dirty furnaces, and smoke-obscured targets**, where single-wavelength instruments would fail catastrophically.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pyrometer Family Comparison

| Parameter | Optical (Disappearing Filament) | Total Radiation | Infrared (Single-Color) | Two-Color (Ratio) |
|-----------|--------------------------------|-----------------|-------------------------|-------------------|
| Spectral range | 0.4–0.7 µm (visible) | 0.1–∞ (all) | 0.7–14 µm (selectable) | Two narrow bands |
| Detector | Human eye | Thermopile | Thermopile / Photodiode | Two matched detectors |
| T range (°C) | 700–3500 | 600–3000 | −50 to 3000 | 800–3500 |
| ε sensitivity | High | High (∝ ε) | High (∝ ε) | Low (cancels if gray) |
| Smoke/dust immunity | Poor | Moderate | Filter-dependent | Excellent |
| Typical accuracy | ±10 °C | ±1–2% of reading | ±0.5–2% of reading | ±0.5–1% of reading |
| Cost | Low | Moderate | Low–Moderate | High |
| KTU exam weight | Medium | High | High | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Total Radiation Pyrometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Target Surface T_hot] --> B[Optical Mirror / Lens CaF2 or Ge]
    B --> C[Adjustable Aperture Diaphragm]
    C --> D[Beam Splitter / Chopper]
    D --> E1[Reference Blackbody Cavity 373 K]
    D --> E2[Multi-junction Thermopile Detector]
    E2 --> F[Low-Noise Preamplifier]
    E1 --> F
    F --> G[AC Coupling and Phase-Sensitive Demodulation]
    G --> H[Microcontroller Linearization T4 to T]
    H --> I[LCD / 4-20 mA Output Display in deg C]
    J[Emissivity Setting Knob eps 0.10 to 1.00] --> H
    K[Ambient Temperature Compensation PT100] --> H
    L[Distance to Spot Calibration Lookup] --> H
```

```mermaid
    A[Target Object 600 to 3000 C] --> B[IR Objective Lens ZnSe or Germanium]
    B --> C[Bandpass Filter 1 to 14 um]
    C --> D[Field Stop Aperture Defines Spot Size]

    E --> F[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    F --> G[Ambient Reference Detector Compensates Drift]
    G --> H[Sigma-Delta 16-bit ADC]
    H --> I[DSP Linearization and Emissivity Compensation]
    I --> J[Digital Display deg C]
    I --> K[Analog Output 4-20 mA or 0-10 V]
    I --> L[Serial RS-485 / Modbus / HART]

```

```mermaid
        S2 --> S1
    end
```

```mermaid
    A1[Reference Thermocouple T_ref] --> B1[True T axis horizontal deg C]
    A2[Pyrometer Reading T_pyr] --> C1[Pyrometer T axis vertical deg C]
    B1 --- D1[Linear Region Above 600 C slope 1.00]
    D1 --- D2[Zero Drift Offset small below 400 C]
```
