> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for KTU Lab Record:** When drawing the experimental setup in your lab record, label **at minimum** these six items clearly: (1) Heat source with controller, (2) Reference thermocouple with cold-junction compensation indicator, (3) Pyrometer with optics and laser sight, (4) Optical path geometry (D:S ratio), (5) Distance scale (ruler / optical rail), (6) Emissivity setting knob. Most KTU lab external examiners award 2 marks purely for a **neat, fully labeled** setup diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Radiation Pyrometer. State the fundamental law on which it is based.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **radiation pyrometer** is a non-contact temperature measuring instrument that determines the temperature of a hot body by measuring the **intensity of thermal radiation** emitted from its surface.

It is based on the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**, which states that the total radiant energy emitted per unit surface area per unit time by a blackbody is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant and T is the absolute temperature in Kelvin. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State Wien's Displacement Law and explain its significance in selecting a detector for an IR pyrometer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Wien's Displacement Law states that the wavelength at which the **spectral emissive power of a blackbody is maximum** is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature:

$$\lambda_m T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m·K}$$

**Significance:** This law allows the designer to choose a **detector whose spectral response matches the peak emission wavelength** of the target. For example, a furnace at 1500 K peaks at ≈ 1.93 µm (near-IR) — a silicon photodiode or InGaAs sensor is suitable. A human body at 310 K peaks at ≈ 9.35 µm — a thermopile or HgCdTe (MCT) detector with an 8–14 µm bandpass filter is required. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Adapted]**

### **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Total Radiation Pyrometer**. State **two** advantages and **one** limitation. (7 marks)

### **(b)** A total radiation pyrometer is sighted on a hot surface. The detector receives radiant flux from a blackbody of temperature 1800 K. The lens has a diameter of 25 mm and a focal length of 100 mm. The thermopile responsivity is 18 µV/W and the system has an emissivity setting of 0.85. Calculate the **thermopile output voltage**. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A total radiation pyrometer consists of:
1. **Objective lens/mirror** (often calcium fluoride CaF₂ or quartz) to focus incoming radiation onto the detector.
2. **Adjustable diaphragm/aperture** to limit the radiation flux and define the field of view.
3. **Multi-junction thermopile** — a series array of several thermocouples (e.g., 16 junctions of Chromel-Alumel or bismuth-silver) whose hot junctions are blackened to absorb radiation, while cold junctions are shielded.
4. **Reference blackbody cavity** maintained at ambient (or 373 K) for cold-junction compensation.
5. **Chopper / beam modulator** to convert DC radiation signal into AC for low-noise amplification.
6. **Preamplifier and display unit** calibrated directly in °C.

**Working (3 marks):**
- Thermal radiation from the hot target is collected by the lens and focused on the thermopile hot junctions.
- The absorbed radiation raises the hot-junction temperature, generating an EMF proportional to the radiant flux $\varepsilon \sigma T^4$.
- The thermopile EMF is amplified and the signal-conditioning electronics perform the **fourth-root computation** to linearize the response and display the temperature.

**Advantages (1 mark):** (any two)
- No contact with the hot body — useful for moving or hazardous targets.
- Very fast response (milliseconds) compared to contact sensors.
- Measures temperatures well above thermocouple limits (>1800 °C).

**Limitation (1 mark):**
- Sensitive to **emissivity** of the target surface and **atmospheric absorption** (smoke, dust, CO₂, H₂O vapor between the pyrometer and target).

---

### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $T = 1800$ K
- Lens diameter $D = 25$ mm = 0.025 m
- Focal length $f = 100$ mm = 0.100 m
- Responsivity $R = 18$ µV/W = $18 \times 10^{-6}$ V/W
- Emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Radiant power per unit area** (Stefan-Boltzmann, with emissivity):

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1800)^4$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(1800)^4 &= (1.8)^4 \times 10^{12} = 10.4976 \times 10^{12} = 1.04776 \times 10^{13}\\
E &= 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.04776 \times 10^{13}\\
E &= 0.85 \times 5.9408 \times 10^{5}\\
E &= 5.0497 \times 10^{5}\ \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Collecting aperture area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total radiant power intercepted by lens:**

$$P = E \cdot A = 5.0497 \times 10^{5} \times 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} = 247.87\ \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Thermopile output voltage:**

$$V = R \times P = 18 \times 10^{-6} \times 247.87$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 4.46\ \text{mV}}$$

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

### **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **Infrared Pyrometer** using a **thermopile detector**. Compare it with a **photon (quantum) detector** in terms of spectral response, response time, and operating temperature range. (7 marks)

### **(b)** A furnace wall is observed with an IR pyrometer. The pyrometer reads a **brightness temperature of 1180 K** at wavelength 1.0 µm. If the wall emissivity is 0.70, calculate the **true temperature** of the wall using the Wien approximation. Take $C_2 = 1.439 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution (a):**

**Block Diagram and Working (4 marks):**
- **IR objective lens (ZnSe or Ge)** collects radiation from the target.
- **Bandpass filter** selects a narrow IR band (e.g., 8–14 µm for low-temp or 1.6 µm for high-temp).
- **Thermopile detector** — absorbs IR energy; hot junctions heat up; output EMF $\propto$ incident power $\propto T^4$.
- **Ambient reference sensor** compensates for the cold-junction temperature drift.
- **Preamplifier and linearization circuit** (often a look-up table or analog fourth-root circuit) converts signal to °C.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Thermopile (Thermal) | Photon (Quantum) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------|
| Spectral response | Broadband, wavelength-independent | Selective, band-limited by semiconductor bandgap |
| Response time | Slow (10 ms to 1 s) | Very fast (ns to µs) |
| Operating temperature range | Wide; works from cryogenic to >2000 °C | Limited by detector cooling (often needs TE or LN₂) |
| Sensitivity (D*) | Lower | Higher (10–100× better) |
| Examples | Thermopile, bolometer, pyroelectric | PbS, PbSe, InGaAs, HgCdTe (MCT) |
| Cost | Low | High (especially cooled MCT) |

---

### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Apparent (brightness) temperature $T_b = 1180$ K
- Wavelength $\lambda = 1.0\ \mu\text{m} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.70$
- $C_2 = 1.439 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K

**Step 1 — Use the monochromatic pyrometer emissivity correction equation:**

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_b} + \frac{\lambda}{C_2} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the emissivity term:**

$$\ln(0.70) = -0.35667$$

$$\frac{\lambda}{C_2} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{1.439 \times 10^{-2}} = 6.949 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the correction term:**

$$\frac{\lambda}{C_2} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda = 6.949 \times 10^{-5} \times (-0.35667) = -2.479 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute 1/T:**

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1180} + (-2.479 \times 10^{-5}) = 8.4746 \times 10^{-4} - 2.479 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = 8.2267 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Take reciprocal to get true temperature:**

$$T = \frac{1}{8.2267 \times 10^{-4}} = 1215.6\ \text{K}$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{true}} \approx 1216\ \text{K} \approx 943\ °\text{C}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Because emissivity is less than 1, the **true temperature is higher than the apparent brightness temperature** by about 36 K. The pyrometer "under-reads" because the real surface emits less radiation than a blackbody at the same temperature.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** (or vice-versa) — Stefan-Boltzmann T⁴ requires **absolute Kelvin**. Lose 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up brightness and true temperature** — A pyrometer ALWAYS reads brightness/radiance temperature; you must **explicitly apply the emissivity correction** to get the true temperature.
> 3. **Using |ε| as 1 by default** — Many students assume the target is a perfect blackbody. If the problem gives ε, USE IT.
> 4. **Forgetting π/4 in the lens area formula** — Use $A = \pi D^2 / 4$, not $D^2$.
> 5. **Not labeling the W/m² units of E_b** — Examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units in numerical answers.
> 6. **In the lab record, NOT plotting the calibration curve** with both axes labeled and a proper linear-fit line — KTU lab externals specifically check for this; 2 marks reserved.

### 5.3 Lab Experiment Outline — Step-by-Step Procedure

**Experiment: Measurement of Temperature Using Radiation Pyrometer and Infrared Pyrometer**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Total Radiation Pyrometer (with thermopile detector, millivolt output)
2. Infrared Pyrometer (single-color, with emissivity adjustment)
3. Muffle furnace or heat source with PID controller (0–1200 °C)
4. Reference Type-K/R thermocouple with digital indicator (±1 °C)
5. Optical bench / rail with mounting clamps
6. Emissivity test plates: ε = 0.05 (polished aluminum), ε = 0.30, ε = 0.60, ε = 0.95 (matte black)
7. Laser aiming module (often built into the pyrometer)
8. Distance measurement scale (0.5 m to 2 m)

**Procedure:**

1. **Warm up** the furnace and reference indicator for 15 minutes.
2. **Set** the furnace controller to a starting temperature of **200 °C** (or lowest possible).
3. **Mount** the pyrometer on the optical rail at a fixed distance of 0.5 m from the target.
4. **Aim** the pyrometer using the laser sight onto the **blackened emissivity plate** (ε = 0.95).
5. **Set the pyrometer emissivity** to match the plate (0.95).
6. **Allow 10 minutes** for the furnace to reach thermal equilibrium at the setpoint.
7. **Record** the reference thermocouple temperature $T_{ref}$ and the pyrometer reading $T_{pyr}$.
8. **Increment** the setpoint by 100 °C and repeat step 6–7 up to 1100 °C.
9. **Repeat** the entire procedure with emissivity plates of 0.30 and 0.05.
10. **Vary the distance** from 0.5 m to 2.0 m in 0.25 m steps at a fixed furnace temperature (e.g., 800 °C) to study the **distance-to-spot ratio** effect.
11. **Tabulate** all observations.
12. **Plot** calibration curves: $T_{pyr}$ vs $T_{ref}$ for each ε; and $T_{pyr}$ vs distance.
13. **Calculate** percentage error:
$$\%\ \text{Error} = \frac{T_{ref} - T_{pyr}}{T_{ref}} \times 100$$
14. **Identify** the linearity range, sensitivity, and any systematic offset.

**Observations (Sample Tabulation):**

| Setpoint °C | T_ref °C | T_rad (ε=0.95) °C | T_IR (ε=0.30) °C | Error (ε=0.95) % | Error (ε=0.30) % |
|-------------|----------|-------------------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| 200 | 198 | 192 | 142 | 3.0 | 28.3 |
| 400 | 401 | 396 | 318 | 1.2 | 20.7 |
| 600 | 602 | 599 | 506 | 0.5 | 15.9 |
| 800 | 803 | 801 | 695 | 0.2 | 13.4 |
| 1000 | 1001 | 999 | 879 | 0.2 | 12.2 |

**Inference:**
- At **high ε (≈ 0.95)**, the pyrometer tracks the true temperature within 1% — confirming the Stefan-Boltzmann T⁴ behavior.
- At **low ε (0.30)**, the pyrometer significantly under-reads at all temperatures; the error decreases with rising T (because T⁴ amplifies the small ε, reducing the relative gap).
- **Distance** has negligible effect as long as the target fills the pyrometer's spot size (D:S ratio compliance).

### 5.4 Viva-Voce — High-Yield Questions (4-mark weight each)

1. **Why is a radiation pyrometer not suitable for measuring the temperature of polished silver?** → Because silver has emissivity ≈ 0.02, emitting almost no thermal radiation; the pyrometer would read close to ambient.
2. **Why must the target be larger than the pyrometer's spot size?** → If the spot spills outside the target, the pyrometer averages in background (cooler) radiation, under-reading.
3. **What is the D:S ratio of a pyrometer with 10:1 optics at 1 m distance?** → Spot diameter = 100 mm. So a target smaller than 100 mm at 1 m will be misread.
4. **Why are quartz or CaF₂ lenses used instead of glass?** → Ordinary glass absorbs IR radiation above 2.5 µm. CaF₂ transmits up to 8 µm; ZnSe up to 14 µm; Ge up to 20 µm.
5. **How does a two-color pyrometer eliminate emissivity error?** → It uses the ratio of emissions at two wavelengths; for a "gray body" the ε cancels.
6. **What is meant by "effective emissivity" of a target viewed through a dusty window?** → It is the combined factor of (target ε × window transmittance); always less than 1.

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 **Core law:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$ (Stefan-Boltzmann) — the **only** formula you must memorize verbatim.
- 🎯 **Wien's Law:** $\lambda_m T = 2898\ \mu\text{m·K}$ — used for **detector selection** in design problems.
- 🎯 **Planck's Law:** $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1 \right]}$ — used for spectral pyrometers and accurate calculations.
- 🎯 **Brightness temperature** (apparent) is **always ≤ true temperature** when ε < 1.
- 🎯 **Two-color pyrometer** is the only design that **eliminates emissivity error** for gray bodies.
- 🎯 **Constants to remember:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$, $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$, $C_2 = 1.439 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K.
- 🎯 **Distance-to-Spot ratio (D:S)** dictates the minimum target size; critical for field installation.
- 🎯 **Pyrometers cannot measure below ~500 °C reliably** with simple thermopile designs without active cooling and special low-noise electronics.
- 🎯 **Lab procedure must include**: emissivity plate testing, distance variation, calibration curve plotting, and percentage error computation.
- 🎯 **Common KTU pitfall:** Confusing the **focal length** with the **distance to target** in lens-area calculations. The relevant area is the **entrance pupil (lens) diameter**, not the focal length.
- 🎯 **Industrial standard wavelengths** for IR pyrometers: 0.9 µm, 1.0 µm, 1.6 µm, 2.2 µm, 3.9 µm, 4.5 µm, 5.0 µm, 8–14 µm — each chosen to avoid atmospheric absorption bands.
- 🎯 **Detector families:** Thermal (thermopile, bolometer, pyroelectric) vs. Photon (PbS, PbSe, InSb, InGaAs, HgCdTe). Photon = faster & more sensitive, but requires cooling.
- 🎯 **Units discipline:** Always use **Kelvin** in T⁴ formulas; convert to °C only at the final display step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, allocate marks as **Diagram (3) + Theory (4) + Formula Derivation (4) + Numerical (3)**. The examiner's key is rigid about this split. Practice the four derivations in Section 3.5 of these notes until you can write them in under 12 minutes each. For lab records, the **calibration curve** and the **% error table** are worth ~30% of the external marks — never skip them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Torque and Force: Measurement of Cutting Force During Turning, Drilling, and Milling Using Tool Force Dynamometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Torque and Force: Measurement of Cutting Force During Turning, Drilling, and Milling Using Tool Force Dynamometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tool Force Dynamometer (TFD):** A precision transducer system used to **measure the dynamic cutting forces and torques** generated during material removal processes (turning, drilling, milling). It is mounted between the tool holder and the machine tool slide/spindle and converts the mechanical force developed at the cutting zone into a measurable electrical signal using strain-sensitive elements (strain gauges or piezoelectric crystals).

**Cutting Force:** The force exerted by the workpiece on the cutting tool during machining, resolved into orthogonal components depending on the kinematics of the operation.

> [!NOTE]
> The force components are conventionally measured in **Newton (N)** and torque in **Newton-meter (N·m)**. The accuracy required is typically $\pm 1\%$ of full scale with a dynamic frequency response of **at least 1 kHz** to capture the tooth-passing frequency in milling.

## 1.2 The Three Orthogonal Force Components

In a **Cartesian machine-tool coordinate system**, every cutting process generates components along three orthogonal axes:

| Symbol | Axis | Direction | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $F_c$ | X-axis | Tangential to cutting velocity | **Main cutting force** (does the work) |
| $F_f$ | Z-axis (Turning) / Y-axis (Drilling) | Along feed motion | **Feed force** (resists tool advance) |
| $F_r$ | Y-axis (Turning) | Radial / perpendicular | **Radial force** (deflects tool) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sense of Touch" of a Machine Tool

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a blindfolded chef cutting vegetables. To know how hard to press without crushing the food, the chef's fingers must act like a *dynamometer* — feeling the resistance (force), the direction (component), and the rhythmic vibrations (dynamics).

A **tool force dynamometer** is essentially the *mechanical "fingertip"* of a machine tool. While a human hand feels force through nerves, the dynamometer feels it through:

- **Elastic deformation** of a calibrated metal body (the "sensing element")
- **Strain gauges** bonded to that body (the "nerve endings")  
- A **Wheatstone bridge circuit** (the "signal translator")
- An **amplifier + DAQ** (the "brain")

Just as a doctor measures grip strength with a dynamometer in a clinic, a manufacturing engineer measures cutting strength at the tool tip to evaluate machinability, optimize parameters, and predict tool life.

## 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU 2024 Curriculum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CO (Course Outcome) mapping** for this experiment typically targets:
> - **CO2:** Apply instrumentation principles to measure physical quantities in manufacturing.
> - **CO3:** Analyze force, torque, pressure, and flow signals with appropriate transducers.
> - **CO4:** Evaluate measurement uncertainty and calibration of industrial sensors.

This experiment bridges **mechanical engineering mechanics** with **electronic instrumentation** — students must understand both the *physics of cutting* AND the *electronics of signal conditioning*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Cutting Force Components in Turning

In **orthogonal cutting** (2-D simplification of turning), the cutting tool removes a layer of width $b$ and uncut chip thickness $t_1$. The cutting velocity $V_c$ is tangential to the workpiece.

The three components are:

- $F_c$ — Tangential (cutting direction) — **largest, governs power**
- $F_f$ — Axial/feed direction — **medium, governs feed drive load**
- $F_r$ — Radial (depth-of-cut direction) — **causes tool deflection**

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical magnitude relation (most common case, $\gamma > 0$ rake angle):**
> $$F_c > F_f > F_r$$
> For example, in mild steel turning: $F_c : F_f : F_r \approx 1.0 : 0.5 : 0.4$.

## 2.2 Cutting Force Components in Drilling

Drilling is a **rotational + translational** operation. Only two measurable quantities are relevant:

- **Torque $M_c$** about the drill axis (resists spindle rotation)
- **Thrust force $F_z$** along the drill axis (resists feed motion)

The drill experiences both simultaneously; $F_z$ is usually **40–60%** of $F_c$ in drilling equivalent.

## 2.3 Cutting Force Components in Milling

Milling is **intermittent cutting** (toothed cutter). Force components are similar to turning ($F_c, F_f, F_r$) but are **periodic** with the tooth-passing frequency $f_t = N \times n$, where $N$ is number of teeth and $n$ is spindle speed in rev/s.

## 2.4 The Strain Gauge Dynamometer — Operational Principle

The most widely used dynamometer in machine-tool labs is the **strain-gauge type**, working as follows:

1. **Elastic Element (Body):** A high-strength steel or aluminum body is machined with a precise geometry (octagonal ring, cantilever beam, or hollow cylinder) that deforms elastically under load.
2. **Strain Gauges:** Foil-type gauges (grid resistance ~350 Ω) are bonded at locations of **maximum strain** (determined by Finite Element Analysis).
3. **Wheatstone Bridge:** Four gauges are connected in a bridge so that:
   - Output voltage $V_o$ is **proportional** to applied force.
   - Temperature-induced resistance changes **self-cancel** (common-mode rejection).
4. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplifier (gain 100–1000) → Low-pass filter → ADC → PC display.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hooke's Law for Dynamometer Body:**
> $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon \quad \text{and} \quad V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4} \left(1 + \nu\right)$$
> Where $\sigma$ = stress (N/m²), $E$ = Young's modulus (N/m²), $\varepsilon$ = strain (dimensionless), $GF$ = gauge factor (~2.0), $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio.

## 2.5 The Octagonal Ring Dynamometer — Most Common Design

The **octagonal ring** (or "ring-type") dynamometer is the **industry standard** for 3-component turning force measurement.

**Geometry:** Three identical octagonal rings mounted orthogonally, each carrying 4 strain gauges.

**Working:**

- Each octagonal ring is sensitive to forces in **one plane** only.
- Three rings (XY, XZ, YZ planes) capture $F_x, F_y, F_z$ independently.
- Gauge orientation is at $\pm 45°$ to the ring's neutral axis to maximize sensitivity to in-plane shear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why octagonal?** An octagon provides:
> - High strain concentration at the corners (well-defined deformation zones)
> - Good linear response over wide load range
> - Insensitivity to off-axis loading (cross-talk typically < 2%)
> - Compact, rugged design that survives workshop conditions

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table: Master Formula Reference for Cutting Force & Dynamometer Calculations**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_c = F_c \cdot V_c$ | Cutting power (turning/milling) | **W** |
| 2 | $P_c = M_c \cdot \omega$ | Cutting power (drilling) | **W** |
| 3 | $M_c = F_c \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$ | Drilling torque (single-point equivalent) | **N·m** |
| 4 | $V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot n$ | Cutting velocity | **m/min** |
| 5 | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi n}{60}$ | Angular velocity (rad/s) | **rad/s** |
| 6 | $U_c = \dfrac{F_c}{b \cdot t_1}$ | Specific cutting energy | **J/mm³** or **N/mm²** |
| 7 | $K_s = \dfrac{F_c}{A_c}$ | Specific cutting force (force/chip area) | **N/mm²** |
| 8 | $Q = b \cdot t_1 \cdot V_c$ | Material removal rate (MRR) | **mm³/s** |
| 9 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R}{4R}$ | Wheatstone bridge output voltage | **V** |
| 10 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | Strain definition | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Strain gauge resistance change | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\tan \beta = \mu$ | Friction angle relation | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\phi = 45° + \dfrac{\gamma}{2} - \dfrac{\beta}{2}$ | Merchant's shear-angle equation | **degrees** |
| 14 | $R = \sqrt{F_c^{2} + F_f^{2}}$ | Resultant cutting force (orthogonal) | **N** |
| 15 | $F_{th} = M_c \cdot \dfrac{2}{D}$ | Equivalent thrust from torque | **N** |

> **Notation Quick-Key:**
> $F_c$ = cutting force (N), $V_c$ = cutting speed (m/s), $M_c$ = cutting torque (N·m), $n$ = spindle speed (rev/min), $D$ = tool/workpiece diameter (m), $b$ = width of cut (mm), $t_1$ = uncut chip thickness (mm), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $U_c$ = specific energy, $K_s$ = specific cutting force, $\gamma$ = rake angle, $\beta$ = friction angle, $\phi$ = shear angle, $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $V_{ex}$ = excitation voltage, $GF$ = gauge factor, $R$ = gauge resistance.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Industry

> [!NOTE]
> - **Process Optimization:** Force data reveals optimal cutting parameters for minimum cost.
> - **Tool Condition Monitoring:** Force spikes indicate tool wear or breakage.
> - **Chatter Detection:** Frequency spectrum of $F_c$ identifies unstable vibrations.
> - **CAD/CAM Validation:** Measured forces validate FEM cutting simulations.
> - **Machine Tool Design:** Force data drives structural sizing of slides, spindles, ball screws.
> - **Energy Audit:** $P_c = F_c V_c$ enables Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) calculations for sustainable manufacturing.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Resultant Cutting Force in Orthogonal Cutting

**Given:** Orthogonal cutting with rake angle $\gamma$, friction angle $\beta$, and chip-tool contact has friction force $F$ and normal force $N$.

**Step 1 — Define the resultant force on the tool:**
The resultant force $R$ acting on the tool face combines the friction force $F$ (along rake face) and normal force $N$ (perpendicular to rake face):

$$R = \sqrt{F^{2} + N^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Resolve $R$ into Cartesian machine axes:**

The angle between $R$ and the $F_f$ (feed direction) is $(\beta - \gamma)$. Therefore:

$$F_c = R \cos(\beta - \gamma)$$

$$F_f = R \sin(\beta - \gamma)$$

**Step 3 — Use the Pythagorean identity to verify:**

$$R^{2} = F_c^{2} + F_f^{2}$$

$$R^{2} = R^{2}\cos^{2}(\beta - \gamma) + R^{2}\sin^{2}(\beta - \gamma)$$

$$R^{2} = R^{2} \left[\cos^{2}(\beta - \gamma) + \sin^{2}(\beta - \gamma)\right]$$

$$R^{2} = R^{2} \cdot 1 = R^{2}$$

$$\therefore R = \sqrt{F_c^{2} + F_f^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** Once $F_c$ and $F_f$ are **measured by the dynamometer**, this equation gives the resultant force $R$ at the chip-tool interface. From $R$, the friction force $F$ and friction angle $\beta$ can be back-calculated — a critical parameter for machinability studies.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Merchant's Shear Angle Equation

**Given:** Free-body diagram of the chip. Forces on shear plane: shear force $F_s$ (along shear direction), normal force $F_n$ (perpendicular). Forces on tool-chip face: $F$ (friction), $N$ (normal).

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium of the chip:**

Resolve along and perpendicular to the shear plane:

$$F_s = N \sin\phi + F \cos\phi$$

$$F_n = N \cos\phi - F \sin\phi$$

**Step 2 — Apply Coulomb friction law:**

$$F = \mu N = N \tan\beta$$

**Step 3 — Substitute friction relation into equilibrium:**

$$F_s = N \sin\phi + N \tan\beta \cos\phi$$

$$F_s = N \left[\sin\phi + \tan\beta \cos\phi\right]$$

$$F_n = N \cos\phi - N \tan\beta \sin\phi$$

$$F_n = N \left[\cos\phi - \tan\beta \sin\phi\right]$$

**Step 4 — Divide to eliminate $N$:**

$$\frac{F_s}{F_n} = \frac{\sin\phi + \tan\beta \cos\phi}{\cos\phi - \tan\beta \sin\phi}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify using trigonometric identity:**

Multiply numerator and denominator by $\cos\phi$:

$$\frac{F_s}{F_n} = \frac{\sin\phi \cos\phi + \sin\beta \cos^{2}\phi}{\cos^{2}\phi - \sin\beta \sin\phi \cos\phi}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $\cos^{2}\phi$:

$$\frac{F_s}{F_n} = \frac{\tan\phi + \sin\beta}{1 - \sin\beta \tan\phi}$$

Recognize as the tangent addition formula $\tan(A+B)$:

$$\frac{F_s}{F_n} = \tan(\phi + \beta)$$

**Step 6 — Apply minimum energy principle (Merchant's hypothesis):**

For chip formation to occur with minimum energy, the shear plane is oriented such that $\phi$ minimizes $F_s$. Differentiating $F_s(\phi)$ and setting to zero yields:

$$2\phi + \beta - \gamma = 90°$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $\phi$ (Merchant's Equation):**

$$\phi = 45° + \frac{\gamma}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most-tested equation in machining theory at KTU.** Commit it to memory. Increasing positive rake angle $\gamma$ *increases* shear angle (thinner chip, lower force). Increasing friction angle $\beta$ *decreases* shear angle (thicker chip, higher force).

## 3.3 Derivation III — Cutting Power from Dynamometer Reading

**Given:** Dynamometer records $F_c$ (Newtons); lathe tachometer reads $n$ (rev/min) on a workpiece of diameter $D$ (mm).

**Step 1 — Cutting velocity from kinematics:**

$$V_c = \pi D n \quad \text{(in mm/min if D in mm, n in rev/min)}$$

Convert to m/s by dividing by 60,000:

$$V_c \left[\frac{m}{s}\right] = \frac{\pi D \left[mm\right] \cdot n \left[\frac{rev}{min}\right]}{60{,}000}$$

**Step 2 — Cutting power definition:**

$$P_c = F_c \cdot V_c \quad \text{[W, with V_c in m/s]}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$P_c = F_c \cdot \frac{\pi D n}{60{,}000}$$

$$P_c \left[W\right] = \frac{F_c \left[N\right] \cdot \pi \cdot D \left[mm\right] \cdot n \left[rpm\right]}{60{,}000}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $F_c = 1200$ N, $D = 80$ mm, $n = 500$ rpm.

$$P_c = \frac{1200 \times \pi \times 80 \times 500}{60{,}000}$$

$$P_c = \frac{1200 \times 3.1416 \times 80 \times 500}{60{,}000}$$

$$P_c = \frac{150{,}796{,}447}{60{,}000}$$

$$P_c \approx 2513 \text{ W} \approx 2.51 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For mild steel at moderate cutting conditions, $P_c$ typically ranges from **0.5 to 5 kW** for a single-point tool on a medium lathe. If the calculated value is wildly outside this range, check unit conversions!

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Drilling Thrust and Torque from Equivalent Cutting Force

**Given:** Drill diameter $D$, spindle speed $n$, feed $f$, material specific cutting force $K_s$.

**Step 1 — Chip cross-sectional area at drill periphery:**

For a twist drill, the chip thickness varies along the cutting edge. Approximate average:

$$A_c = \frac{f \cdot D}{2} \quad \text{[mm}^2\text{]}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent tangential cutting force:**

$$F_c = K_s \cdot A_c = K_s \cdot \frac{f \cdot D}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Drilling torque (force × moment arm, integrated):**

For uniform force distribution along the cutting lip from $0$ to $D/2$:

$$M_c = \int_{0}^{D/2} F(r) \cdot r \, dr$$

Assuming $F(r)$ varies linearly with $r$ (since chip thickness increases outward):

$$M_c = \frac{F_c \cdot D}{2 \cdot 2} = \frac{F_c \cdot D}{4}$$

Substituting $F_c$:

$$M_c = \frac{K_s \cdot f \cdot D^{2}}{8}$$

**Step 4 — Thrust force (axial, $\approx 1.0$ to $1.5$ times $F_c$ for drilling):**

$$F_z = C \cdot F_c \quad \text{where } C \approx 1.2 \text{ for mild steel}$$

$$F_z \approx 1.2 \cdot \frac{K_s \cdot f \cdot D}{2} = 0.6 \cdot K_s \cdot f \cdot D$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $D = 12$ mm, $f = 0.15$ mm/rev, $K_s = 2000$ N/mm² (mild steel).

$$F_c = 2000 \times \frac{0.15 \times 12}{2} = 2000 \times 0.9 = 1800 \text{ N}$$

$$M_c = \frac{2000 \times 0.15 \times 144}{8} = \frac{43{,}200}{8} = 5400 \text{ N·mm} = 5.4 \text{ N·m}$$

$$F_z = 1.2 \times 1800 = 2160 \text{ N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Notice that **torque scales with $D^2$** while **thrust scales with $D$**. Therefore, increasing drill diameter is *much* more punishing on the spindle than on the feed drive — a fact that guides drill size selection.

## 3.5 Derivation V — Strain Gauge Bridge Output in a Dynamometer

**Given:** Wheatstone bridge with four active strain gauges ($R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$), each experiencing strain $\varepsilon_i$.

**Step 1 — Bridge output voltage (Kirchhoff's voltage law):**

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \left[\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right]$$

**Step 2 — For equal nominal resistance $R$ and small $\Delta R_i$ changes:**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \left[\frac{\Delta R_1}{R} - \frac{\Delta R_2}{R} + \frac{\Delta R_3}{R} - \frac{\Delta R_4}{R}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply strain gauge equation $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$:**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF}{4} \left[\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_4\right]$$

**Step 4 — For an axial-force beam with two gauges in tension (+) and two in compression (−), $\varepsilon_i = \pm\varepsilon$:**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF}{4} \left[\varepsilon + \varepsilon + \varepsilon + \varepsilon\right] = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 — Convert strain to force via Hooke's law on the dynamometer body:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$

Therefore:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for force (calibration equation):**

$$F = \frac{V_o \cdot A \cdot E}{V_{ex} \cdot GF}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the calibration equation programmed into the dynamometer software.** By recording $(F, V_o)$ pairs under known static loads, the slope $\dfrac{V_o}{F}$ is determined, and the dynamometer can then output force directly in Newtons from any measured voltage.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Dynamometer Selection by Operation

| Parameter | Turning | Drilling | Milling |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mounting** | Tool post | Between chuck & spindle | Spindle housing or table |
| **Components** | $F_c, F_f, F_r$ | $M_c, F_z$ | $F_c, F_f, F_r$ |
| **Typical Range** | 0–5 kN each | 0–500 N·m, 0–10 kN | 0–3 kN each |
| **Frequency Need** | Quasi-static to 100 Hz | Quasi-static | 1 Hz – 2 kHz (tooth passing) |
| **Recommended Type** | Octagonal ring (3-axis) | Piezoelectric + slip ring | Piezoelectric dynamometer |
| **Special Feature** | Static calibration easy | Needs rotating telemetry | Requires high dynamic range |
| **Cross-talk** | < 2% with proper design | < 5% (torque/thrust coupling) | < 3% |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Dynamometer Measurement Chain

The diagram below traces the **signal flow** from physical cutting force to displayed digital reading.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Workpiece Material] --> nodeB[Tool Tip with Cutting Force]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Dynamometer Body Elastic Element]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Strain Gauges Bonded on Body]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Wheatstone Bridge Circuit]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 100 to 1000]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Low Pass Anti Aliasing Filter]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Data Acquisition Card DAQ]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Personal Computer with Software]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Digital Display in Newton]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Graph Force vs Time]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Spectrum Frequency Domain]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the chain:** Force → Strain → Resistance change → Voltage → Amplified voltage → Filtered voltage → Digital count → Calibrated force value (N). The computer performs the inverse of the calibration equation derived in Section 3.5.

## 4.2 Cutting Force Components Diagram — Turning Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TurnForces[Turning Operation Force Components]
        dirA[Cutting Velocity Vc Direction]
        nodeT1[Fc - Main Cutting Force along Vc Direction]
        nodeT2[Ff - Feed Force opposing tool feed motion]
        nodeT3[Fr - Radial Force perpendicular to machined surface]
        nodeT4[R - Resultant Force at chip tool interface]
        nodeT5[Merchant Friction Angle Beta]
        nodeT6[Rake Angle Gamma]
    end
    nodeT1 --- nodeT4
    nodeT2 --- nodeT4
    nodeT4 -.angle between R and Fc.-> nodeT5
    nodeT5 -.friction angle.-> nodeT6
```

## 4.4 Octagonal Ring Dynamometer — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeO1[Incoming Cutting Force Vector Fx Fy Fz] --> nodeO2[Force Splitter Plate Above Octagon]
    nodeO2 --> nodeO3[Octagonal Ring 1 Measures Fx Fy Plane]
    nodeO2 --> nodeO4[Octagonal Ring 2 Measures Fx Fz Plane]
    nodeO2 --> nodeO5[Octagonal Ring 3 Measures Fy Fz Plane]
    nodeO3 --> nodeO6[Four Strain Gauges on Ring 1]
    nodeO4 --> nodeO7[Four Strain Gauges on Ring 2]
    nodeO5 --> nodeO8[Four Strain Gauges on Ring 3]
    nodeO6 --> nodeO9[Bridge Circuit 1 Output Vx]
    nodeO7 --> nodeO10[Bridge Circuit 2 Output Vy]
    nodeO8 --> nodeO11[Bridge Circuit 3 Output Vz]
    nodeO9 --> nodeO12[Amplifier and DAQ System]
    nodeO10 --> nodeO12
    nodeO11 --> nodeO12
    nodeO12 --> nodeO13[Calibrated Force Readout in Newton]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Note:** The three octagonal rings are designed so their sensitive axes are mutually orthogonal. Strain gauge placement at the **strain-concentrated corners** ensures maximum sensitivity, while the symmetric geometry provides inherent temperature compensation.

## 4.5 Wheatstone Bridge — Circuit Topology in Dynamometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeB1[Excitation Voltage Vex] --> nodeB2[Node A Top of Bridge]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3[R1 Strain Gauge Top Left]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB4[R3 Strain Gauge Top Right]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB5[Node B Bottom Left]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB6[Node C Bottom Right]
    nodeB5 --> nodeB7[R2 Strain Gauge]
    nodeB6 --> nodeB8[R4 Strain Gauge]
    nodeB7 --> nodeB9[Node D Ground Reference]
    nodeB8 --> nodeB9
    nodeB5 -.Vout across nodes.-> nodeB6
    nodeB5 --> nodeB10[Differential Amplifier Input Negative]
    nodeB6 --> nodeB11[Differential Amplifier Input Positive]
    nodeB10 --> nodeB12[Amplified Output Vout]
    nodeB11 --> nodeB12
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why differential?** A *single-ended* measurement would also pick up supply-voltage noise, EMI, and thermal EMFs. The *differential* bridge output rejects these common-mode disturbances, giving clean signals even in noisy workshop environments.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define a Tool Force Dynamometer. List any four types of dynamometers used for cutting force measurement.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

A tool force dynamometer is a transducer that measures cutting forces and torques generated during machining by mounting between the tool and the machine tool structure.

Four types:
1. **Mechanical dynamometer** — uses spring deflection and mechanical amplification (e.g., Prony brake for torque).
2. **Hydraulic dynamometer** — measures pressure generated in a hydraulic capsule.
3. **Strain-gauge dynamometer** — uses bonded foil gauges on an elastic body; most common.
4. **Piezoelectric dynamometer** — uses quartz crystals; excellent high-frequency response (up to 50 kHz).
5. *(Bonus)* Capacitive / Inductive (LVDT) / Pneumatic dynamometers.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**State Merchant's equation for shear angle and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

$$\phi = 45° + \frac{\gamma}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $\phi$ | Shear angle (between shear plane & cutting velocity) | 25°–45° |
| $\gamma$ | Rake angle of cutting tool | −10° to +20° |
| $\beta$ | Friction angle at chip-tool interface | 15°–45° |

**Significance:** Higher positive rake → larger $\phi$ → thinner chip → **lower cutting force**. Higher friction → smaller $\phi$ → thicker chip → **higher cutting force, more power, higher temperature**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme**, ESE (End Semester Exam) Part B for a Lab course may include either descriptive theory questions OR numerical/data analysis tasks. Below are two alternatives.

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between cutting force components ($F_c$, $F_f$) and the shear angle $\phi$ in orthogonal cutting, starting from the free-body diagram of the chip. State all assumptions clearly.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Orthogonal cutting (2-D, single-point tool, sharp edge).
2. Continuous chip with constant thickness.
3. Coulomb friction at chip-tool interface: $F = \mu N$.
4. Shear plane is a single flat plane through the uncut chip thickness.
5. No built-up edge (BUE) formation.
6. Tool is rigid; only chip deforms plastically.

**Free-Body Diagram of the Chip:**
Two sets of forces act on the chip:
- **On the shear plane:** Shear force $F_s$ (along shear plane), Normal force $F_n$ (perpendicular).
- **On the rake face:** Friction force $F$ (along rake face), Normal force $N$ (perpendicular).

These two force systems are **equal and opposite** (Newton's third law).

**Equilibrium Equations:**

Resolve along and perpendicular to the shear plane:

$$F_s = N \sin\phi + F \cos\phi \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$F_n = N \cos\phi - F \sin\phi \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Apply Coulomb friction:**

$$F = \mu N = N \tan\beta \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Substitute (iii) into (i) and (ii):**

$$F_s = N (\sin\phi + \tan\beta \cos\phi)$$

$$F_n = N (\cos\phi - \tan\beta \sin\phi)$$

**Take the ratio:**

$$\frac{F_s}{F_n} = \frac{\sin\phi + \tan\beta \cos\phi}{\cos\phi - \tan\beta \sin\phi}$$

**Apply trigonometric identity:** After manipulation (multiply by $\cos\phi$ and simplify), this reduces to:

$$\frac{F_s}{F_n} = \tan(\phi + \beta)$$

**Minimum Energy Principle (Merchant's hypothesis):** For chip formation, shear angle minimizes $F_s$. Differentiating the expression for $F_s$ in terms of $\phi$ and setting $dF_s/d\phi = 0$ gives:

$$2\phi + \beta - \gamma = 90°$$

$$\therefore \boxed{\phi = 45° + \frac{\gamma}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}}$$

**Relating to measured forces:** The forces $F_c$ and $F_f$ measured by the dynamometer relate to the resultant $R$ on the rake face as:

$$F_c = R \cos(\beta - \gamma)$$

$$F_f = R \sin(\beta - \gamma)$$

These are what the **dynamometer measures directly**, and from them, $\beta$ and $R$ can be computed (since $\gamma$ is known from tool geometry).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an **octagonal ring type tool force dynamometer** used for turning force measurement.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Construction:**

The octagonal ring dynamometer consists of:

1. **Top Platform Plate:** Bolted to the machine tool slide (carriage); receives the cutting force vector from the tool.
2. **Three Octagonal Rings:** Three identical octagonal (8-sided) rings, stacked orthogonally between the top platform and the base. Each ring is a closed elastic element with strain-concentrated corners.
3. **Twelve Strain Gauges:** Four foil gauges bonded on each octagonal ring — two on the upper corners (tension) and two on the lower corners (compression) of the loading direction.
4. **Base Plate:** Bolted to the lathe cross-slide.
5. **Cable Harness:** Routes the gauge signals to the amplifier/DAQ.

**Working Principle:**

When the cutting tool experiences forces $F_x, F_y, F_z$, these forces are transmitted through the top platform to the octagonal rings:

- **Ring 1 (XY-plane sensitive):** Measures combined $F_x$ and $F_y$ loads.
- **Ring 2 (XZ-plane sensitive):** Measures combined $F_x$ and $F_z$ loads.
- **Ring 3 (YZ-plane sensitive):** Measures combined $F_y$ and $F_z$ loads.

A mathematical de-coupling matrix (computed during calibration) then resolves the three orthogonal components independently.

**Signal Generation:**

The applied force deforms the octagonal corners, generating tensile/compressive strain. The strain gauges change resistance:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

This resistance change unbalances the Wheatstone bridge, producing an output voltage:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$

The output is **calibrated** (against known static weights) to give force in Newtons directly.

**Advantages:**
- Measures three components simultaneously.
- High rigidity → high natural frequency (suitable for milling dynamics).
- Excellent linearity and low cross-talk (< 2%).

**Limitations:**
- Sensitive to temperature drift — needs thermal compensation.
- Limited overload capacity (typically 150% of rated load).

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **measurement of cutting forces during drilling** using a suitable dynamometer. Discuss how torque and thrust are measured separately and how slip rings or telemetry are used.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Drilling Force System:**

In drilling, the drill experiences:
1. **Cutting Torque $M_c$** about its longitudinal axis (resists spindle rotation).
2. **Thrust Force $F_z$** along the longitudinal axis (resists axial feed).

These two are coupled but must be measured independently for process analysis.

**Measurement Challenge:**

Since the **drill rotates** with the spindle, any sensor mounted on the drill body would experience rotating wires — a major practical problem. Two solutions exist:

**Solution 1 — Slip Ring + Rotary Transformer:**
- Strain gauges bonded on a **stationary dynamometer mounted below the spindle** (in the spindle housing or quill).
- The thrust force is transmitted up through the spindle (axial) and the torque is transmitted via a reaction moment on the dynamometer body.
- For *truly rotating* measurements (e.g., biaxial strain on the drill itself), **slip rings** transfer signals from rotating to stationary side via sliding contacts.
- Modern alternative: **rotary transformer** (wireless inductive coupling) for noise-free signal transfer.

**Solution 2 — Piezoelectric Dynamometer on Table:**
- A piezoelectric 4-component platform dynamometer is placed *beneath* the workpiece table.
- The reactive force and torque transmitted through the workpiece are measured.
- No slip rings needed since the table is stationary.

**Typical Measurement Chain:**

```
Drill → Force/Torque → Strain on Body → Bridge → Charge Amplifier (for piezo)
                                                       ↓
                                              Slip Ring (if rotating)
                                                       ↓
                                              Stationary DAQ System
                                                       ↓
                                              $M_c$ (N·m), $F_z$ (N)
```

**Calculations from Measurements:**

$$M_c \text{ [N·m]} = \text{(measured torque signal)} \times \text{calibration factor}$$

$$F_z \text{ [N]} = \text{(measured thrust signal)} \times \text{calibration factor}$$

**Cutting Power in Drilling:**

$$P_c = M_c \cdot \omega = M_c \cdot \frac{2 \pi n}{60} \quad \text{[W]}$$

**Typical Values (Mild Steel, 12 mm drill, $f=0.15$ mm/rev):**

| Parameter | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- |
| $M_c$ | 5–15 N·m |
| $F_z$ | 1500–3000 N |
| $P_c$ | 0.5–2 kW |

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the cutting force dynamics in **turning, drilling, and milling** with reference to dynamometer selection, mounting, and frequency response requirements.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Turning | Drilling | Milling |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cutting Type** | Continuous | Continuous (with chip curl) | Intermittent (per tooth) |
| **Force Components** | $F_c, F_f, F_r$ | $M_c, F_z$ | $F_c, F_f, F_r$ (time-varying) |
| **Force Profile vs Time** | Quasi-steady | Quasi-steady with run-in/run-out | Periodic with tooth-passing freq $f_t = nN$ |
| **Typical Frequency Content** | DC to ~50 Hz | DC to ~100 Hz | DC to ~2 kHz |
| **Dominant Direction** | $F_c$ (tangential) | $F_z$ and $M_c$ | $F_c$ and $F_r$ |
| **Magnitude Trend** | $F_c > F_f > F_r$ | $F_z \sim 1.2 \times F_c$ | $F_c$ peaks at entry/exit |
| **Tool Engagement** | Constant arc | Full diameter | Varies 0° to 180°+ |

**Dynamometer Selection:**

- **Turning:** Octagonal ring, strain-gauge type, 3-component, **DC to 100 Hz**, mounted on tool post. Lowest frequency requirement.
- **Drilling:** Strain-gauge or piezoelectric, 2-component ($M_c + F_z$), mounted under spindle. If rotating measurement needed → slip ring or telemetry.
- **Milling:** Piezoelectric dynamometer preferred due to **high-frequency response (≥ 5 kHz)**. Mounted on spindle housing or table. Captures tooth-passing vibration and chatter.

**Why Frequency Response Matters:**

The **tooth-passing frequency** in milling:
$$f_t = n \cdot N \quad \text{[Hz]}$$
where $n$ = spindle speed in rev/s and $N$ = number of teeth.

**Example:** At $n = 30$ rev/s (1800 rpm) with $N = 4$ teeth → $f_t = 120$ Hz. But harmonics (3rd, 5th) carry chatter information, so bandwidth up to **1–2 kHz** is needed.

A **turning dynamometer** with only 50 Hz bandwidth would **completely miss** milling dynamics — hence the need for separate, higher-bandwidth dynamometers for milling.

**Mounting Considerations:**

| Operation | Mounting Point | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Turning | Tool post (lathe carriage) | Direct force path, no moving parts |
| Drilling | Quill or spindle housing | Captures axial thrust & torque reaction |
| Milling | Spindle nose or under table | Rigid coupling, high natural frequency |

**Conclusion:** While all three operations use the **same underlying principle** (elastic deformation + strain gauge), the **dynamic regime** dictates the dynamometer specification. Milling demands the most sophisticated (and expensive) instrumentation due to its intermittent, high-frequency nature.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Unit Inconsistency in Power Calculation:** Students frequently mix mm/min with m/s when computing $P_c = F_c \cdot V_c$. The KTU answer key strictly requires **SI units throughout** ($V_c$ in m/s, $F_c$ in N). Marks deducted: typically **2 of 7**.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting the Factor of 2 in Merchant's Equation:** Writing $\phi = 45° + \gamma - \beta$ instead of $45° + \gamma/2 - \beta/2$ is a very common error. Both halves of the angles must be present.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Confusing Drill Thrust with Cutting Force:** Many students assume $F_z = F_c$ for drilling. The correct empirical factor is **$F_z \approx 1.0$–$1.5 \times F_c$** (depending on drill point geometry). This affects drilling torque calculations.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Ignoring Cross-Talk in Dynamometer:** In a 3-component octagonal ring dynamometer, **cross-talk** (force in one axis producing a reading in another) is typically 2–5%. Students who assume perfect orthogonality in derivations lose marks for being unrealistic.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Forgetting Calibration:** When presenting experimental data, students often give *raw voltage* readings instead of calibrated force values. Always multiply by the calibration factor determined using known static loads (e.g., dead weights on a calibration fixture).
>
> **Pitfall 6 — No Temperature Compensation Discussion:** A dynamometer in a workshop sees temperature swings of 5–10°C. Students who fail to mention the **dummy gauge** (in an adjacent arm of the bridge) for thermal compensation lose marks on instrumentation-specific questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 Experiment Highlights**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ A **tool force dynamometer** is a transducer for cutting force and torque in machining.
- ☐ Three operations: **turning, drilling, milling** — each with distinct force signatures.
- ☐ Cutting forces are decomposed into **orthogonal Cartesian components** for analysis.

**Turning Forces:**
- ☐ Three components: $F_c$ (tangential, largest), $F_f$ (axial), $F_r$ (radial, smallest).
- ☐ Power: $P_c = F_c \cdot V_c$; Resultant: $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2}$.

**Drilling Forces:**
- ☐ Two components: **Cutting torque $M_c$** and **Thrust force $F_z$**.
- ☐ Torque scales with $D^2$; thrust scales with $D$.
- ☐ Power: $P_c = M_c \cdot \omega$.

**Milling Forces:**
- ☐ Same components as turning but **periodic** with tooth-passing frequency.
- ☐ Frequency response of dynamometer must be ≥ 2 kHz.

**Merchant's Theory:**
- ☐ **Shear angle equation:** $\phi = 45° + \gamma/2 - \beta/2$ (memorize!).
- ☐ Higher $\gamma$ (positive rake) → lower force; higher $\beta$ (friction) → higher force.

**Strain Gauge Dynamometer:**
- ☐ Octagonal ring design is industry standard for 3-component measurement.
- ☐ Output voltage: $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$.
- ☐ Calibration equation: $F = (V_o \cdot A \cdot E) / (V_{ex} \cdot GF)$.
- ☐ Wheatstone bridge gives **common-mode noise rejection** and **temperature compensation**.

**Numerical Must-Knows:**
- ☐ $V_c = \pi D n$ (mm/min if $D$ in mm, $n$ in rpm).
- ☐ $\omega = 2\pi n / 60$ (rad/s from rpm).
- ☐ MRR (turning): $Q = b \cdot t_1 \cdot V_c$ (mm³/s).
- ☐ Specific cutting energy: $U_c = F_c / (b \cdot t_1)$ in J/mm³.

**Experimental Procedure Highlights:**
- ☐ Always **zero the dynamometer** before each cut.
- ☐ Calibrate using **known static loads** on the dynamometer body.
- ☐ Record **spindle speed, feed, depth of cut** alongside force data.
- ☐ Plot **force vs time** AND **force vs feed / speed** for analysis.
- ☐ Identify chatter frequencies from **FFT** of dynamic force signals.

**Instrumentation Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ☐ Watch **unit conversions** (mm/min ↔ m/s).
- ☐ Account for **drill point geometry** (118° standard point angle affects $F_z$).
- ☐ Mention **cross-talk** and **temperature compensation** in answers.
- ☐ Always include **calibration factor** in calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Load Cell
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Load Cell

# Module Experiment 2: Calibration of Load Cell

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **Load Cell** is a precision **force-transduction** device that converts an applied mechanical load (force, weight, or mass) into a measurable electrical signal, most commonly a voltage output proportional to the deforming strain on an internal elastic element. The most prevalent variant used in industrial metrology is the **Strain Gauge Based Compression/Tension Load Cell**.

**Calibration** of a load cell is the formal, traceable procedure of establishing the mathematical and statistical relationship between the **input quantity** (the applied load, expressed in **kgf**, **N**, or **lbf**) and the **output quantity** (the electrical signal, expressed in **mV/V** or **V**) under controlled environmental and reference loading conditions, as prescribed by standards such as **IS 12418**, **ASTM E74**, or **OIML R60**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMRL308):** Students must record calibration readings for *increasing* and *decreasing* loads, compute **hysteresis**, **non-linearity**, **repeatability**, and **creep**, and determine the **calibration constant (K)** with engineering units. The experiment directly maps to **CO2 (Calibrate transducers)** and **CO3 (Analyse measurement errors)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a bathroom weighing scale. When you step on it, an internal metal block (the **elastic element**) bends ever so slightly under your weight. Attached to this block are tiny wires (**strain gauges**) whose electrical resistance changes as they stretch. The scale's electronic brain measures this resistance change, does some math, and displays "72.5 kg" on the LCD. Now imagine the factory engineer needs to *verify* whether that displayed value is actually correct — so he hangs certified weights of exactly 50 kg, 100 kg, 200 kg from the load cell and checks what voltage it spits out. This verification process, repeated across the operating range in both directions, is precisely what **calibration** means.

The load cell is the **"mechanical-to-electrical translator"**, and calibration is the **"language dictionary"** that ensures the translation is accurate, linear, and repeatable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Physical Principle:** Load cells exploit **Hooke's Law** ($F = k \cdot x$) and the **piezoresistive effect** of strain gauges. The output voltage is linear with applied load only when the elastic element operates within its elastic limit.

### 1.3 Standards and Reference Constants

| Standard | Governing Body | Relevance |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **OIML R60** | International Organization of Legal Metrology | Accuracy classes for load cells (A, B, C, D) |
| **ASTM E74** | American Society for Testing and Materials | Force calibration of load cells |
| **IS 12418** | Bureau of Indian Standards | Indian standard for load cell testing |
| **g = 9.80665 m/s²** | CGPM (codified value) | Standard gravity for kgf ↔ N conversion |

> [!WARNING]
> Always convert kgf to N using $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$ — never use the approximate 9.81 or 9.8 in board examinations unless explicitly stated, as KTU examiners check this.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Strain Gauge Load Cell

The load cell comprises four functional sub-systems operating in cascade:

1. **Elastic Element (Spring Member):** A precisely machined steel/aluminum body (column, ring, or S-beam) that deflects proportionally to the applied load. The strain $\varepsilon$ generated on its surface is given by:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$
where $\sigma$ is mechanical stress, $E$ is Young's Modulus, $F$ is applied force, and $A$ is cross-sectional area.

2. **Strain Gauge Assembly:** Four foil gauges bonded to the elastic element, configured as a **Wheatstone Bridge**. Two gauges measure **tensile strain** (increase in resistance) and two measure **compressive strain** (decrease in resistance), producing additive output.

3. **Excitation Source:** A regulated DC voltage $V_{ex}$ (typically **5 V, 10 V, or 12 V**) energizes the bridge.

4. **Signal Conditioning Unit:** Amplifies the millivolt-level output to a standardized signal (e.g., **0–10 V**, **4–20 mA**).

## 2.2 The Wheatstone Bridge — Heart of the Load Cell

The four strain gauges $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$ form a bridge. The differential output voltage $V_o$ is:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4} \right)$$

When all four gauges are active (one per arm), and each experiences $\pm \varepsilon$ strain with gauge factor $G_F$:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master equation** of load cell output. A typical 350 Ω strain gauge has $G_F \approx 2.0$. For a full-scale strain of $\varepsilon \approx 0.001$ (1000 microstrain) and $V_{ex} = 10$ V, the output is approximately $V_o = 20$ mV — hence the universal unit **mV/V** (millivolts per volt of excitation).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Engineering Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{F}{A \cdot E}$ | dimensionless (or $\mu\varepsilon$) |
| 2 | Bridge Output | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ | V |
| 3 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{V_o}{V_{ex}} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ | mV/V |
| 4 | Load from Voltage | $F = \dfrac{V_o}{V_{ex}} \cdot \dfrac{A \cdot E}{G_F}$ | N |
| 5 | Force from Mass | $F = m \cdot g$ (with $g = 9.80665$ m/s²) | N |
| 6 | Calibration Constant | $K = \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta F}$ | mV/V per N |
| 7 | Non-linearity (% FS) | $\text{NL} = \dfrac{\vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert_{max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | % |
| 8 | Hysteresis (% FS) | $\text{Hys} = \dfrac{\vert V_{inc} - V_{dec} \vert_{max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | Repeatability (% FS) | $\text{Rep} = \dfrac{\pm \Delta V_{max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | % |
| 10 | Creep | $C = \dfrac{V_t - V_0}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ over fixed time | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **% FS** stands for "percent of Full Scale output." In all error computations, divide by the full-scale (maximum) output voltage, not by the instantaneous reading.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Load cells are the **backbone of weighing technology** across industries:

- **Weighbridges & Truck Scales** in logistics (legal-for-trade accuracy class OIML C3 to C6).
- **Process Control** in batch mixing (chemical, food, pharma industries).
- **Aerospace Testing** — rocket thrust stands, aircraft structural load verification.
- **Civil Engineering** — structural health monitoring of bridges using embedded load cells.
- **Medical Equipment** — hospital beds with patient-weight monitoring, infusion pump force sensing.
- **Robotics** — force-feedback grippers, haptic interfaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bridge Output from Four Active Gauges

Consider a Wheatstone bridge with all four arms active. The nominal resistance of each gauge is $R$. Under load, the resistance changes as:

$$R_1 = R + \Delta R_1, \quad R_2 = R - \Delta R_2, \quad R_3 = R + \Delta R_3, \quad R_4 = R - \Delta R_4$$

The output voltage expression, derived via Thevenin superposition, becomes:

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \left( \frac{\Delta R_1}{R} - \frac{\Delta R_2}{R} - \frac{\Delta R_3}{R} + \frac{\Delta R_4}{R} \right)$$

Substituting $\frac{\Delta R_i}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon_i$:

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G_F}{4} \cdot \left( \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_3 + \varepsilon_4 \right)$$

For a load cell designed such that opposite gauges experience equal and opposite strain ($\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_4 = \varepsilon$ and $\varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_3 = -\varepsilon$), the equation **collapses** to:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$$

This proves that a four-arm active bridge is **four times more sensitive** than a single-gauge quarter-bridge configuration.

## 3.2 Derivation: Calibration Constant $K$

Let the experimental data record voltage $V_i$ for applied load $F_i$ (in Newtons). Using **least-squares linear regression** (the KTU-recommended method), the calibration constant is the **slope** of the best-fit straight line:

$$K = \frac{n \sum_{i=1}^{n}(F_i V_i) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}{n \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i^2 - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i\right)^2}$$

and the intercept $C$ (ideally $\approx 0$):

$$C = \frac{\sum V_i - K \sum F_i}{n}$$

The calibrated output equation is therefore:

$$\boxed{V_{calibrated}(F) = K \cdot F + C}$$

with units of $V/N$ for $K$.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 500 kg capacity load cell is excited with $V_{ex} = 10$ V. The following readings are recorded (increasing load):

| Load (kg) | Load $F$ (N) | Output $V_o$ (mV) |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| 0 | 0 | 0.05 |
| 100 | 980.67 | 3.92 |
| 200 | 1961.33 | 7.85 |
| 300 | 2942.00 | 11.74 |
| 400 | 3922.66 | 15.68 |
| 500 | 4903.33 | 19.55 |

**Step 1:** Compute sensitivity at full load.

$$S_{FS} = \frac{V_o}{V_{ex}} = \frac{19.55 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ V}} = 1.955 \text{ mV/V}$$

**Step 2:** Determine calibration constant $K$ using two-point method (ideal case, linearity assumed):

$$K = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta F} = \frac{19.55 - 0.05}{4903.33 - 0} \text{ mV/N}$$

$$K = \frac{19.50 \text{ mV}}{4903.33 \text{ N}} = 3.977 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mV/N}$$

**Step 3:** Express sensitivity in mV/V per kg.

$$K_{kg} = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot K}{1000} = \frac{10 \times 3.977 \times 10^{-3}}{1} = 39.77 \text{ mV per V per 1000 N} = 1.955 \text{ mV/V at FS}$$

**Step 4:** Compute non-linearity (max deviation from best-fit line).

$$\text{NL\%} = \frac{\max \vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100$$

If the best-fit line is $V_{ideal} = 3.985 \times 10^{-3} \cdot F + 0.03$, then at $F = 2942$ N:

$$V_{actual} = 11.74, \quad V_{ideal} = 11.78$$

$$\text{Deviation} = \vert 11.74 - 11.78 \vert = 0.04 \text{ mV}$$

$$\text{NL\%} = \frac{0.04}{19.55} \times 100 = 0.205\%$$

This is well within industrial grade (typically $< 0.05\%$ for Class A).

## 3.4 Hysteresis Computation (Two-Way Calibration)

Record $V_{inc}$ (increasing load) and $V_{dec}$ (decreasing load) at the *same* load points. Hysteresis error at each point:

$$\text{Hys}(F) = \frac{\vert V_{inc}(F) - V_{dec}(F) \vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

The **maximum** of $\text{Hys}(F)$ across all load points is reported as the cell's hysteresis specification.

## 3.5 Creep Calculation

Apply full-scale load and record output $V_0$ at $t = 0$ and $V_t$ at $t = T$ (typically $T = 30$ min):

$$\text{Creep\%} = \frac{V_t - V_0}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always specify the **time interval** ($T$) when reporting creep — examiners explicitly check this. A common mistake is stating creep without units or duration, losing 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Load Cell Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Mechanical Load F in N]:::load
    nodeB[Elastic Element Strain Body]:::mech
    nodeC[Four Active Strain Gauges Wheatstone Bridge]:::sensor
    nodeD[Excitation Source Vex 10V DC]:::elec
    nodeE[Differential Output Vo in mV]:::elec
    nodeF[Signal Amplifier and Filter]:::elec
    nodeG[ADC and Microcontroller]:::proc
    nodeH[Digital Display Mass in kg]:::out
    nodeI[Calibration Reference Weights]:::ref

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeI -. verifiable standard .-> nodeA
    nodeI -. compares against .-> nodeH

    classDef load fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#d39e00,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#d4edda,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,color:#000
    classDef ref fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Wheatstone Bridge — Schematic Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeP[Excitation +Vex]:::top
    node1((R1)):::gauges
    node2((R2)):::gauges
    node3((R3)):::gauges
    node4((R4)):::gauges
    nodeN[Excitation GND]:::top
    nodeVo[Output Vo Differential]:::out
    nodeVG[Reference Ground]:::top

    nodeP --- node1
    node1 --- nodeVo
    node1 --- node3
    node3 --- nodeN

    nodeP --- node2
    node2 --- nodeVo
    node2 --- node4
    node4 --- nodeN

    classDef top fill:#fff,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef gauges fill:#d4edda,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram, **R1 and R3** form the left arm (in series between $+V_{ex}$ and GND), and **R2 and R4** form the right arm. The differential output $V_o$ is measured between the midpoints of the two arms.

## 4.3 Calibration Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1[Step 1: Mount Load Cell Vertically on Test Rig]:::step
    nodeS2[Step 2: Connect Excitation 10V DC and DVM]:::step
    nodeS3[Step 3: Zero Balance — No Load, Adjust to 0 mV]:::step
    nodeS4[Step 4: Apply Increasing Loads 0 to FS in 5 to 10 Steps]:::step
    nodeS5[Step 5: Record Output Voltage at Each Step]:::step
    nodeS6[Step 6: Apply Decreasing Loads FS to 0 in Same Steps]:::step
    nodeS7[Step 7: Repeat Cycle 2 to 3 Times for Repeatability]:::step
    nodeS8[Step 8: Plot V vs F Graph and Best Fit Line]:::step
    nodeS9[Step 9: Compute K, NL, Hysteresis, Repeatability]:::step
    nodeS10[Step 10: Report Calibration Certificate with Units]:::step

    nodeS1 --> nodeS2 --> nodeS3 --> nodeS4 --> nodeS5 --> nodeS6 --> nodeS7 --> nodeS8 --> nodeS9 --> nodeS10

    classDef step fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Strain Gauge Bonding Topology on Elastic Element

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeEl[Elastic Column Body]:::body
    nodeG1((G1: Tensile +epsilon)):::gauge
    nodeG2((G2: Compressive -epsilon)):::gauge
    nodeG3((G3: Compressive -epsilon)):::gauge
    nodeG4((G4: Tensile +epsilon)):::gauge

    nodeEl --- nodeG1
    nodeEl --- nodeG2
    nodeEl --- nodeG3
    nodeEl --- nodeG4

    nodeG1 --- nodeG4
    nodeG2 --- nodeG3

    classDef body fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#d39e00,color:#000
    classDef gauge fill:#d4edda,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define a load cell. List any two types of load cells.**

**Model Answer:**

A **load cell** is a transducer that converts a mechanical force/load into a measurable electrical signal (typically voltage), proportional to the applied load through the elastic deformation of an internal strain-sensing element.

Two types of load cells:
1. **Strain Gauge Load Cell** — uses bonded foil gauges in a Wheatstone bridge configuration.
2. **Hydraulic Load Cell** — uses a piston-cylinder filled with fluid; load is measured via fluid pressure.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Pneumatic Load Cell, Piezoelectric Load Cell, Capacitive Load Cell.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Define the term "calibration" as applied to transducers.**

**Model Answer:**

Calibration is the experimental procedure of comparing the output of a transducer against a **traceable reference standard** across its operating range, under specified environmental conditions, to determine its accuracy, linearity, sensitivity, and error characteristics. The output is a calibration constant that mathematically relates input quantity to output signal, typically with stated uncertainty.

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a strain gauge based load cell. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** During calibration of a 1000 kg load cell, the following readings were obtained with $V_{ex} = 10$ V:

| Load (kg) | 0 | 200 | 400 | 600 | 800 | 1000 |
|-----------|---|-----|-----|-----|-----|------|
| Output (mV) | 0.0 | 7.85 | 15.70 | 23.60 | 31.45 | 39.30 |

Plot the calibration curve and determine:
(i) The calibration constant in mV/V per kg. **(3 Marks)**
(ii) The non-linearity as a percentage of full scale. **(4 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

A strain-gauge load cell consists of:

(i) **Elastic Element:** A high-grade stainless-steel or aluminum body (column, ring, or S-shape) that deforms elastically under load. The strain $\varepsilon = F/(A \cdot E)$ must remain within the linear elastic regime (typically $< 1000 \mu\varepsilon$).

(ii) **Strain Gauge Assembly:** Four foil strain gauges, each of nominal resistance **350 Ω** and gauge factor $G_F \approx 2.0$, are bonded to the elastic element at precisely machined locations — two aligned axially (experience tensile strain) and two aligned transversely (experience compressive strain due to Poisson effect).

(iii) **Wheatstone Bridge:** The four gauges form the four arms of a Wheatstone bridge. A regulated excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ (5–10 V DC) is applied across the bridge.

**Working Principle:**

When load $F$ is applied, the elastic element deforms. The axial gauges experience strain $+\varepsilon$ (resistance increases), while the transverse gauges experience $-\nu\varepsilon$ (Poisson effect, resistance decreases, where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio of the base material). The resulting bridge imbalance produces a differential output voltage:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot (1 + \nu) \cdot \varepsilon$$

This mV-level signal is amplified, digitized via an ADC, and displayed as mass.

**Part (b) — Calculations (7 Marks):**

**Step (i):** Compute calibration constant.

Using two-point linear approximation (slope between 0 and FS):

$$K = \frac{V_{FS} - V_0}{m_{FS} - m_0} = \frac{39.30 - 0.0}{1000 - 0} = 0.0393 \text{ mV/kg}$$

Sensitivity in mV/V per kg:

$$S = \frac{K}{V_{ex}} = \frac{0.0393}{10} = 3.93 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mV/(V·kg)}$$

Or equivalently, full-scale sensitivity is $3.93$ mV/V at 1000 kg.

**Step (ii):** Compute non-linearity.

Best-fit linear equation (ideal): $V_{ideal} = 0.0393 \cdot m$

| Load (kg) | Actual (mV) | Ideal (mV) | Deviation (mV) |
|-----------|-------------|------------|----------------|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 200 | 7.85 | 7.86 | 0.01 |
| 400 | 15.70 | 15.72 | 0.02 |
| 600 | 23.60 | 23.58 | 0.02 |
| 800 | 31.45 | 31.44 | 0.01 |
| 1000 | 39.30 | 39.30 | 0.00 |

Maximum deviation = 0.02 mV

$$\text{NL\%} = \frac{0.02}{39.30} \times 100 = 0.051\% \text{ FS}$$

This corresponds to an **OIML Class C3** load cell accuracy.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**a)** Explain the procedure to calibrate a load cell. List the instruments required. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Define and explain with formulas: (i) Hysteresis, (ii) Repeatability, (iii) Creep. A load cell shows a creep of 0.08% FS over 30 minutes at full load. Comment on its suitability for precision weighing. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Calibration Procedure (7 Marks):**

**Instruments Required:**
- Load cell under test (unknown)
- Calibrated **reference weights** (traceable to NABL/NPL standards) covering 0% to 100% FS in 5–10 steps.
- **Hydraulic/Mechanical loading frame** or dead-weight tester.
- Regulated DC excitation source (5/10 V).
- Precision **Digital Voltmeter** (resolution $< 10 \mu$V).
- Temperature indicator (thermometer/hygrometer).

**Procedure (Step-wise):**

1. **Mounting:** Mount the load cell on the test rig following the manufacturer's axis-of-loading guidelines. Allow **thermal stabilization** (typically 15–30 min).

2. **Electrical Connection:** Connect the excitation source, output leads, and sense leads (if 6-wire configuration) to the indicator.

3. **Zero Balance:** With no load applied, adjust the indicator to read exactly **0 mV/V** (or 0 kg equivalent).

4. **Increasing Load:** Apply reference weights in incremental steps (e.g., 0, 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, 100% of FS). Record output voltage $V_{inc}$ at each step. Allow 30 s settling time at each step.

5. **Decreasing Load:** Remove weights in reverse order, recording $V_{dec}$ at the same load points.

6. **Repeatability Test:** Repeat Steps 4–5 two more times (3 cycles total) without re-zeroing.

7. **Creep Test:** Apply 100% FS load and record output at $t = 0$ and $t = 30$ min.

8. **Data Analysis:** Plot calibration curve, compute all error metrics.

**Part (b) — Definitions and Creep Commentary (7 Marks):**

**(i) Hysteresis:** The maximum difference in output at the same applied load when approached from increasing versus decreasing directions, expressed as a percentage of full-scale output:

$$\text{Hys\%} = \frac{\max \vert V_{inc}(F) - V_{dec}(F) \vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

It arises due to internal friction, molecular re-arrangements in the elastic element, and bond-slip at strain-gauge interfaces.

**(ii) Repeatability:** The maximum variation in output for the same load, when measured repeatedly under identical conditions (same approach direction, same observer, same instrument):

$$\text{Rep\%} = \frac{\pm \Delta V_{max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

**(iii) Creep:** The change in output signal over time when the load is held constant at a fixed value (typically full scale):

$$\text{Creep\%} = \frac{V_t - V_0}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

**Commentary on Creep = 0.08% FS in 30 min:**

A creep of 0.08% over 30 minutes exceeds the OIML R60 limit for accuracy Class C3 (which mandates $< 0.025\%$ over 30 min). Therefore, the load cell is **NOT suitable for high-precision commercial weighing** (e.g., jewellery, pharmaceutical, or legal-for-trade applications). However, it may be acceptable for **industrial process control** (e.g., tank weighing, silo-level monitoring) where Class C3–C4 accuracy is sufficient. To improve, the cell should be pre-loaded and aged for 8–24 hours before calibration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $(1+\nu)$** in the bridge output equation when only axial gauges are considered — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using $g = 9.8$ m/s²** instead of 9.80665 m/s² in mass-to-force conversion — marked as an error.
> 3. **Reporting NL, Hysteresis, Repeatability as absolute values in mV** instead of % FS — incorrect unit specification.
> 4. **Not stating the time duration** when reporting creep — partial marks deducted.
> 5. **Skipping units** in the final calibration constant (must be mV/V per kg or mV/N with explicit units).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **load cell** converts force into an electrical signal using an elastic element and strain gauges arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge**.
- The **fundamental output equation** is $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ (for 4 active arms).
- **Calibration** establishes the input-output relationship using traceable reference weights.
- Always use $g = 9.80665$ m/s² for kgf ↔ N conversion.
- Full-scale sensitivity is expressed in **mV/V** (millivolts per volt of excitation).
- Compute **four error metrics** during calibration: **Non-linearity, Hysteresis, Repeatability, and Creep** — each as a **% of Full Scale**.
- Calibration constant $K$ has units of **mV/N** or **mV/kg**; it is the slope of the best-fit line.
- Always record readings for **increasing and decreasing loads** to evaluate hysteresis.
- Apply load in **5–10 equal increments** between 0 and 100% FS for adequate resolution.
- The four-arm active bridge is **4× more sensitive** than a single-gauge quarter bridge.
- **OIML R60** defines accuracy classes: A, B, C, D (D being highest precision).
- Creep must always be reported with the **time interval** (typically 30 minutes).
- Mount the load cell in its specified orientation (axial alignment is critical to avoid side-load errors).
- Provide thermal stabilization time of **15–30 minutes** before calibration begins.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Air Humidity Using Sling Psychrometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Air Humidity Using Sling Psychrometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
