| **0.006 < Ra ≤ 0.02** | 0.08 | **0.08** | 0.4 |
| **0.02 < Ra ≤ 0.1** | 0.25 | **0.25** | 1.25 |
| **0.1 < Ra ≤ 2.0** | 0.8 | **0.8** | 4.0 |
| **2.0 < Ra ≤ 10.0** | 2.5 | **2.5** | 12.5 |
| **10.0 < Ra ≤ 80.0** | 8.0 | **8.0** | 40.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaluation Length (ln) = 5 × Sampling Length (lr) = 5 × λc**
> 
> The first and last sampling lengths are discarded (to avoid end effects), and Ra is calculated as the mean of the **remaining 3 sampling lengths** (or 5 if no end effects exist, depending on the standard version).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ra** | $Ra = \dfrac{1}{lr} \displaystyle\int_{0}^{lr} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | **µm** | Arithmetic Mean Deviation of the profile |
| **Rq (Rrms)** | $Rq = \sqrt{ \dfrac{1}{lr} \displaystyle\int_{0}^{lr} y(x)^2 \, dx }$ | **µm** | Root-Mean-Square (RMS) Deviation |
| **Rz** | $Rz = \dfrac{R_{z1} + R_{z2} + R_{z3} + R_{z4} + R_{z5}}{5}$ | **µm** | Mean of 5 highest peak-to-valley values |
| **Rt** | $Rt = Rp_{max} - Rv_{max}$ | **µm** | Maximum peak-to-valley height over evaluation length |
| **Sampling length (lr)** | $lr = \lambda_c$ | mm | The cut-off wavelength used |
| **Evaluation length (ln)** | $ln = 5 \cdot \lambda_c$ | mm | Total length over which Ra is averaged |
| **Stylus tip radius (r)** | $2 \, \mu m$ or $5 \, \mu m$ | µm | Standard tip geometry |
| **Static measuring force** | $\le 1.5 \, mN$ | mN | Force on the stylus to avoid surface damage |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL SYNTAX RULE FOR KTU:** Do **NOT** use the vertical bar `\vert` in the table itself, but in your **derivation steps (Section 3)**, always use `\vert y(x) \vert` for absolute value notation. The `|` character breaks markdown table parsers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Why Surface Finish Matters |
|---|---|
| **Automotive (Cylinder liners, bearings)** | Oil retention, low friction, long life |
| **Aerospace (Turbine blades)** | Fatigue life, aerodynamic efficiency |
| **Hydraulics (Seal surfaces)** | Sealing integrity, leak prevention |
| **Optical components (Lenses, mirrors)** | Light scattering, image quality |
| **Semiconductor wafers** | Film adhesion, electrical contact |
| **Medical implants (Hip joints)** | Biocompatibility, wear rate |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Ra (Arithmetic Mean Roughness)

The parameter **Ra** is defined as the **arithmetic mean of the absolute values of the ordinate (height) of the profile**, measured from the mean line, over one sampling length.

### Step 1: Define the Mean Line

The mean line is a reference line parallel to the general direction of the profile, such that the sum of the areas enclosed between the profile and this line (above and below) are equal — i.e., the mean ordinate value is zero:

$$\int_{0}^{lr} y(x) \, dx = 0$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the mathematical condition for the "centered" profile. The instrument's filter enforces this condition automatically by removing the DC offset (the bulk shape of the part).

### Step 2: Express the Mean Absolute Deviation

For a continuous profile over the sampling length $lr$, the **arithmetic mean of the absolute ordinate values** is given by the integral form:

$$Ra = \frac{1}{lr} \int_{0}^{lr} \left\vert y(x) \right\vert \, dx$$

> **Conversion logic:** We take the absolute value because we want the **magnitude** of deviation, regardless of whether the profile point is above (peak) or below (valley) the mean line. Dividing by $lr$ converts the total area-under-the-absolute-profile into an average height.

### Step 3: Discretize for Digital Sampling

In a real digital instrument, the continuous profile $y(x)$ is sampled at $N$ discrete points. The continuous integral becomes a discrete summation:

$$Ra \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\vert y_i \right\vert$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the Riemann sum approximation. The instrument's A/D converter typically samples at 1–4 µm intervals, giving $N$ on the order of thousands per sampling length.

### Step 4: Full Evaluation Length (5-Sample Average)

Since ISO 4288 uses 5 sampling lengths and the first/last are usually excluded for end-effect removal (in some standards), the final reported Ra is:

$$Ra = \frac{1}{5 \cdot lr} \int_{0}^{5 \cdot lr} \left\vert y(x) \right\vert \, dx$$

Or, expressed in discrete form for **n = 5** consecutive sampling lengths:

$$Ra = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{j=1}^{5} Ra_j$$

> **Conversion logic:** Each $Ra_j$ is the average over the $j$-th sampling length; the final Ra is the mean of these five values to reduce local noise.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

### **Problem:**
A stylus profilometer with a cut-off of $\lambda_c = 0.8 \, mm$ is used to measure a ground steel surface. The output voltage from the LVDT (sensitivity = **8 mV/µm**) was sampled at 12 equally-spaced points across one sampling length. The sampled voltages (in mV) are:

$$[\, 0.4, \; -1.2, \; 0.8, \; -0.3, \; 1.1, \; -0.9, \; 0.2, \; -0.5, \; 0.7, \; -1.0, \; 0.3, \; -0.6 \,]$$

**Calculate Ra in µm.**

### **Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Convert each voltage reading to height $y_i$ (µm) using sensitivity $k = 8 \, mV/\mu m$:

$$\begin{aligned}
y_i &= \frac{V_i}{k} \\
y_1 &= 0.4 / 8 = 0.050 \, \mu m \\
y_2 &= -1.2 / 8 = -0.150 \, \mu m \\
y_3 &= 0.8 / 8 = 0.100 \, \mu m \\
y_4 &= -0.3 / 8 = -0.0375 \, \mu m \\
y_5 &= 1.1 / 8 = 0.1375 \, \mu m \\
y_6 &= -0.9 / 8 = -0.1125 \, \mu m \\
y_7 &= 0.2 / 8 = 0.025 \, \mu m \\
y_8 &= -0.5 / 8 = -0.0625 \, \mu m \\
y_9 &= 0.7 / 8 = 0.0875 \, \mu m \\
y_{10} &= -1.0 / 8 = -0.125 \, \mu m \\
y_{11} &= 0.3 / 8 = 0.0375 \, \mu m \\
y_{12} &= -0.6 / 8 = -0.075 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Verify the mean is approximately zero (mean line condition):

$$\bar{y} = \frac{1}{12} \sum_{i=1}^{12} y_i = \frac{-0.175}{12} \approx -0.0146 \, \mu m \approx 0$$

(Within instrument noise — acceptable.)

**Step 3:** Compute the absolute values and sum:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum \left\vert y_i \right\vert = & \; 0.050 + 0.150 + 0.100 + 0.0375 + 0.1375 + 0.1125 \\
& + 0.025 + 0.0625 + 0.0875 + 0.125 + 0.0375 + 0.075 \\
= & \; 1.000 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compute Ra:

$$Ra = \frac{1}{N} \sum \left\vert y_i \right\vert = \frac{1.000}{12} = 0.0833 \, \mu m$$

**Step 5:** If the standard requires averaging over 5 sampling lengths and all are similar:

$$Ra_{final} \approx 0.08 \, \mu m \quad (\text{rounded to 2 decimal places})$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $Ra \approx 0.08 \, \mu m$, this falls in the range $0.02 < Ra \le 0.1$, so the cut-off $\lambda_c = 0.25 \, mm$ is the *correct* one per ISO 4288. The original cut-off of 0.8 mm would be **incorrect** for this surface, and using the wrong cut-off is a common lab error.

## 3.3 Derivation of Rz (Mean Peak-to-Valley Height)

The parameter **Rz** is the mean of the **5 largest peak-to-valley heights** $Z_i = Rp_i - Rv_i$ measured over a single sampling length:

$$Rz = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} \left( Rp_i - Rv_i \right)$$

> **Conversion logic:** For each of the 5 highest peaks in the sampling length, we find the corresponding valley directly below (within the same local region), and the difference gives one peak-to-valley value. The mean of 5 such values gives Rz.

## 3.4 Rq (RMS) — For Reference

The Root-Mean-Square parameter is often used because it is **more sensitive to occasional high peaks** (e.g., scratches):

$$Rq = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i^{2} }$$

The empirical relationship for most machined surfaces is:

$$Rq \approx 1.11 \cdot Ra$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Stylus Profilometer

The figure below shows the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a typical stylus-type surface roughness measuring instrument, mapping the signal flow from mechanical contact to digital readout.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[WORKPIECE SURFACE] -->|Mechanical Contact| B[DIAMOND STYLUS Tip radius 2 or 5 um]
    B -->|Vertical Micro-displacement| C[PIVOT ARM Assembly]
    C -->|Core Movement| D[LVDT PICKUP Primary plus two Secondaries]
    D -->|AC Output mV| E[OSCILLATOR Excitation 5 kHz]
    E -->|Carrier Excitation| D
    D -->|Modulated AC Signal| F[PREAMPLIFIER Gain stage]
    F -->|Amplified Signal| G[PHASE SENSITIVE DEMODULATOR]
    G -->|DC Signal proportional to y| H[GAUSSIAN LOW PASS FILTER Cut off lambda c]
    H -->|Filtered Roughness Signal| I[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER ADC]
    I -->|Digital Samples| J[MICROCONTROLLER DSP unit]
    J -->|Calculated Ra Rz Rq| K[LCD DIGITAL DISPLAY]
    J -->|D to A Converted| L[CHART RECORDER Profile Trace]
    M[DRIVE MOTOR Constant Speed 0.5 mm per sec] -->|Traverse| A
    J -->|Motor Speed Control| M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Start from the left (workpiece → stylus), follow the mechanical displacement up to the LVDT, then trace the electrical signal path through amplification, demodulation, filtering, digitization, and finally to the display. The drive motor provides the horizontal scanning motion, controlled by the same microcontroller that processes the data.

## 4.2 Signal Flow — Stylus to Display

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stylus rides on surface] --> A2[LVDT produces AC voltage]
    A2 --> A3[Demodulator gives DC]
    A3 --> A4[Filter lambda c removes waviness]
    A4 --> A5[ADC samples the profile]
    A5 --> A6[Math unit computes Ra]
    A6 --> A7[Display shows value in um]
```

## 4.3 Components Layout of a Typical Stylus Profilometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Mechanical Assembly]
        A1[Skid Shoe]
        A2[Stylus Arm]
        A3[Diamond Tip]
    end
    subgraph B[Transducer Stage]
        B1[LVDT Core]
        B2[Coils]
    end
    subgraph C[Electronic Stage]
        C1[Oscillator]
        C2[Amplifier]
        C3[Filter lambda c]
        C4[ADC]
        C5[Processor]
    end
    subgraph D[Output Stage]
        D1[Digital LCD]
        D2[Recorder]
    end
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    C1 --> B2
    B2 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> D1
    C5 --> D2
```

## 4.4 Cross-Section of Stylus on Workpiece (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Surface[Workpiece Surface Cross Section]
        S1[Peak 1] --> S2[Valley 1] --> S3[Peak 2] --> S4[Valley 2] --> S5[Peak 3]
    end
    subgraph Stylus
        ST[Stylus Arm]
        TIP((Diamond Tip r = 2 um))
    end
    TIP -.->|Contact| S1
    TIP -.->|Contact| S2
    TIP -.->|Contact| S3
    ST --> TIP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of the stylus cross-section:** The stylus tip radius is **smaller than the typical peak-to-valley spacing** of a fine surface, so it accurately follows the profile. However, if the surface is *smoother* than the tip radius itself, the tip cannot resolve the profile — this is the **stylus tip limit** of the instrument.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define the term "Surface Roughness" and mention any two roughness parameters used to quantify it.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Surface roughness** is the measure of the closely spaced, small-scale irregularities present on a manufactured surface, resulting from the machining process or material deformation. It is described as the finer component of surface texture (with shorter wavelengths), as opposed to waviness (longer wavelengths).
> 
> **Two commonly used roughness parameters (per ISO 4287):**
> 1. **Ra** — Arithmetic Mean Deviation of the profile from the mean line (most widely used).
> 2. **Rz** — Mean Peak-to-Valley height, calculated as the average of the 5 highest peak-to-valley measurements within the sampling length.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is the function of the cut-off filter (λc) in a stylus-type profilometer? Why is its selection important?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **cut-off filter (λc filter)** is a **phase-corrected low-pass Gaussian filter** built into the electronic signal conditioning stage of the profilometer. Its function is to **separate the high-frequency roughness component from the low-frequency waviness** in the measured surface profile.
> 
> The filter removes all components of the signal whose wavelength is **longer than λc**, ensuring that only genuine roughness (and not geometric form errors or waviness) contributes to the Ra/Rz calculation.
> 
> **Importance of correct selection:**
> - If λc is **too small**, waviness contaminates the reading and Ra is overestimated.
> - If λc is **too large**, fine roughness details are averaged out and Ra is underestimated.
> - **ISO 4288** prescribes standard λc values (0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0 mm) based on the expected Ra range of the workpiece.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, KTU Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**With the help of a block diagram, explain the working principle of a stylus-type surface roughness measuring instrument. Discuss the role of (a) LVDT pickup, and (b) the cut-off filter in the measurement.**

#### **Part (a) — Working Principle & Block Diagram (7 Marks)**

The stylus-type surface roughness measuring instrument (profilometer) works on the principle of **contact-based displacement measurement and inductive transduction**.

**Working Principle:**

1. The workpiece is placed on the measuring table, and the **stylus carriage** is positioned so that the diamond-tipped stylus (tip radius 2 µm or 5 µm) gently contacts the surface. A small static force (≤ 1.5 mN) is applied to maintain contact without damaging the surface.
2. A precision **drive motor** traverses the stylus (or the workpiece) horizontally at a constant speed (typically 0.5 mm/s) across a defined **sampling length**.
3. As the stylus moves, it rises and falls with the peaks and valleys of the surface profile. This micro-displacement (of the order of 0.01 µm to 100 µm) is transferred to the core of an **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** via a pivoted arm.
4. The LVDT produces a **modulated AC output voltage** proportional to the displacement.
5. This signal is **amplified, demodulated** (phase-sensitive rectifier), and passed through the **cut-off (λc) low-pass Gaussian filter** to remove low-frequency waviness.
6. The filtered roughness signal is **digitized by an ADC** and processed by a microcontroller to compute Ra, Rz, Rq, etc.
7. The numerical value is shown on an **LCD display**, and an optional **chart recorder** provides a magnified profile trace.

**Block Diagram:**

```
Workpiece → Stylus → LVDT → Amplifier → Demodulator → λc Filter → ADC → Processor → Display
   ↑                                                                                       |
   └─────────────── Drive Motor (Constant Speed) ←─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### **Part (b) — Role of LVDT and Cut-off Filter (7 Marks)**

**Role of the LVDT Pickup:**

The **LVDT** is the displacement transducer at the heart of the instrument. It consists of a **primary coil** and two **secondary coils** wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable **ferromagnetic core** attached to the pivoted stylus arm.

- When the **oscillator** excites the primary coil with a stable AC carrier (typically 5 kHz), the two secondaries produce equal and opposite voltages.
- When the core shifts due to stylus displacement, the coupling between primary and secondaries becomes unequal, producing a **net differential voltage** proportional to the displacement.
- This differential voltage has a **phase** (positive or negative) that indicates the **direction** of the displacement (upward peak or downward valley).
- LVDTs are preferred in profilometers because they offer:
  - **High resolution** (sub-micron)
  - **Frictionless operation** (no contact between core and coils)
  - **High linearity**
  - **Robustness** against environmental noise

**Role of the Cut-off Filter (λc):**

The λc filter is a **phase-corrected Gaussian low-pass filter** that acts on the demodulated signal. Its roles are:

1. **Separation of roughness from waviness:** Machined surfaces contain both short-wavelength roughness (peaks/valleys from tool feed) and long-wavelength waviness (from machine vibration, deflection, etc.). The λc filter passes wavelengths **shorter than λc** (roughness) and attenuates **longer wavelengths** (waviness).
2. **Standardization of measurement:** ISO 4288 mandates specific λc values (0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0 mm) for different Ra ranges, ensuring **comparability of measurements** across different labs and instruments.
3. **Prevention of errors:** Without proper λc selection, low-frequency geometric form (e.g., the curvature of a cylindrical shaft) would dominate the calculation, giving meaningless Ra values.
4. **Phase correction:** Unlike a simple RC filter, the Gaussian filter is **phase-corrected** (zero phase shift at all frequencies), which means it does not distort the location of peaks and valleys on the profile — critical for accurate Rz and Rt measurement.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define the parameters Ra and Rz. Derive the expression for Ra. A sample length of 0.8 mm is measured on a surface with the LVDT sensitivity 10 mV/µm, and the following voltage readings (in mV) are recorded at 10 equally spaced points: 0.5, –1.0, 0.8, –0.6, 1.2, –0.4, 0.7, –1.1, 0.3, –0.9. Calculate Ra.**

#### **Part (a) — Definitions and Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Definition of Ra:**

> **Ra (Arithmetic Mean Deviation):** It is the arithmetic average of the absolute values of the ordinates of the roughness profile from the mean line, measured over one sampling length $lr$. It is the most widely used international roughness parameter.

**Definition of Rz:**

> **Rz (Mean Peak-to-Valley Height):** It is the average of the 5 largest peak-to-valley separations $Z_i = Rp_i - Rv_i$ measured over one sampling length. It is more sensitive to occasional deep scratches or high peaks than Ra.

**Derivation of Ra:**

The mean line is defined such that the area above it equals the area below it:

$$\int_{0}^{lr} y(x) \, dx = 0$$

The arithmetic mean of the absolute ordinates over the sampling length is the integral of the absolute profile divided by the length:

$$Ra = \frac{1}{lr} \int_{0}^{lr} \left\vert y(x) \right\vert \, dx$$

For discrete digital samples (N points), the integral becomes a summation:

$$Ra \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\vert y_i \right\vert$$

If the standard requires averaging over **5 consecutive sampling lengths** (ISO 4288):

$$Ra_{final} = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{j=1}^{5} Ra_j$$

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Sampling length $lr = 0.8 \, mm$
- LVDT sensitivity $k = 10 \, mV/\mu m$
- N = 10 voltage readings (mV): $0.5, -1.0, 0.8, -0.6, 1.2, -0.4, 0.7, -1.1, 0.3, -0.9$

**Step 1 — Convert voltages to displacements:**

$$\begin{aligned}
y_i &= \frac{V_i}{k} \\
y_1 &= 0.5 / 10 = 0.050 \, \mu m \\
y_2 &= -1.0 / 10 = -0.100 \, \mu m \\
y_3 &= 0.8 / 10 = 0.080 \, \mu m \\
y_4 &= -0.6 / 10 = -0.060 \, \mu m \\
y_5 &= 1.2 / 10 = 0.120 \, \mu m \\
y_6 &= -0.4 / 10 = -0.040 \, \mu m \\
y_7 &= 0.7 / 10 = 0.070 \, \mu m \\
y_8 &= -1.1 / 10 = -0.110 \, \mu m \\
y_9 &= 0.3 / 10 = 0.030 \, \mu m \\
y_{10} &= -0.9 / 10 = -0.090 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Verify the mean is approximately zero:**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{1}{10} \left( 0.050 - 0.100 + 0.080 - 0.060 + 0.120 - 0.040 + 0.070 - 0.110 + 0.030 - 0.090 \right) = \frac{-0.050}{10} = -0.005 \, \mu m$$

This is very close to zero ✓ (the mean-line condition is satisfied within instrument noise).

**Step 3 — Take absolute values and sum:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum \left\vert y_i \right\vert = & \; 0.050 + 0.100 + 0.080 + 0.060 + 0.120 + 0.040 + 0.070 + 0.110 + 0.030 + 0.090 \\
= & \; 0.750 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Ra:**

$$Ra = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\vert y_i \right\vert = \frac{0.750}{10} = 0.075 \, \mu m$$

**Step 5 — If the full 5-sampling-length evaluation is used and all 5 are similar, the final reported Ra is:**

$$\boxed{Ra \approx 0.075 \, \mu m \;\; (\text{or } 0.08 \, \mu m \text{ rounded})}$$

> Since $0.02 < 0.075 \le 0.1$, the appropriate cut-off per ISO 4288 is $\lambda_c = 0.25 \, mm$, *not* the 0.8 mm used. **This is a common KTU exam pitfall to highlight.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in surface roughness questions (and lose marks on):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to take absolute values** in the Ra calculation. Without `|y|`, positive and negative readings cancel out and you get near zero. **Marks deducted: 2-3**
> 
> 2. **Wrong cut-off selection.** ISO 4288 prescribes specific λc for specific Ra ranges. Students often quote "0.8 mm for everything" — this is a major conceptual error. **Marks deducted: 2-3**
> 
> 3. **Confusing sampling length (lr) with evaluation length (ln).** Many students compute Ra over the full evaluation length (5·λc) using a single average instead of averaging 5 separate Ra values. The standard requires the latter. **Marks deducted: 1-2**
> 
> 4. **Missing the LVDT sensitivity factor.** Voltage readings must be divided by the sensitivity (mV/µm) to get physical displacement in µm. Many students leave the answer in mV. **Marks deducted: 2**
> 
> 5. **Confusing Ra with Rz.** Ra is the *arithmetic mean* of absolute deviations; Rz is the *mean of 5 peak-to-valley heights*. These are not interchangeable, and the formulas differ fundamentally.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting the stylus tip radius and force** in block diagram / instrument description questions. The KTU examiner expects "2 µm or 5 µm tip" and "≤ 1.5 mN force" to be mentioned.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam):**

- **Surface Roughness =** fine, high-frequency, short-wavelength component of surface texture (separate from waviness).
- **Stylus tip radius =** **2 µm or 5 µm** (diamond); static force ≤ **1.5 mN**.
- **Transducer =** **LVDT** (most common) or piezoelectric — converts vertical displacement to electrical signal.
- **Cut-off (λc) is the most critical setting** — wrong λc = meaningless Ra.
- **ISO 4288 λc values:** 0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0 mm (depending on Ra range).
- **Sampling length (lr) = λc**; **Evaluation length (ln) = 5 × λc**.
- **Ra formula:** $Ra = \frac{1}{lr} \int_{0}^{lr} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ (always with absolute value!).
- **Rz formula:** mean of 5 highest peak-to-valley values $Z_i$ in the sampling length.
- **Rq ≈ 1.11 × Ra** (empirical relationship for most surfaces).
- **Filter type =** Phase-corrected Gaussian low-pass (removes waviness, keeps roughness).
- **Drive speed =** typically **0.5 mm/s** or **1 mm/s**.
- **Two key standards:** **ISO 4287** (parameters), **ISO 4288** (cut-off selection), **ISO 3274** (instrument specification).
- **Practical use of cut-off:** choose the *smallest* λc that still gives a stable reading (the rule of thumb is to vary λc and pick the one where Ra stops changing).
- **Common applications:** cylinder bores, bearing journals, sealing surfaces, optical flats.
- **Why not use a "skid" in modern instruments?** Skids filter waviness mechanically, but introduce a moving reference error; modern **skidless** instruments with electronic λc filters are preferred.
- **Always express Ra in µm** (micrometers), never in mm or nm.
- **In numerical problems: convert voltage → displacement using LVDT sensitivity first**, *then* take absolute values, *then* average.
- **Block diagram flow:** Stylus → LVDT → Amplifier → Demodulator → λc Filter → ADC → Processor → Display.
- **Examiner's pet topics:** "Why cut-off is selected", "Difference between Ra and Rz", "Why stylus radius is small", "Why evaluation length is 5× sampling length".
